The multi-Region setting is a single toggle that applies organization-wide, requiring no per-Region configuration or additional trails. This approach leverages the trail already in place, which minimizes change risk and maintains the existing S3 bucket, log encryption, and retention policies without modification.

The startup can enable multi-Region logging through the AWS Console or AWS CLI in minutes, and log delivery from the new Regions begins immediately without waiting for new trail creation or propagation delays. This is the least operationally burdensome solution because it changes only one attribute on one resource.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-create-and-update-a-trail-by-using-the-aws-cli-update-trail.html

Incorrect options:

Create separate CloudTrail trails for each of the three newly added Regions and manually configure each trail to point to the same centralized S3 bucket - Creating separate trails for each Region requires manually configuring each trail, specifying the destination S3 bucket for each, and managing authentication and encryption settings independently. This approach multiplies the configuration complexity and creates more resources to monitor and maintain. Separate region-specific trails also produce logs with different organizational structures in the S3 bucket, making consolidated analysis more difficult than a single multi-Region trail.

Update the existing CloudTrail trail to enable multi-Region logging so all Regions deliver logs to the same bucket - AWS Config is a resource compliance and configuration tracking service, not a log aggregation service. Config does not control CloudTrail log delivery. Adding Config does not solve the immediate problem and introduces unnecessary service dependencies and monitoring overhead. Kinesis Data Streams would require additional data processing pipeline code and ongoing infrastructure management, all of which are more complex than enabling an existing trail's multi-Region capability.

Establish a new organization-wide CloudTrail trail in AWS Organizations that automatically covers all enabled Regions - AWS Organizations CloudTrail trails are valuable for multi-account visibility, but the startup operates a single account and this option creates an entirely new trail. If the startup later onboards additional member accounts, an organization trail would be beneficial, but for a single-account scenario with multiple Regions, updating the existing trail to multi-Region mode is simpler than creating a new organization trail.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-user-guide.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-create-and-update-a-trail-by-using-the-aws-cli-update-trail.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-update-a-trail-console.html

Domínio Domain 1: Detection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company reviews its password standards and discovers that no minimum password length is enforced for two identity systems used by its workforce and applications. The first identity system uses AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) federation with an on-premises Active Directory environment. The second identity system uses Amazon Cognito user pools as the user directory for a custom cloud application.

A security engineer must implement a required minimum password length for both identity systems while keeping password enforcement aligned with the system that actually manages the user credentials.

Which combination of actions should the security engineer take to meet these requirements? (Select two)

Update the account password policy in AWS Identity and Access Management so that federated users and Amazon Cognito users must use longer passwords
opcoes: Modify the password policy in the on-premises Active Directory environment so that the federated users must meet the new minimum password length
Modify the password policy settings for the Amazon Cognito user pools so that the application users must meet the new minimum password length
Enable AWS IAM Identity Center password requirements and use those settings to propagate a minimum password length to the federated Active Directory users and the Amazon Cognito user pools
Configure an AWS Config rule to check for password policy compliance in IAM and Cognito, and rely on the rule to block users from setting passwords that are shorter than the required minimum
respostas_corretas: Modify the password policy in the on-premises Active Directory environment so that the federated users must meet the new minimum password length
Modify the password policy settings for the Amazon Cognito user pools so that the application users must meet the new minimum password length
explicacao:
Correct option:

Modify the password policy settings for the Amazon Cognito user pools so that the application users must meet the new minimum password length

Amazon Cognito user pools maintain their own password policy settings for the users stored in the user pool. Because the custom cloud application uses Cognito user pools as its user database, the minimum password length for those application users must be enforced directly in the Cognito configuration.

Updating the Cognito password policy is the correct control point because Cognito is the system of record for those credentials. This allows the company to enforce minimum password length where the passwords are created, stored, and validated for the application.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/managing-users-passwords.html

Modify the password policy in the on-premises Active Directory environment so that the federated users must meet the new minimum password length

For users who authenticate through IAM federation with an on-premises Active Directory, the passwords are not managed in IAM. The external directory remains the authoritative identity source and performs the password validation.

Because Active Directory manages those user credentials, the minimum password length must be enforced in the on-premises Active Directory password policy. This ensures the federated users are subject to the required standard before authentication assertions are sent to AWS.

Incorrect options:

Update the account password policy in AWS Identity and Access Management so that federated users and Amazon Cognito users must use longer passwords - The IAM account password policy applies only to IAM users that exist in the AWS account. It does not control password requirements for federated users who authenticate through an external directory, and it does not control Amazon Cognito user pool passwords. As a result, updating the IAM password policy would not solve the stated problem for either of the two identity systems in the scenario. It targets the wrong credential stores.

Configure an AWS Config rule to check for password policy compliance in IAM and Cognito, and rely on the rule to block users from setting passwords that are shorter than the required minimum - AWS Config can evaluate whether certain resource configurations comply with desired settings, and there are managed rules for IAM and Cognito password policy checks. However, Config is primarily a detective compliance service, not a preventative password enforcement mechanism. Using AWS Config can help the company identify that a password policy is too weak, but it does not itself block a user from setting a short password. The enforcement still must happen in Active Directory and Cognito.

Enable AWS IAM Identity Center password requirements and use those settings to propagate a minimum password length to the federated Active Directory users and the Amazon Cognito user pools - AWS IAM Identity Center has its own password requirements only when it is the identity source. In this scenario, the company uses on-premises Active Directory for federated users and Amazon Cognito user pools for the application users. Because IAM Identity Center is not the authoritative identity source for either set of users described in the scenario, its password settings would not propagate to those systems. This option applies controls in the wrong place.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/managing-users-passwords.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito-user-identity-pools/latest/APIReference/API_PasswordPolicyType.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_passwords_account-policy.html

Domínio Domain 4: Identity and Access Management
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company publishes REST APIs by using Amazon API Gateway. The application team wants to understand how clients are accessing the APIs, including traffic patterns and request behavior. The team wants to analyze this information without building a custom log-parsing workflow. A security engineer must recommend a solution that provides visibility into API access patterns with the least operational effort.

Which combination of steps should the security engineer implement to meet these requirements? (Select two)

Stream API Gateway logs into Amazon OpenSearch Service and build dashboards to analyze request distribution and caller behavior over time
opcoes: Enable Detailed CloudWatch Metrics for the required API stage to capture per-method API metrics
Configure AWS X-Ray tracing for the API stage and use trace maps to analyze long-term API access patterns across different client segments
Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights to query and analyze API access information from the API Gateway access logs
Enable access logging for the required API stage so that API Gateway records detailed request and response information
respostas_corretas: Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights to query and analyze API access information from the API Gateway access logs
Enable access logging for the required API stage so that API Gateway records detailed request and response information
explicacao:
Correct option:

Enable access logging for the required API stage so that API Gateway records detailed request and response information

Enabling access logging on an API Gateway stage is required to capture detailed request-level information such as source IP, user agent, request path, and response status. This data forms the foundation for analyzing API access patterns.

Without access logs, there is no granular visibility into how clients interact with the API. Therefore, enabling access logging is a necessary first step to meet the requirement of analyzing API usage.

Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights to query and analyze API access information from the API Gateway access logs

Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights provides a serverless and efficient way to query and analyze API Gateway access logs without building a custom parsing solution. It allows the team to run structured queries directly on log data and extract meaningful insights.

This satisfies the requirement of analyzing access patterns without manual log parsing. Combined with access logging, it delivers a low-effort and native AWS solution for operational visibility.

Incorrect options:

Enable Detailed CloudWatch Metrics for the required API stage to capture per-method API metrics - Detailed CloudWatch metrics provide aggregated statistics such as request count and latency. However, they do not provide request-level details such as source IP or user agent. Because the requirement focuses on analyzing access patterns, including client behavior, metrics alone are insufficient.

Configure AWS X-Ray tracing for the API stage and use trace maps to analyze long-term API access patterns across different client segments - AWS X-Ray focuses on tracing request paths and diagnosing performance issues across distributed systems. It is not designed for analyzing API access patterns at scale. It also requires additional configuration and does not replace the need for access logs.

Stream API Gateway logs into Amazon OpenSearch Service and build dashboards to analyze request distribution and caller behavior over time - Amazon OpenSearch Service can provide advanced analytics capabilities, but it requires provisioning, ingestion pipelines, and ongoing management. This adds unnecessary complexity compared to native CloudWatch tools and does not align with the requirement for minimal effort.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/set-up-logging.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/AnalyzingLogData.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/aws-xray.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/opensearch-service/latest/developerguide/what-is.html

Domínio Domain 1: Detection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A security engineer is designing security controls for a fleet of 500 EC2 instances running a custom web application in a VPC. The organization requires the ability to continuously identify software vulnerabilities across the entire fleet and automatically remediate discovered issues to maintain compliance with industry standards.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend to meet these requirements?
opcoes: Install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on the EC2 instances, enable detailed logging, and use Amazon EventBridge rules to inspect software logs for suspicious patterns
Use Amazon GuardDuty Malware Protection for EC2 to scan the instances for software vulnerabilities, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy fixes
Use Amazon Inspector to identify software vulnerabilities on the EC2 instances, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy security patches and operating system updates
Use Amazon Inspector to perform vulnerability discovery, and use AWS Systems Manager Run Command to execute ad hoc patching scripts on each EC2 instance instead of using a managed patching service
respostas_corretas: Use Amazon Inspector to identify software vulnerabilities on the EC2 instances, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy security patches and operating system updates
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use Amazon Inspector to identify software vulnerabilities on the EC2 instances, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy security patches and operating system updates

Amazon Inspector is the AWS service built for vulnerability management on Amazon EC2. AWS documents that Inspector continually scans EC2 instances for package vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure, and produces findings when it detects software vulnerabilities. That directly satisfies the detection part of the requirement.

AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager is the native AWS service for patch remediation on managed nodes. AWS documents that Patch Manager automates the process of patching managed nodes with security-related and other updates. Using Inspector for discovery and Patch Manager for remediation creates the cleanest managed solution for detecting and mitigating software vulnerabilities on the EC2 fleet.

This combination also has the lowest ongoing operational burden among the choices because it uses AWS-native managed services for both phases of the workflow. The engineer gets continuous vulnerability findings and an established patching mechanism instead of building custom detection or update logic.

Incorrect options:

Use Amazon Inspector to perform vulnerability discovery, and use AWS Systems Manager Run Command to execute ad hoc patching scripts on each EC2 instance instead of using a managed patching service - Amazon Inspector is the correct service for vulnerability detection, so this option is close. However, replacing Patch Manager with ad hoc Run Command patch scripts increases administrative effort and reduces consistency compared with using the managed patching workflow that Systems Manager Patch Manager already provides. The requirement is to implement a solution for both detection and mitigation. Patch Manager is the more appropriate remediation service because it is designed specifically for patch management at scale rather than one-off command execution.

Install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on the EC2 instances, enable detailed logging, and use Amazon EventBridge rules to inspect software logs for suspicious patterns - The CloudWatch agent and detailed logging can help with observability and anomaly detection, but they do not perform software vulnerability scanning. Reviewing logs for suspicious patterns is not the same as detecting known package vulnerabilities and exposure issues. EventBridge also is not a vulnerability analysis engine. This option focuses on monitoring behavior rather than identifying and remediating vulnerable software packages on EC2 instances.

Use Amazon GuardDuty Malware Protection for EC2 to scan the instances for software vulnerabilities, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy fixes - GuardDuty Malware Protection for EC2 scans attached EBS volumes to detect the potential presence of malware. AWS documents it as malware detection, not as a service for identifying package vulnerabilities or unintended network exposure on EC2 instances. Patch Manager is valid for applying updates, but the detection half of this option is wrong for the stated requirement. Malware scanning and vulnerability scanning are different security functions, and Amazon Inspector is the service that covers software vulnerabilities.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/inspector/latest/user/scanning-ec2.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/inspector/latest/user/what-is-inspector.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/patch-manager.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/malware-protection.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/on-demand-malware-scan.html

Domínio Domain 1: Detection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A security team must immediately isolate a potentially compromised EC2 instance to prevent lateral movement while maintaining the ability to investigate the instance later. The team applied a network ACL to the target instance's subnet that denies all inbound and outbound traffic. However, the instance still responds to SSH requests and the security team can ping the instance from other hosts on the network. The initial containment measures are insufficient and the team requires a more comprehensive isolation solution.

What should the security team do to isolate the instance?
opcoes: Stop the target instance, create an Amazon Machine Image of the instance, and relaunch the instance in a quarantine subnet that allows access only from the forensics team
Remove the security group rule that allows port 22 inbound and reconfigure the instance to use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager instead of SSH
Execute an AWS Systems Manager document that deploys host-level firewall rules on the instance to block all network communication while preserving forensic data
Create a network ACL on the target instance's subnet that places deny-all-inbound rules above all other rules at the top priority and deny-all-outbound rules above all other rules at the top priority
respostas_corretas: Create a network ACL on the target instance's subnet that places deny-all-inbound rules above all other rules at the top priority and deny-all-outbound rules above all other rules at the top priority
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create a network ACL on the target instance's subnet that places deny-all-inbound rules above all other rules at the top priority and deny-all-outbound rules above all other rules at the top priority

Network ACLs are stateless layer 3 and 4 filtering mechanisms that operate on the subnet. Network ACL rules are evaluated in order from lowest rule number to highest, with processing stopping at the first matching rule. By placing explicit deny-all-inbound and deny-all-outbound rules at the top priority (rule numbers 1-2), all subsequent rules become unreachable for matching traffic. This creates a complete traffic isolation that blocks SSH, ICMP (ping), and all other protocols.

The scenario describes SSH still responding and ping still succeeding despite an existing network ACL, which indicates the original ACL did not have deny rules at the top priority. Perhaps the original deny rules were numbered lower (higher values) than allow rules, making them unreachable. By explicitly creating properly-ordered deny rules at the top, the team ensures no traffic of any kind can reach or leave the instance at the network layer.

This approach provides comprehensive isolation at the infrastructure level that is independent of instance configuration, making it impossible for malware or an attacker to circumvent through host-level configuration changes. The instance remains intact and accessible for later forensic investigation once approved by incident response leadership.

Incorrect options:

Stop the target instance, create an Amazon Machine Image of the instance, and relaunch the instance in a quarantine subnet that allows access only from the forensics team - Stopping and relaunching the instance in a quarantine subnet is disruptive and can alter the system before the investigation is complete. It also takes more effort and more time than necessary for the immediate isolation requirement. This option may be used later as part of a containment or rebuild strategy, but it is not the cleanest first action for isolating active network traffic in the current test scenario. The scenario points to the need for a network control that immediately blocks the active session.

Remove the security group rule that allows port 22 inbound and reconfigure the instance to use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager instead of SSH - Removing a single security group rule for port 22 does not prevent the instance from responding to SSH requests if the rule still exists elsewhere or if there are overlapping rules. Moreover, reconfiguring the instance to use Session Manager requires the instance to still have outbound network access to Systems Manager endpoints and EC2 Instance Metadata Service, which conflicts with the isolation requirement. This approach requires modifying the instance configuration rather than enforcing isolation at the network layer.

Execute an AWS Systems Manager document that deploys host-level firewall rules on the instance to block all network communication while preserving forensic data - Host-level firewall rules are maintained on the instance itself and can potentially be disabled, reconfigured, or bypassed by an attacker with sufficient privileges on the compromised instance. The scenario indicates the instance is potentially compromised, so relying on controls running on the compromised system is not reliable containment. Network-layer controls (security groups, network ACLs) cannot be modified or bypassed from within the guest OS, making them more trustworthy for isolation.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/security.html

Domínio Domain 2: Incident Response
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
An AWS administrator created an IAM group for the DevOps team and applied the following managed policy to require multi-factor authentication for all Amazon EC2 operations:

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "ec2:*", "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "BlockAnyAccessUnlessSignedInWithMFA", "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "ec2:*", "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "BoolIfExists": { "aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent": "false" } } } ] } After the policy is applied, engineers report that they cannot run Amazon EC2 commands using the AWS CLI even after registering their MFA devices.

What should the administrator do to resolve this problem in the most operationally efficient manner, while still enforcing multi-factor authentication?
opcoes: Direct engineers to run the aws sts get-session-token command with the --serial-number and --token-code flags to obtain temporary session credentials, then configure the AWS CLI profile to use the returned AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, and SessionToken values for subsequent API calls

Deploy AWS IAM Identity Center with MFA-enabled permission sets assigned to each DevOps engineer, then instruct engineers to authenticate through the IAM Identity Center portal to receive temporary AWS CLI credentials that already carry the correct MFA session context required by the BoolIfExists condition evaluation
Instruct users to regenerate their long-term access keys after enabling MFA so that subsequent CLI calls automatically include MFA context in the request

Define an IAM role with a trust policy condition that requires MFA authentication, instruct engineers to call the aws sts assume-role command with --serial-number and --token-code parameters, store the returned temporary credentials as the AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and AWS_SESSION_TOKEN environment variables, and add sts:AssumeRole to the policy's NotAction list to prevent session context bypass
respostas_corretas: Direct engineers to run the aws sts get-session-token command with the --serial-number and --token-code flags to obtain temporary session credentials, then configure the AWS CLI profile to use the returned AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, and SessionToken values for subsequent API calls
explicacao:
Correct option:

Direct engineers to run the aws sts get-session-token command with the --serial-number and --token-code flags to obtain temporary session credentials, then configure the AWS CLI profile to use the returned AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, and SessionToken values for subsequent API calls

The BoolIfExists condition key evaluates aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent against the current session context. When engineers use long-term IAM user access keys directly with the AWS CLI, those credentials are not associated with an MFA session, so aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent is either absent or false. The Deny statement with BoolIfExists fires in both cases, blocking all ec2:* actions. The engineers have registered MFA devices, but they have not obtained session tokens that carry MFA authentication context, which is why the CLI calls still fail.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_GetSessionToken.html

Running aws sts get-session-token with --serial-number set to the MFA device ARN and --token-code set to the current one-time password generates temporary credentials that include an MFA authentication context. When these temporary credentials are used in the AWS CLI, aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent evaluates to true, the Deny condition is not satisfied, and the Allow statement from the first policy statement takes effect. Engineers configure their CLI profile with the three returned credential values to enable the MFA-authenticated session.

This approach requires no changes to the existing IAM policy and enforces MFA exactly as intended. The policy continues to deny access to engineers who have not authenticated with MFA, and engineers who have obtained session tokens through sts get-session-token operate normally.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_GetSessionToken.html

Incorrect options:

Instruct users to regenerate their long-term access keys after enabling MFA so that subsequent CLI calls automatically include MFA context in the request - Regenerating access keys does not include MFA context in API requests. Long-term credentials remain static and cannot satisfy the MFA condition. Temporary credentials from STS are required.

Deploy AWS IAM Identity Center with MFA-enabled permission sets assigned to each DevOps engineer, then instruct engineers to authenticate through the IAM Identity Center portal to receive temporary AWS CLI credentials that already carry the correct MFA session context required by the BoolIfExists condition evaluation - AWS IAM Identity Center is a valid solution for enforcing MFA across AWS CLI access, and it does produce temporary credentials with appropriate session context. However, deploying IAM Identity Center requires configuring an identity source, assigning permission sets, and migrating the DevOps engineers from IAM user credentials to Identity Center credentials. This is a broader infrastructure change than required to resolve the described problem.

The existing IAM users, MFA devices, and policy are already in place. The engineers only need to use the sts get-session-token command to obtain session credentials that carry MFA context, which resolves the issue immediately without any additional infrastructure deployment or credential migration.

Define an IAM role with a trust policy condition that requires MFA authentication, instruct engineers to call the aws sts assume-role command with --serial-number and --token-code parameters, store the returned temporary credentials as the AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and AWS_SESSION_TOKEN environment variables, and add sts:AssumeRole to the policy's NotAction list to prevent session context bypass - Using sts:AssumeRole with MFA in the trust policy is an alternative MFA enforcement pattern, but it is more complex than the get-session-token approach for this use case. The engineers are IAM users in the same account who need to run EC2 commands; assuming a role adds an unnecessary layer of credential exchange and role management. Adding sts:AssumeRole to the policy's NotAction list would also require restructuring the existing policy significantly. The get-session-token approach in the correct option directly produces temporary credentials with MFA context for the existing IAM identity without requiring role assumption, role trust policy configuration, or policy restructuring.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_GetSessionToken.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/enhance-programmatic-access-for-iam-users-using-yubikey-for-multi-factor-authentication/

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
An organization is implementing AWS Identity Center as the centralized identity management platform for the entire AWS organization spanning multiple accounts. The organization's AWS administrator needs to establish the prerequisites for delegating Identity Center administration to a designated person in the management account.

Which of the following statements correctly describe the required steps? (Select three)

Create IAM users in the management account and assign them to Identity Center as the source of identity authentication

Create user and group assignments within the Identity Center directory structure that establishes the initial organization membership
opcoes: Set up and configure a second AWS Region in the management account to provide geographic redundancy for Identity Center operations
Deploy an on-premises Active Directory Connector in the management account to facilitate hybrid identity integration with existing corporate directories
Create or link an AWS Identity Center directory in the management account to serve as the identity source for the organization
Create permission sets in the management account that define the roles and access levels that users will be assigned across member accounts
respostas_corretas: Create or link an AWS Identity Center directory in the management account to serve as the identity source for the organization
Create permission sets in the management account that define the roles and access levels that users will be assigned across member accounts
explicacao:
Correct options:

Create or link an AWS Identity Center directory in the management account to serve as the identity source for the organization

AWS Identity Center requires an identity source (directory) in the management account. Organizations can either create a new Identity Center directory managed by AWS or link an existing AWS Directory Service (Managed Microsoft Active Directory or Simple AD). This directory serves as the authoritative source of identity and is prerequisite to all other Identity Center configurations. Without a directory, the platform has no users or groups to manage, making this step logically first.

Create permission sets in the management account that define the roles and access levels that users will be assigned across member accounts

Permission sets are Identity Center's mechanism for defining IAM roles and their permissions. Permission sets serve as templates that are applied to users and groups across member accounts. Organizations define permission sets in the management account that specify job functions and their associated IAM permissions (PowerUser, Billing Admin, Developer, etc.). These permission sets are then assigned to users and groups, automatically creating corresponding IAM roles in member accounts. Permission sets must be created before user assignments can be made effective.

Create user and group assignments within the Identity Center directory structure that establishes the initial organization membership

User and group assignments establish which individuals and teams have access to which accounts and permission sets. Assignments are created within the Identity Center directory, linking directory users or groups to specific AWS accounts and permission sets. These assignments generate the actual temporary credentials and session tokens that users receive when they authenticate. Creating assignments is a prerequisite to users being able to access AWS accounts through Identity Center.

Incorrect options:

Deploy an on-premises Active Directory Connector in the management account to facilitate hybrid identity integration with existing corporate directories - While Active Directory Connectors can be used for hybrid identity scenarios, they are optional and are used to synchronize corporate directory users into Identity Center after initial setup. The connector does not need to be deployed in the management account (it typically runs in an on-premises data center or through an EC2 instance). Active Directory integration is not a prerequisite for basic Identity Center setup; it is an optional enhancement for organizations with existing corporate directories.

Set up and configure a second AWS Region in the management account to provide geographic redundancy for Identity Center operations - AWS Identity Center operates across AWS Regions automatically without requiring redundancy setup in a second Region. The directory created in the management account is replicated and available across all Regions. Multi-Region configuration for Identity Center is not a prerequisite and is handled transparently by AWS infrastructure. This is not part of the standard prerequisite setup.

Create IAM users in the management account and assign them to Identity Center as the source of identity authentication - Traditional IAM users are not the source of identity for Identity Center. Identity Center is specifically designed to eliminate the need for individual IAM users in AWS accounts. Users authenticate to Identity Center (which uses its managed directory or an external identity provider), and Identity Center provisions temporary credentials. Creating IAM users in the management account for Identity Center authentication is the opposite of the intended architecture.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/getting-started.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/manage-your-identity-source.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/permissionsets.html

Domínio Domain 4: Identity and Access Management
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company has enabled AWS Config across an AWS Organizations environment and has deployed hundreds of Amazon S3 buckets across multiple accounts. A security engineer must identify any buckets that are not configured to use AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) for default encryption. The company also must ensure that new objects cannot be uploaded unless the uploads use AWS KMS encryption. The solution must work across the organization with centralized governance and low operational overhead.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create bucket policies on each bucket to deny s3:PutObject only when the request specifies server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create an AWS Config remediation action that automatically rewrites every PutObject request that does not specify AWS KMS encryption

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption
respostas_corretas: Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption

The AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms is the correct managed rule for identifying buckets that are not configured to use AWS KMS for default encryption. AWS documents that this rule evaluates whether S3 buckets use AWS KMS default encryption and marks buckets as noncompliant if they are not configured accordingly.

To prevent uploads that are not encrypted with AWS KMS, the company needs a preventative control on s3:PutObject. A service control policy can act as an organization-wide guardrail so that member accounts cannot upload objects unless the request satisfies the required encryption conditions. This provides centralized governance and avoids the operational burden of managing individual bucket policies across hundreds of buckets.

This combination meets both parts of the requirement. AWS Config provides detection of buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and the SCP provides centralized prevention for future object uploads that do not use AWS KMS.

Incorrect options:

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create bucket policies on each bucket to deny s3:PutObject only when the request specifies server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys - The AWS Config rule in this option is correct, but the bucket policy approach is weaker operationally because the company has hundreds of buckets across an organization. Writing and maintaining a bucket policy on every bucket introduces much more administrative overhead than an organization-level SCP.

This option is also too narrow in the way it is described. Denying only requests that specify SSE-S3 does not fully guarantee that all uploads use AWS KMS, because requests could omit the header or use another encryption mode. The preventative control should require AWS KMS, not simply deny one undesired encryption method.

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption - The s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only AWS Config managed rule checks whether bucket policies require requests to use SSL or TLS. It is a transport security rule, not a rule that checks whether a bucket is configured to use AWS KMS default encryption. Although the SCP portion is directionally plausible, the detection half of the solution is wrong. Because the rule does not identify buckets that are not KMS-encrypted, this option does not meet the requirement.

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create an AWS Config remediation action that automatically rewrites every PutObject request that does not specify AWS KMS encryption - AWS Config remediation actions are useful for correcting noncompliant resource configuration after AWS Config detects drift. They do not sit inline on every PutObject request and cannot rewrite each upload request in real time before the object is stored. Because the requirement is to prevent unencrypted uploads, the control must be preventative rather than post-detection remediation. This option therefore uses the wrong enforcement model.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/specifying-kms-encryption.html

Domínio Domain 6: Security Foundations and Governance
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A financial services organization operates 18 AWS accounts distributed across AWS Organizations. These accounts expose multiple REST APIs through Amazon API Gateway to support mobile applications and partner integrations. The organization must defend these APIs against common web-based attack patterns including SQL injection and sophisticated bot activity. The solution should require minimal ongoing tuning and administrative effort after deployment.

Which approach best meets these requirements?
opcoes: Consolidate all APIs into a single Amazon CloudFront distribution with multiple custom origins representing each API Gateway endpoint, attach a unified AWS WAF web ACL to CloudFront, and route all traffic through the distribution to centralize threat protection across all 18 accounts

Deploy AWS Firewall Manager with a centralized AWS WAF policy that incorporates the AWS Bot Control and AWS Managed Rules for SQL databases, then assign the policy scope to API Gateway stages across the organization

Leverage AWS Security Hub for detecting exposed API Gateway resources and configuring Amazon EventBridge to route findings to Lambda, then program Lambda functions to instantiate AWS WAF rules on unprotected resources and monitor WAF logs for attack attempts

Establish an AWS WAF web ACL independently for each API, incorporate managed rules for SQL injection and bot detection, use AWS Config to identify unprotected resources, and set up automated remediation via Lambda to apply WAF policies to new resources
respostas_corretas: Deploy AWS Firewall Manager with a centralized AWS WAF policy that incorporates the AWS Bot Control and AWS Managed Rules for SQL databases, then assign the policy scope to API Gateway stages across the organization
explicacao:
Correct option:

Deploy AWS Firewall Manager with a centralized AWS WAF policy that incorporates the AWS Bot Control and AWS Managed Rules for SQL databases, then assign the policy scope to API Gateway stages across the organization

AWS Firewall Manager enables the creation of a single centralized WAF policy that is automatically distributed and enforced across all member accounts in the AWS Organizations hierarchy. The policy can incorporate AWS Managed Rules, including the Bot Control rule group for bot mitigation and the SQL database rule group for SQL injection protection. By setting the resource scope to API Gateway stages, Firewall Manager ensures that every current and future API Gateway stage across all 18 accounts automatically receives the same WAF protection without requiring individual configuration per stage.

The critical advantage is that once the policy is created in Firewall Manager, new accounts that join the organization are automatically enrolled in the policy. New API Gateway stages created in existing accounts also inherit the policy without additional administrative action. This eliminates the operational burden of discovering unprotected resources and provisioning WAF rules reactively; protection is proactive and inherited by default.

Firewall Manager also provides centralized rule management. Any updates to the Bot Control managed rules or SQL injection rules are applied uniformly across all 18 accounts from a single policy definition. This operational model minimizes tuning and maintenance overhead compared to managing 18 separate WAF configurations or relying on reactive remediation.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-bot-control.html

Incorrect options:

Establish an AWS WAF web ACL independently for each API, incorporate managed rules for SQL injection and bot detection, use AWS Config to identify unprotected resources, and set up automated remediation via Lambda to apply WAF policies to new resources - Creating individual WAF web ACLs for each API introduces significant operational overhead because a large API Gateway deployment could involve dozens or hundreds of APIs across 18 accounts. Reactive discovery and remediation via AWS Config and Lambda adds latency between the creation of a new API and the application of WAF protection. This approach requires ongoing management effort to monitor Config findings and debug Lambda remediation failures, and it does not automatically protect new accounts that join the organization.

Consolidate all APIs into a single Amazon CloudFront distribution with multiple custom origins representing each API Gateway endpoint, attach a unified AWS WAF web ACL to CloudFront, and route all traffic through the distribution to centralize threat protection across all 18 accounts - Routing all 18 accounts' APIs through a single CloudFront distribution creates a single point of failure and complicates multi-account architecture by centralizing traffic through one edge location. CloudFront adds latency compared to direct API Gateway access, and a single CloudFront distribution cannot represent the best architectural practice of distributed API ownership. Additionally, CloudFront is designed for content delivery, not for API aggregation, and this pattern complicates API endpoint discovery, access controls, and DNS routing for partner integrations.

Leverage AWS Security Hub for detecting exposed API Gateway resources and configuring Amazon EventBridge to route findings to Lambda, then program Lambda functions to instantiate AWS WAF rules on unprotected resources and monitor WAF logs for attack attempts - AWS Security Hub aggregates findings but does not provide a deployment or enforcement mechanism for WAF rules. Routing findings to EventBridge and Lambda introduces a delay between detection of an unprotected resource and the application of WAF protection. Lambda functions must be authored and tested to instantiate WAF rules, which increases complexity. Additionally, this reactive pattern leaves a window during which new APIs are unprotected while awaiting Lambda execution.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/what-is-aws-waf.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/aws-managed-rule-groups.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-bot-control.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company is migrating a legacy application from an on-premises data center to AWS. The application tier will run on AWS, but the backend database must remain in the on-premises environment because of compliance requirements. The application exchanges latency-sensitive traffic with the on-premises database. The company also requires that all traffic between AWS and the on-premises data center be protected with IPsec encryption.

Which combination of AWS solutions should the security engineer recommend to meet these requirements? (Select two)

Use VPC peering to connect the application VPC in AWS directly to the on-premises data center network
opcoes: Use AWS Client VPN to provide encrypted remote access from the application server in AWS to the on-premises database network over TLS
Use AWS Site-to-Site VPN to provide IPsec-encrypted connectivity between AWS and the on-premises data center
Use AWS Direct Connect to provide a dedicated, lower-latency network connection between AWS and the on-premises data center
Use AWS VPN CloudHub to connect the AWS workload and the on-premises database through a hub-and-spoke VPN topology
respostas_corretas: Use AWS Site-to-Site VPN to provide IPsec-encrypted connectivity between AWS and the on-premises data center
Use AWS Direct Connect to provide a dedicated, lower-latency network connection between AWS and the on-premises data center
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use AWS Site-to-Site VPN to provide IPsec-encrypted connectivity between AWS and the on-premises data center

AWS Site-to-Site VPN is the AWS service that provides IPsec-encrypted connectivity between an on-premises environment and AWS. This directly satisfies the requirement that traffic between AWS and the data center must use IPsec encryption. AWS documents that Site-to-Site VPN creates encrypted tunnels between customer networks and AWS. By itself, Site-to-Site VPN provides encryption, but it is not always the best choice for minimizing latency because the traffic typically traverses the public internet. That is why it should be paired with a dedicated connectivity option when latency sensitivity is important.

In this scenario, Site-to-Site VPN is the encryption component of the final design. It ensures compliance with the IPsec requirement while complementing a lower-latency transport path.

Use AWS Direct Connect to provide a dedicated, lower-latency network connection between AWS and the on-premises data center

AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated network connection from the on-premises data center to AWS. This helps reduce latency and improve the consistency of network performance compared with internet-based connectivity, which makes it a strong fit for a latency-sensitive database dependency. However, Direct Connect by itself does not natively provide IPsec encryption. AWS documents that if encryption is required, customers commonly run Site-to-Site VPN over Direct Connect. This combines the dedicated low-latency connectivity of Direct Connect with the IPsec encryption of Site-to-Site VPN.

Together, Direct Connect and Site-to-Site VPN meet both requirements. Direct Connect addresses the network latency concern, and Site-to-Site VPN satisfies the mandatory IPsec encryption requirement.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-vpc-connectivity-options/aws-direct-connect-site-to-site-vpn.html

Incorrect options:

Use AWS VPN CloudHub to connect the AWS workload and the on-premises database through a hub-and-spoke VPN topology - AWS VPN CloudHub is designed to provide secure communication between multiple remote sites by using AWS as a VPN hub. It is useful in hub-and-spoke connectivity patterns where multiple branch offices or customer sites need to communicate with each other. This scenario involves a single on-premises database connection requirement rather than a multi-site VPN hub design. VPN CloudHub also does not improve latency in the way a dedicated Direct Connect connection does, so it is not the best fit.

Use VPC peering to connect the application VPC in AWS directly to the on-premises data center network - VPC peering connects two VPCs. It cannot connect an AWS VPC directly to an on-premises data center network. Because the database remains on premises, VPC peering does not apply to this requirement. It also does not provide IPsec encryption for hybrid connectivity.

Use AWS Client VPN to provide encrypted remote access from the application server in AWS to the on-premises database network over TLS - AWS Client VPN provides secure remote-user access to AWS and on-premises resources. It is intended for end-user or device remote connectivity rather than for application-to-database hybrid network architecture. This does not match the requirement for a production application server connecting to an on-premises database with low latency. It is the wrong service model for this workload.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/s2svpn/VPC_VPN.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-vpc-connectivity-options/aws-direct-connect-site-to-site-vpn.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/s2svpn/VPN_CloudHub.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/what-is-vpc-peering.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/clientvpn-admin/what-is.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A multinational corporation uses AWS Organizations and AWS IAM Identity Center (AWS Single Sign-On) to manage access across 22 member accounts. A security architect is authoring a permission set in IAM Identity Center that will be deployed to users across all 22 accounts. The permission set includes both an AWS managed policy and a customer-created policy. The architect holds full administrative privileges and is operating within the management account. When attempting to assign the permission set to an IAM Identity Center user who holds user access to multiple member accounts, the assignment operation fails.

What action should the architect take to resolve this failure?
opcoes: Abandon the permission set modification and instead edit the user's existing permission set by adding the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy directly to the existing role
Verify that the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy do not contain conflicting permissions, and reconcile any overlapping statements before redeploying the permission set

Create a service role in the management account that contains both policies, link the permission set to the service role through a cross-account trust relationship configured across all 22 member accounts, and delegate credential assumption to IAM Identity Center
Create the customer-managed policy in every member account that will receive the permission set, while ensuring that the policy name and permissions are identical in each account
respostas_corretas: Create the customer-managed policy in every member account that will receive the permission set, while ensuring that the policy name and permissions are identical in each account
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create the customer-managed policy in every member account that will receive the permission set, while ensuring that the policy name and permissions are identical in each account

IAM Identity Center permission sets that include customer-managed policies require that the customer-managed policy be present in every account where the permission set is assigned. Customer-managed policies are account-scoped resources; they do not replicate automatically across accounts like AWS managed policies do. When IAM Identity Center attempts to assign the permission set to a user in a member account, it references the customer-managed policy by name, but if that policy does not exist in the target account, the assignment fails.

The resolution is to create the customer-managed policy in all 22 member accounts with the identical name and identical permissions. Once the policy exists in each account, IAM Identity Center can successfully reference it from any member account, and the permission set assignment completes. The AWS managed policy, in contrast, is already available in all accounts and requires no additional provisioning.

This approach ensures that the permission set behaves consistently across all member accounts and that users receive the intended permissions regardless of which account they access through IAM Identity Center.

Incorrect options: Create a service role in the management account that contains both policies, link the permission set to the service role through a cross-account trust relationship configured across all 22 member accounts, and delegate credential assumption to IAM Identity Center - Creating a service role in the management account and establishing cross-account trust does not align with IAM Identity Center's permission set model. Permission sets are inherently designed to be used by IAM Identity Center to create roles within member accounts, not to assume cross-account service roles in the management account. This approach is architecturally incorrect for the IAM Identity Center workflow.

Verify that the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy do not contain conflicting permissions, and reconcile any overlapping statements before redeploying the permission set - Verifying for conflicting policies is a good general practice, but conflicting policy logic is not the cause of assignment failure in this scenario. IAM Identity Center fails the assignment because it cannot locate the customer-managed policy in the member account, not because of policy conflicts. Reconciling permissions does not resolve the missing policy problem.

Abandon the permission set modification and instead edit the user's existing permission set by adding the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy directly to the existing role - Editing the user's existing permission set does not address the organizational requirement to deploy the permission set across 22 accounts. Additionally, this approach is not scalable because it requires modifying each user individually rather than leveraging a centralized permission set that can be reused across multiple users.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/what-is.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/permissionsets.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_create-console.html

Domínio Domain 4: Identity and Access Management
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A security engineer must design a key management solution for a company that performs cryptographic operations on sensitive application data. The solution must create and manage symmetric AWS KMS keys whose key material is generated and used inside a custom key store backed by an AWS CloudHSM cluster. The company also needs to generate symmetric and asymmetric data key pairs for local use within applications. In addition, the company must maintain an audit trail of key usage and key management activity.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the keys, and use AWS CloudTrail to audit key usage and key management activity
Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon GuardDuty to audit key management and cryptographic operations

Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the symmetric KMS keys, use data keys and data key pairs for local cryptographic operations inside the applications, and use AWS Config to capture and audit every cryptographic operation that uses the keys
Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon Audit Manager to audit key management and cryptographic operations
respostas_corretas: Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the keys, and use AWS CloudTrail to audit key usage and key management activity
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the keys, and use AWS CloudTrail to audit key usage and key management activity

AWS Key Management Service supports custom key stores that are backed by AWS CloudHSM clusters. This allows the company to create symmetric KMS keys whose key material is generated and used in the custom key store while still using the AWS KMS control plane and APIs. AWS KMS also supports generating data keys and data key pairs for local cryptographic use in applications, which satisfies the requirement to use symmetric and asymmetric key material outside of AWS KMS.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/keystore-cloudhsm.html

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/manage-cmk-keystore.html

AWS CloudTrail is the correct auditing service for AWS KMS activity. AWS documents that CloudTrail logs all AWS KMS API calls, including key management operations such as CreateKey and cryptographic operations such as GenerateDataKey and Decrypt. This provides the required audit trail for both administrative activity and key usage.

This combination best meets all requirements with native AWS services. It uses AWS KMS with a custom key store for the required key control model, supports data keys and data key pairs for local application use, and uses CloudTrail for a complete audit record of AWS KMS operations.

Incorrect options:

Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon Audit Manager to audit key management and cryptographic operations - AWS Audit Manager is a compliance evidence collection service. It can collect evidence from services like CloudTrail and Config for assessments and audit reports, but it does not itself serve as the direct operational audit trail for KMS activity. So, this option is incorrect.

Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon GuardDuty to audit key management and cryptographic operations - AWS KMS with a custom key store is again the correct key management service, but Amazon GuardDuty is not an audit service for AWS KMS usage. GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes telemetry sources for suspicious activity. GuardDuty does not record every KMS API call or provide the required audit trail of key management and cryptographic operations. For this requirement, CloudTrail is the correct service.

Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the symmetric KMS keys, use data keys and data key pairs for local cryptographic operations inside the applications, and use AWS Config to capture and audit every cryptographic operation that uses the keys - AWS KMS with a custom key store and the use of data keys and data key pairs is directionally correct and makes this option sound very plausible. However, AWS Config does not capture every cryptographic operation such as GenerateDataKey or Decrypt as an audit log of API activity. AWS Config records resource configuration state and compliance changes, not full operational audit trails for cryptographic API usage. The correct audit mechanism for AWS KMS API calls is AWS CloudTrail, not AWS Config.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/keystore-cloudhsm.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/manage-cmk-keystore.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/logging-using-cloudtrail.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/WhatIsConfig.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/what-is-guardduty.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company operates hundreds of AWS accounts within an AWS Organizations structure in a single AWS Region. The company has designated a centralized security tooling account as the delegated administrator for both Amazon GuardDuty and AWS Security Hub. GuardDuty and Security Hub are automatically enabled for all existing and new accounts. During a security validation exercise, the security team launches an Amazon EC2 instance in one of the member accounts and attempts to generate a DNS-based GuardDuty finding by making repeated DNS queries to a test domain. However, no corresponding finding appears in the centralized Security Hub account.

What is the most likely reason that the finding did not appear in the delegated administrator account?
opcoes: The EC2 instance does not have an IAM role that allows publishing DNS activity logs to GuardDuty, which prevents the detection from being generated
The GuardDuty service-linked role was not created in the member account, which prevents GuardDuty from collecting DNS query data for analysis
The VPC uses a custom DNS resolver instead of the default AmazonProvidedDNS, which prevents GuardDuty from analyzing DNS query logs
The integration between GuardDuty and Security Hub was not enabled in the member account where the activity was generated
respostas_corretas: The VPC uses a custom DNS resolver instead of the default AmazonProvidedDNS, which prevents GuardDuty from analyzing DNS query logs
explicacao:
Correct option:

The VPC uses a custom DNS resolver instead of the default AmazonProvidedDNS, which prevents GuardDuty from analyzing DNS query logs

Amazon GuardDuty relies on DNS logs that are generated by the AmazonProvidedDNS resolver within the VPC. When a VPC is configured to use a custom DNS resolver, such as an external or third-party DNS service, those DNS queries are no longer visible to GuardDuty.

Because GuardDuty cannot inspect DNS traffic that bypasses AmazonProvidedDNS, it cannot generate DNS-based findings. As a result, even though the activity occurred, no finding is created and therefore nothing is forwarded to Security Hub.

This is a well-known limitation and a common exam trap. GuardDuty DNS findings require the use of the default AWS DNS resolver to function correctly.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_data-sources.html

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_data-sources.html

Incorrect options:

The GuardDuty service-linked role was not created in the member account, which prevents GuardDuty from collecting DNS query data for analysis - GuardDuty automatically creates and manages its service-linked role when the service is enabled. If GuardDuty is already enabled across the organization, the service-linked role exists. The absence of a finding is not related to role creation but to the inability to observe DNS traffic.

The integration between GuardDuty and Security Hub was not enabled in the member account where the activity was generated - In an organization where GuardDuty and Security Hub are automatically enabled, integration between the two services is typically configured centrally. If GuardDuty is properly enabled and integrated, findings are automatically forwarded to Security Hub. The absence of a finding indicates that the detection did not occur, not that the integration failed.

The EC2 instance does not have an IAM role that allows publishing DNS activity logs to GuardDuty, which prevents the detection from being generated - GuardDuty does not require an IAM role on EC2 instances to collect DNS activity. It analyzes traffic at the AWS infrastructure level rather than relying on instance-level permissions. This option incorrectly assumes that instance roles are required for threat detection, which is not the case for GuardDuty.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_data-sources.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_finding-types-active.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/securityhub-accounts.html

pergunta:
A company runs workloads in an AWS account and has Amazon GuardDuty enabled for threat detection. A security engineer notices unusual GuardDuty findings and wants to investigate a particular IAM role in more depth. The engineer needs a report that includes anomalous activity details and any indicators of compromise associated with that role.

Which solution should the security engineer use to meet these requirements?
opcoes: Use Amazon Detective to investigate the IAM role and generate an investigation report that includes anomalous behavior and indicators of compromise

Use AWS Security Hub to create a custom insight for the IAM role, export the matching findings, and rely on the insight output as the investigation report for anomalous behavior and compromise indicators
Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment for the IAM role and generate an assessment report for the suspicious activity
Use Amazon Inspector to run an on-demand scan of the IAM role and review the resulting report for indicators of compromise
respostas_corretas: Use Amazon Detective to investigate the IAM role and generate an investigation report that includes anomalous behavior and indicators of compromise
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use Amazon Detective to investigate the IAM role and generate an investigation report that includes anomalous behavior and indicators of compromise

Amazon Detective is the AWS service designed to help security teams analyze, investigate, and identify the root cause of suspicious activity and GuardDuty findings. AWS documents that Detective Investigations can be used to investigate IAM users and IAM roles by using indicators of compromise, and that the investigation summary highlights anomalous indicators that require attention. This makes Detective the best fit for the requirement because the engineer specifically wants to investigate an IAM role and generate a report that contains details about anomalous behavior and indicators of compromise. Detective is purpose-built for this type of investigation workflow rather than for compliance assessment or vulnerability scanning.

Using Detective also minimizes operational effort because it provides a native investigative experience tied to security findings, instead of requiring the company to build a separate reporting and correlation pipeline.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/detective/latest/userguide/detective-investigation-about.html

Incorrect options:

Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment for the IAM role and generate an assessment report for the suspicious activity - AWS Audit Manager is designed for compliance assessments and evidence collection against audit frameworks. It helps organizations map controls and gather evidence for standards such as PCI DSS or CIS, but it is not an incident investigation service for anomalous IAM role behavior. This option does not align with the requirement to analyze indicators of compromise and unusual GuardDuty-related activity. Audit Manager focuses on governance and compliance reporting, not on threat investigation of a specific IAM role.

Use AWS Security Hub to create a custom insight for the IAM role, export the matching findings, and rely on the insight output as the investigation report for anomalous behavior and compromise indicators - AWS Security Hub can aggregate and normalize findings from multiple security services, and custom insights can help filter findings related to a particular IAM role. That makes Security Hub useful for centralized visibility and prioritization. However, an insight is not the same as an investigation report with detailed anomalous behavior analysis and indicators of compromise. Security Hub helps surface findings, but Amazon Detective is the more appropriate service for the investigative report requested in this scenario.

Use Amazon Inspector to run an on-demand scan of the IAM role and review the resulting report for indicators of compromise - Amazon Inspector on-demand scans are associated with supported code security or workload scanning use cases, not with investigating IAM roles for GuardDuty-style suspicious behavior. Even though this option sounds plausible because it mentions a report, Inspector is not the right service for analyzing IAM role anomalies or compromise indicators. The requirement is investigative, not vulnerability-oriented.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/detective/latest/userguide/detective-investigation-about.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/what-is-guardduty.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/audit-manager/latest/userguide/what-is.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/inspector/latest/user/what-is-inspector.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/what-is-securityhub-v2.html

pergunta:
A company operates several AWS accounts in a multi-account environment. Developers sign in to a dedicated development account for their day-to-day work. A subset of those developers now needs controlled access to selected resources in a separate production account. The security team wants a solution that follows least privilege principles, avoids long-term credential sharing, and provides the most secure cross-account access model for production resources.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Create an IAM role in the production account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access the production resources

Create an IAM user in the production account for cross-account access, grant the required permissions to that user, and allow developers in the development account to use those user credentials when they need production access

Create a resource-based policy on each required production resource that allows all principals in the development account to access the resources directly, and rely on developer-side IAM policies in the development account to control who actually uses that access
Create an IAM role in the development account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access production resources
respostas_corretas: Create an IAM role in the production account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access the production resources
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create an IAM role in the production account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access the production resources

The most secure way to provide cross-account access is to create an IAM role in the production account and allow trusted principals from the development account to assume that role. This design uses AWS Security Token Service to issue temporary credentials instead of relying on long-term shared credentials.

This approach follows AWS best practices for multi-account access. Permissions are defined in the production account where the protected resources exist, and the trust policy on the role explicitly identifies which principals in the development account can assume the role. That keeps administrative control close to the resources being protected.

This model also supports least privilege and strong auditability. Developers continue to authenticate in their own account, then assume a narrowly scoped role only when they need access to production resources. The use of temporary credentials reduces credential exposure and avoids password or access key sharing.

Incorrect options:

Create a resource-based policy on each required production resource that allows all principals in the development account to access the resources directly, and rely on developer-side IAM policies in the development account to control who actually uses that access - Resource-based policies can support cross-account access for some services, but allowing all principals in the development account is overly broad and weakens the security boundary. It shifts too much reliance to the source account to prevent misuse. This option is also operationally heavier because each production resource would need to be managed individually. A role in the production account provides a cleaner and more secure central access point with explicit trust and least privilege permissions.

Create an IAM role in the development account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access production resources - Creating the IAM role in the development account does not properly solve cross-account access to production resources. The role that grants access to production resources should exist in the production account because that is where the resources and trust boundary reside. If the role exists only in the development account, it cannot by itself serve as the correct cross-account authorization point for production. The trust relationship and permissions need to be anchored in the target account.

Create an IAM user in the production account for cross-account access, grant the required permissions to that user, and allow developers in the development account to use those user credentials when they need production access - Creating an IAM user in the production account for developers to use introduces long-term credentials and credential distribution risks. Even if each developer had a separate user, this would still be less secure than using temporary role-based access. This option also increases administrative overhead because the production account would need to manage user lifecycle tasks directly. AWS recommends using IAM roles for cross-account access rather than cross-account IAM users.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_common-scenarios_aws-accounts.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html#id_roles_terms-and-concepts

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html

Domínio Domain 4: Identity and Access Management
