pergunta:
A telecommunications provider has implemented a strict security policy requiring that all data exchanged between the company's on-premises data center and AWS KMS remain within the AWS backbone network at all times. Direct public internet connections between the data center and KMS endpoints are prohibited under the policy. The company uses VPC endpoints to establish private connectivity from its VPC to other AWS services.

Which two actions together BEST accomplish this security requirement? (Select two)

Create an interface VPC endpoint for AWS KMS, but leave private DNS disabled and update all applications to continue using the default public AWS KMS endpoint names
opcoes: Modify the KMS key policy to restrict API calls based on source IP address using the condition aws:SourceIp with a CIDR block matching the on-premises network range
Create a VPC endpoint for the AWS KMS service with private DNS enabled so that all DNS queries for the KMS endpoint resolve to the VPC endpoint interface rather than public KMS IP addresses
Use the AWS KMS import key feature to transport the KMS master key to on-premises through an encrypted VPN tunnel, storing a copy locally to avoid public service endpoint access
Add a condition to the KMS key policy that restricts decryption operations only to requests arriving through the company's designated VPC endpoint using the aws:sourceVpce resource restriction
respostas_corretas: Create a VPC endpoint for the AWS KMS service with private DNS enabled so that all DNS queries for the KMS endpoint resolve to the VPC endpoint interface rather than public KMS IP addresses
Add a condition to the KMS key policy that restricts decryption operations only to requests arriving through the company's designated VPC endpoint using the aws:sourceVpce resource restriction
explicacao:
Correct options:

Add a condition to the KMS key policy that restricts decryption operations only to requests arriving through the company's designated VPC endpoint using the aws:sourceVpce resource restriction

The aws:sourceVpce condition in the KMS key policy then creates a second layer of control by verifying that every decryption operation comes through the specific VPC endpoint ID that the company has designated. Even if an application or credential is leaked and used from outside the VPC, the KMS key policy rejects any decryption attempts that do not originate from the approved VPC endpoint. The combination of a private DNS endpoint plus a key policy condition ensures that both the network path and the policy layer enforce the requirement that communications stay within AWS backbone infrastructure.

Create a VPC endpoint for the AWS KMS service with private DNS enabled so that all DNS queries for the KMS endpoint resolve to the VPC endpoint interface rather than public KMS IP addresses

A VPC endpoint for AWS KMS with private DNS enabled creates a private network path that intercepts all DNS queries for the KMS service and redirects them to a private endpoint interface within the VPC instead of public KMS IP addresses on the internet. From the application's perspective, DNS resolution still points to the KMS service, but traffic flows through the private endpoint gateway instead of the public internet. This eliminates the requirement for applications to route through a NAT gateway or internet gateway to reach KMS.

Together, these two controls satisfy the security policy requirement: private DNS redirects KMS traffic away from public endpoints, and the VPC endpoint condition in the key policy verifies the source before granting access.

Incorrect options:

Create an interface VPC endpoint for AWS KMS, but leave private DNS disabled and update all applications to continue using the default public AWS KMS endpoint names - Creating an interface VPC endpoint for KMS is directionally correct, but leaving private DNS disabled while continuing to use the default public KMS endpoint names defeats the purpose of the setup. Without private DNS, the standard service hostname does not automatically resolve to the interface endpoint inside the VPC.

Use the AWS KMS import key feature to transport the KMS master key to on-premises through an encrypted VPN tunnel, storing a copy locally to avoid public service endpoint access - AWS KMS customer managed keys cannot be exported, and the AWS KMS import key feature is used to import externally generated keys into KMS, not to export KMS-managed keys to on-premises. This option misrepresents KMS functionality and proposes a configuration that AWS does not support. Storing a local copy of a key on-premises would also eliminate the security benefit of centralized key management that AWS KMS provides.

Modify the KMS key policy to restrict API calls based on source IP address using the condition aws:SourceIp with a CIDR block matching the on-premises network range - Restricting the KMS key policy by source IP address does not ensure that traffic stays within AWS backbone infrastructure. An attacker with compromised credentials and network access to the corporate VPN can still place themselves behind the IP address range that the policy allows. IP-based restrictions are network-layer controls that do not prevent routing through public internet paths. The requirement explicitly states that communications must not use public service endpoints, which an IP address condition cannot enforce.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/overview.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policy-services.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company operates a web application deployed behind an Application Load Balancer in AWS. Security logs indicate a credential stuffing attack where attackers are submitting thousands of login requests from different IP addresses, all using a user agent string that identifies the request as coming from a mobile device emulator. The security team needs to block these attack requests while allowing legitimate user traffic to reach the application.

Which solution will meet these requirements?
opcoes: Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that blocks requests when the user agent string matches the mobile device emulator signature
Modify the ALB security group rules to deny all inbound traffic from the IP addresses identified in the attack logs
Deploy an AWS Lambda function behind the ALB that inspects incoming requests and rejects those with suspicious user agent strings before they reach the application
Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that allows requests only when the user agent string matches legitimate mobile device types
respostas_corretas: Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that blocks requests when the user agent string matches the mobile device emulator signature
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that blocks requests when the user agent string matches the mobile device emulator signature

AWS WAF is the AWS-native web application firewall service designed specifically to protect web applications from common web exploits and application-layer attacks including credential stuffing. By creating a WAF web ACL and associating it directly with the ALB, the security team can evaluate and filter HTTP requests before they reach the application. A custom WAF rule that examines the user agent HTTP header can identify and block requests originating from mobile device emulators, effectively filtering out the attack traffic while allowing legitimate requests through.

WAF rules operate at Layer 7 (application layer) where HTTP headers and request content are fully inspectable, making it the ideal service for header-based filtering. Custom rules can use string matching, regex patterns, and size constraints to evaluate user agent strings with high precision. WAF also provides real-time metrics and logging capabilities that help the security team monitor attack patterns and refine detection rules over time.

This approach provides immediate protection without requiring manual IP-based blocking, eliminates operational overhead of managing multiple security group rules, and adapts as attackers change their attack vectors. WAF is purpose-built for application-layer attacks and provides the most efficient and effective solution for this credential stuffing scenario.

Incorrect options:

Deploy an AWS Lambda function behind the ALB that inspects incoming requests and rejects those with suspicious user agent strings before they reach the application - Placing a Lambda function behind the ALB adds processing overhead for every request, including legitimate traffic, which increases latency and costs. Lambda-based request filtering is not as efficient as WAF for high-volume attack scenarios and introduces complexity in deployment and scaling. WAF is purpose-built for this type of request filtering and operates more efficiently at the AWS infrastructure level without impacting application latency.

Modify the ALB security group rules to deny all inbound traffic from the IP addresses identified in the attack logs - While security groups can filter traffic based on source IP addresses, this approach requires the security team to identify and manually maintain a list of attack source IPs. In a distributed credential stuffing attack with sources from many different IP addresses, maintaining this list becomes operationally burdensome and is ineffective because attackers can rotate through different IP addresses faster than the team can block them. Security groups are also better suited for Layer 3 (network) filtering rather than application-layer filtering based on HTTP headers.

Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that allows requests only when the user agent string matches legitimate mobile device types - Creating a rule that allows only specific user agent strings would be overly restrictive and break legitimate traffic from mobile browsers or applications with different user agent identifications. This approach assumes all legitimate traffic comes from predefined user agent strings, which is unrealistic in modern applications where thousands of different user agents may be valid. Maintenance of an allowlist would be cumbersome and would likely block legitimate users whose applications use unexpected user agent strings.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rules.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-string-match.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta: A company uses federation to grant temporary AWS access to members of its operations team. One federated user, Alex, must be blocked from accessing a specific Amazon S3 bucket named finance-audit-archive. The security team wants to enforce this restriction by using only an Amazon S3 bucket policy.
opcoes: The solution must deny Alex's access only to this bucket and must not interfere with any other permissions that Alex has elsewhere in the AWS environment. The company needs a policy statement that targets the correct federated principal and applies an explicit deny only for Amazon S3 actions on the specified bucket.
Which policy should the security engineer use?

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/Alex/session-name" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } }

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "NotPrincipal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } }
respostas_corretas: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "NotPrincipal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } }
explicacao:
Correct option:

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } }

An Amazon S3 bucket policy can explicitly deny access to a specific principal for actions against a specific bucket. In this case, the principal is a federated user, so the correct principal format uses the AWS STS federated-user ARN. By targeting arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex and applying an explicit Deny to s3:* on the bucket, the policy prevents Alex from using Amazon S3 permissions against that bucket.

This design affects only the permissions evaluated for that bucket because bucket policies are resource-based policies scoped to Amazon S3 resources. Alex can continue to retain any unrelated permissions he has for other AWS services or for other resources that are not covered by this bucket policy. That matches the requirement to preserve Alex's other permissions.

The key point is that the policy must identify the correct type of principal. Because Alex is a federated user, the policy must reference the federated-user ARN in STS rather than an IAM user ARN or an assumed-role ARN. An explicit deny in a resource-based policy then overrides any allows that Alex might otherwise have for that bucket.

Incorrect options:

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "NotPrincipal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } - NotPrincipal with an explicit deny reverses the intended logic. Instead of denying Alex, this statement denies everyone except Alex. That would create a broad and unintended access restriction on the bucket.

The requirement is to affect only Alex's S3 permissions for this bucket while leaving all other principals unchanged. This policy does the opposite by making Alex the only principal excluded from the deny, so it clearly fails the requirement.

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } - This policy uses an IAM user ARN, which does not match the identity type in the scenario. Alex is a federated user, so Amazon S3 evaluates his requests under the STS federated-user principal rather than an IAM user principal.

Because the principal ARN is wrong, the deny statement does not reliably target the intended identity. The bucket policy therefore fails to meet the requirement to deny this specific federated user while preserving the rest of his permissions.

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/Alex/session-name" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } - An assumed-role ARN identifies a role session, not a federated user principal. The scenario states that Alex is a federated user, so using an assumed-role principal does not correctly represent the identity that must be denied.

This mismatch matters because resource-based policies depend on the correct principal identity during authorization. If the principal does not match the actual caller type, the deny does not apply as intended, and the policy fails to provide the precise restriction required.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/security_iam_service-with-iam.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/example-bucket-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_identifiers.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_elements_principal.html

Domínio Domain 4: Identity and Access Management
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
During a post-breach forensic investigation, a security team discovers that an attacker gained temporary access to an AWS account with ID 987654321098. The attacker created an AWS KMS key during the compromise. The key policy on this KMS key grants a specific IAM principal kms:* permissions. Although the security team scheduled the key for deletion the previous day, the key remains active and usable today. The key has the ARN arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:987654321098:key/mrk-1a2b3c4d5e6f7891011121314151617.

The forensics team must delete this key as quickly as possible to stop any potential continued data access.

Which solution will meet this requirement?
opcoes: Call the kms:CancelKeyDeletion API on the KMS key to reset its pending deletion status. Then immediately call kms:DeleteKey on the key ARN to remove the key from the account without any waiting period

Call kms:RotateKeyOnDemand on the KMS key to generate new key material. Then schedule the original key material version for immediate deletion because the original version has been superseded by the newly rotated key material and is no longer needed

Update the key policy on the KMS key to add an explicit Allow statement for kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion and all other kms:* actions, specifying the security team's IAM role ARN as an additional key administrator. Then reissue the kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion API call for the key ARN and specify a waiting period of 7 days to satisfy the mandatory KMS deletion waiting period

Identify all AWS Regions where the multi-Region KMS key or any of its replicas currently exist. Schedule the key for deletion in each Region where it is present, using the minimum waiting period of 7 days in every Region
respostas_corretas: Identify all AWS Regions where the multi-Region KMS key or any of its replicas currently exist. Schedule the key for deletion in each Region where it is present, using the minimum waiting period of 7 days in every Region
explicacao:
Correct option:

Identify all AWS Regions where the multi-Region KMS key or any of its replicas currently exist. Schedule the key for deletion in each Region where it is present, using the minimum waiting period of 7 days in every Region

The key ARN contains the prefix mrk-, which identifies this as a multi-Region key (MRK). Multi-Region keys consist of a primary key in one Region and replica keys in one or more additional Regions. All replicas share the same key material and key ID. Because each replica can independently encrypt and decrypt data, a multi-Region key remains usable as long as any replica exists in any Region. To completely eliminate the key, the security team must schedule deletion for the primary key and every replica in every Region where they exist.

AWS requires that all replica keys be scheduled for deletion before the primary key can be deleted. Attempting to delete the primary key while replicas still exist produces an error. The minimum waiting period for KMS key deletion is 7 days, and this minimum applies to both primary and replica keys in every Region. There is no API or mechanism that bypasses this waiting period, even in incident response scenarios. The 7-day window protects against accidental deletion while still allowing prompt action. The team should use the minimum 7-day window since the requirement is to delete the key as quickly as possible.

The reason the key remains active despite a previous deletion schedule is most likely that deletion was scheduled only for the primary key in one Region, while one or more replica keys continue to exist in other Regions. The security team must enumerate all Regions containing the key using the KMS console or the kms:ListKeys and kms:DescribeKey APIs across Regions, then schedule deletion with the minimum 7-day waiting period in each.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-overview.html

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-overview.html

Incorrect options:

Call kms:RotateKeyOnDemand on the KMS key to generate new key material. Then schedule the original key material version for immediate deletion because the original version has been superseded by the newly rotated key material and is no longer needed - AWS KMS on-demand key rotation generates new key material for the key, but all previous key material versions are retained and remain available for decrypting data that was encrypted with them. AWS does not allow selective deletion of individual key material versions by version ID. Deleting a KMS key deletes all versions of its key material simultaneously after the mandatory waiting period expires. There is no API to delete only the original key material version while keeping the key active.

Additionally, rotating the key material does not prevent the attacker from using the key if they have a valid session or API credentials that allow them to call kms:GenerateDataKey or kms:Encrypt. The key remains enabled and usable until scheduled for deletion in all Regions and the waiting period expires.

Update the key policy on the KMS key to add an explicit Allow statement for kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion and all other kms:* actions, specifying the security team's IAM role ARN as an additional key administrator. Then reissue the kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion API call for the key ARN and specify a waiting period of 7 days to satisfy the mandatory KMS deletion waiting period - The key policy already grants the IAM principal kms:* permissions, which already includes kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion. Adding another explicit Allow statement and a key administrator to the key policy is redundant and does not resolve any permission problem. The problem is not a missing permission; it is that deletion was not scheduled across all Regions where the multi-Region key and its replicas exist.

Modifying a key policy during an active security incident also introduces unnecessary changes that complicate the forensic record and audit trail. The policy modification adds no value and does not accelerate the deletion of the key across all Regions.

Call the kms:CancelKeyDeletion API on the KMS key to reset its pending deletion status. Then immediately call kms:DeleteKey on the key ARN to remove the key from the account without any waiting period - The kms:CancelKeyDeletion API cancels a pending deletion schedule and returns the key to an enabled or disabled state. Calling this API moves the key further away from deletion, not closer to it, making this the opposite of what is needed. Furthermore, there is no kms:DeleteKey API in AWS KMS that immediately destroys a key without a waiting period. The only deletion mechanism in AWS KMS is kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion, which enforces a mandatory waiting period of 7 to 30 days. Immediate key deletion is intentionally prevented in AWS KMS to protect against accidental loss.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-overview.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/deleting-keys.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/rotate-keys.html

Domínio Domain 2: Incident Response
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company runs a third-party software package on a fleet of Amazon EC2 instances and on-premises servers. The operations team occasionally needs shell access to these machines to perform maintenance. Outside of those maintenance windows, the servers must remain isolated from the network and must not accept general inbound administrative access. The company does not want to manage SSH key pairs. The company also wants to minimize cost and pay only for the compute time consumed during maintenance access sessions.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Install a VPN client on every Amazon EC2 instance and on-premises server, establish on-demand site-to-site tunnels for maintenance windows, and authenticate administrators through temporary local operating system accounts instead of SSH keys
Create a dedicated interface VPC endpoint for each server so administrators can connect privately to the machines only during approved maintenance windows
Deploy a bastion host, configure SSH port forwarding through the bastion host, and route administrators through the bastion host whenever they need shell access to the servers
Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager to provide temporary shell access to the servers without managing SSH keys
respostas_corretas: Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager to provide temporary shell access to the servers without managing SSH keys
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager to provide temporary shell access to the servers without managing SSH keys

AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides interactive shell access to managed instances without requiring inbound SSH access or SSH key management. Administrators can connect through the Systems Manager service, which satisfies the requirement to avoid maintaining SSH key pairs and helps keep the machines isolated from the rest of the network when they are not being accessed.

Session Manager also works for both Amazon EC2 instances and on-premises servers that are configured as managed nodes in AWS Systems Manager. This makes it a strong fit for a hybrid environment where the same administrative access model must support resources running in AWS and outside AWS.

This approach provides the least operational effort among the available choices for secure temporary access. It removes the need for bastion host administration, avoids direct inbound SSH exposure, and supports controlled maintenance access through a managed service.

Incorrect options:

Deploy a bastion host, configure SSH port forwarding through the bastion host, and route administrators through the bastion host whenever they need shell access to the servers - A bastion host can centralize SSH access, but it still relies on SSH and typically requires key management or some other credential management approach. That directly conflicts with the requirement to avoid maintaining SSH keys. A bastion host also introduces additional infrastructure that must remain available, monitored, patched, and secured. The servers would still depend on a network path for SSH access rather than being accessed through a managed session service.

Install a VPN client on every Amazon EC2 instance and on-premises server, establish on-demand site-to-site tunnels for maintenance windows, and authenticate administrators through temporary local operating system accounts instead of SSH keys - A VPN-based design can provide private connectivity, but it does not eliminate the operational burden of managing machine-level access controls and temporary local accounts. It also fails the requirement to avoid maintaining SSH-related administrative complexity in practice. This approach is also heavier operationally than necessary. The company wants occasional maintenance access with minimal overhead, and Systems Manager Session Manager already provides that without requiring inbound SSH exposure or additional tunneling infrastructure.

Create a dedicated interface VPC endpoint for each server so administrators can connect privately to the machines only during approved maintenance windows - Interface VPC endpoints provide private connectivity from resources in a VPC to supported AWS services. They are not a per-machine remote access mechanism for administrators to connect to EC2 instances or on-premises servers. This option also does not solve the problem of SSH key management or temporary shell access. It focuses on service connectivity rather than administrative session access.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager-getting-started-instance-profile.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/systems-manager-hybrid-multicloud.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/concepts.html

temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A large enterprise uses AWS Organizations to manage dozens of AWS accounts that are grouped into multiple organizational units. The company stores business data in Amazon S3 buckets across these accounts. Teams in different OUs already access the buckets through existing permissions, and that access must continue to work without modification.

The security team needs to ensure that IAM principals that do not belong to the same AWS organization cannot access any of the company’s Amazon S3 buckets. The team wants to enforce this requirement at the organization level without changing the current access model for internal accounts and OUs.

Which solution should the security team implement?
opcoes: Deploy a service control policy that uses the aws:ResourceOrgID condition with ${aws:PrincipalOrgID} so that only principals from the same AWS organization can access the buckets
Create an AWS Config rule that detects access from principals outside the organization and automatically removes the associated bucket permissions
Configure an Amazon S3 bucket policy on each bucket that denies requests unless the aws:userid value matches an approved list of internal IAM principals
Deploy a service control policy that compares aws:ResourceOrgPaths to ${aws:PrincipalOrgPaths} so that only principals from matching organizational paths can access the given buckets
respostas_corretas: Deploy a service control policy that uses the aws:ResourceOrgID condition with ${aws:PrincipalOrgID} so that only principals from the same AWS organization can access the buckets
explicacao:
Correct option:

Deploy a service control policy that uses the aws:ResourceOrgID condition with ${aws:PrincipalOrgID} so that only principals from the same AWS organization can access the buckets

Using a service control policy with the aws:ResourceOrgID and ${aws:PrincipalOrgID} condition enforces an organization-wide boundary based on AWS Organizations membership. This approach allows access only when the principal and the target resource belong to the same organization. Because the condition evaluates organization identity instead of OU-specific paths, internal access across multiple OUs can continue without requiring the company to redesign existing bucket permissions.

This solution works well when the requirement is to block principals from outside the organization while preserving current access for accounts that are already part of the organization. The policy acts as a guardrail at the AWS Organizations layer and prevents external principals from accessing S3 resources, even if a bucket policy or identity-based policy might otherwise allow the request. Internal principals remain unaffected as long as they belong to the same organization and have the necessary IAM and bucket permissions.

The key advantage is that this model enforces a broad organization-level restriction without introducing OU-to-OU segmentation. A condition based on organization paths would be too restrictive because it would require the principal and resource to align on specific paths. By using organization ID instead, the company satisfies the requirement to block external principals while preserving access patterns among its existing organizational units.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/tutorial_cross-account-with-roles.html

Incorrect options:

Configure an Amazon S3 bucket policy on each bucket that denies requests unless the aws:userid value matches an approved list of internal IAM principals - Using individual bucket policies with explicit aws:userid allowlists would require the company to maintain principal-specific mappings for every bucket. That approach does not scale well across many accounts and OUs, especially in a large organization with evolving IAM roles and principals.

This design also shifts enforcement to each bucket instead of applying a centralized organization-level guardrail. The requirement asks for a solution that prevents external principals while preserving internal access. A principal allowlist is operationally complex and brittle, and it does not take advantage of AWS Organizations context keys that are designed for this exact use case.

Create an AWS Config rule that detects access from principals outside the organization and automatically removes the associated bucket permissions - AWS Config can detect configuration drift and noncompliant resource states, and remediation actions can help correct issues after detection. However, Config is not a preventative access-control mechanism for real-time authorization decisions against Amazon S3.

An external principal could potentially access a bucket before the Config rule evaluates the configuration and triggers remediation. The requirement is to prevent access from outside the organization, not to detect it after the fact and attempt cleanup. Because this option does not provide immediate policy enforcement during authorization, it does not satisfy the requirement.

Deploy a service control policy that compares aws:ResourceOrgPaths to ${aws:PrincipalOrgPaths} so that only principals from matching organizational paths can access the given buckets - A service control policy that compares aws:ResourceOrgPaths with ${aws:PrincipalOrgPaths} evaluates whether the principal and the resource belong to matching organizational paths. That condition is more restrictive than the requirement because it can limit access between different OUs inside the same organization.

The scenario states that the solution must not affect existing access that the OUs already have to the S3 buckets. If internal access currently occurs across multiple OUs, a path-based comparison can block that access when the paths do not match exactly. As a result, this option introduces unnecessary OU-level restrictions and does not preserve the current internal access model.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_condition-keys.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/evaluate-config.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
An organization recently rotated EC2 instance host keys in CloudHSM to comply with security policies, but after the rotation, users attempting to connect via EC2 Instance Connect receive host key validation failures and cannot establish connections. The security team confirms that the new host keys are correctly installed on the EC2 instances and SSH connectivity tests from alternative sources succeed, indicating the issue is specific to EC2 Instance Connect functionality.

What is the most likely cause of the connection failures?
opcoes: Host key validation failures indicate that Systems Manager Session Manager must be enabled as a prerequisite to EC2 Instance Connect
The EC2 Instance Connect service requires host key entries to be stored in AWS Secrets Manager before authentication succeeds
The new host keys must be uploaded to the AWS trusted host keys database to enable EC2 Instance Connect verification
The EC2 instance profile lacks the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore IAM policy required for EC2 Instance Connect to function
respostas_corretas: The EC2 instance profile lacks the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore IAM policy required for EC2 Instance Connect to function
explicacao:
Correct option:

The EC2 instance profile lacks the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore IAM policy required for EC2 Instance Connect to function

EC2 Instance Connect requires specific IAM permissions on the EC2 instance profile to function correctly, including the ability to retrieve temporary SSH public keys from the EC2 Instance Connect API and verify them with the running EC2 instance. The AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore IAM policy grants these necessary permissions and allows the EC2 instance to communicate with AWS Systems Manager services.

Without this policy attached to the instance profile, the EC2 instance cannot authenticate the temporary SSH public key that EC2 Instance Connect generates during the connection process. This explains why standard SSH key pairs and direct SSH connections work (they do not require EC2 Instance Connect API communication), but EC2 Instance Connect connections fail.

The host key rotation itself is not the issue, as host keys are used for server identity verification, not client authentication. The problem manifests as a host key validation failure because the connection attempt fails before host key negotiation completes successfully.

Incorrect options:

The EC2 Instance Connect service requires host key entries to be stored in AWS Secrets Manager before authentication succeeds - AWS Secrets Manager stores application secrets, database credentials, and API keys, not SSH host keys or EC2 Instance Connect credentials. EC2 Instance Connect does not interact with Secrets Manager or require host key entries to be stored there. Host key validation occurs through SSH protocol mechanisms, not through AWS secrets management services, making this option technically infeasible.

The new host keys must be uploaded to the AWS trusted host keys database to enable EC2 Instance Connect verification - AWS does not maintain a centralized "trusted host keys database" where administrators upload EC2 instance host keys. EC2 Instance Connect does not validate instance identity through any AWS-managed database of host keys; it validates the SSH connection through IAM policies and temporary credentials. This option misrepresents how EC2 Instance Connect functions and does not match any actual AWS service capability.

Host key validation failures indicate that Systems Manager Session Manager must be enabled as a prerequisite to EC2 Instance Connect - AWS Systems Manager Session Manager is a separate session management feature that provides shell access to EC2 instances without requiring SSH key management. While Session Manager also uses the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy, it is not a prerequisite for EC2 Instance Connect to function. EC2 Instance Connect and Session Manager are independent features, and enabling Session Manager does not resolve EC2 Instance Connect host key validation failures.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/connect-linux-inst-eic.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-instance-connect-set-up.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security

pergunta:
A company uses AWS Config rules to detect Amazon S3 buckets that violate internal data protection requirements. The S3 buckets exist across multiple AWS Regions and multiple AWS accounts that are part of an AWS Organizations structure. The security team needs a centralized solution that can address buckets that are already noncompliant and can also remediate buckets that become noncompliant in the future. The solution must work across the organization and across Regions with minimal ongoing administrative effort.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation, and attach a service control policy to all organizational units that denies API actions that could create noncompliant S3 buckets

Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation across accounts and Regions, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function whenever AWS Config identifies a noncompliant S3 bucket so the function can correct or remove the bucket configuration
Deploy an AWS Config aggregator that includes only the accounts and Regions the company currently uses, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function when AWS Config reports a noncompliant S3 bucket

Deploy AWS Security Hub across the organization, aggregate findings from all accounts and Regions, and use Security Hub automation rules to delete or reconfigure noncompliant S3 buckets whenever related findings appear
respostas_corretas: Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation across accounts and Regions, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function whenever AWS Config identifies a noncompliant S3 bucket so the function can correct or remove the bucket configuration
explicacao:
Correct option:

Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation across accounts and Regions, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function whenever AWS Config identifies a noncompliant S3 bucket so the function can correct or remove the bucket configuration

An AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation provides centralized visibility into compliance results across multiple AWS accounts and multiple AWS Regions. This is the right foundation for the requirement because the company already uses AWS Config rules to identify noncompliant S3 buckets, and the aggregator can collect those results across the entire organization.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aggregate-data.html

Using an AWS Lambda function for remediation addresses the second part of the requirement, which is to remediate both existing noncompliant buckets and future noncompliant buckets. When AWS Config detects a violation, the remediation workflow can invoke Lambda to reconfigure the bucket or take another corrective action. This makes the solution both centralized and automated.

This design meets all stated requirements. It covers current and future noncompliant resources, works across accounts and Regions, and uses AWS Config in the way the service is intended to support organizational compliance visibility and remediation.

Incorrect options:

Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation, and attach a service control policy to all organizational units that denies API actions that could create noncompliant S3 buckets - An organization-wide AWS Config aggregator is useful for visibility, but a service control policy does not remediate resources that already exist. SCPs act as permission guardrails and can restrict future API actions, but they do not inspect and repair noncompliant S3 buckets that are already present. This option therefore addresses prevention only partially and does not satisfy the requirement to remediate existing noncompliant buckets. It also assumes that an SCP can clean up existing drift, which is not how SCPs operate.

Deploy an AWS Config aggregator that includes only the accounts and Regions the company currently uses, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function when AWS Config reports a noncompliant S3 bucket - A remediation Lambda function tied to AWS Config findings is a valid way to correct noncompliant S3 buckets. However, limiting the aggregator to only the accounts and Regions the company currently uses creates a future coverage gap. The scenario explicitly mentions a multi-account, multi-Region organization and requires a solution for future noncompliant buckets as well. If new accounts or Regions come into use, the limited scope would miss them unless administrators manually updated the aggregator. That makes this option less suitable than organization-wide aggregation.

Deploy AWS Security Hub across the organization, aggregate findings from all accounts and Regions, and use Security Hub automation rules to delete or reconfigure noncompliant S3 buckets whenever related findings appear - AWS Security Hub is useful for aggregating and prioritizing security findings, but it is not the primary service for evaluating organization-wide AWS Config rule compliance and remediating S3 bucket configuration drift. Security Hub can centralize findings, but the underlying remediation requirement here is directly tied to AWS Config noncompliance. This option also overstates what Security Hub automation rules do in this context. Security Hub can support workflow actions and integration patterns, but the direct and native fit for this requirement is AWS Config plus remediation logic. Therefore, this option introduces an unnecessary extra layer and does not align as cleanly with the problem.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aggregate-data.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aggregated-create.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/mt/manage-custom-aws-config-rules-with-remediation-using-conformance-packs/

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/what-is-securityhub.html

Domínio Domain 6: Security Foundations and Governance
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A security engineer is troubleshooting an AWS Lambda function named DataProcessingFunction that fails when it attempts to read objects in an Amazon S3 bucket named ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET. The S3 bucket has the following bucket policy:

{ "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "s3:GetObject", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET", "Condition": { "ArnLike": { "aws:SourceArn": "arn:aws:lambda:::function:DataProcessingFunction" } } } Which change should the security engineer make to the policy to allow the Lambda function to read objects in the bucket?
opcoes: Remove the ArnLike condition block and replace it with a StringEquals condition using the aws:SourceAccount key set to the 12-digit AWS account ID to broaden the permission scope beyond a single function ARN

Add a second bucket policy statement with Effect Allow, Action s3:ListBucket, and Resource arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET, because the s3:GetObject permission requires a prior successful bucket-level listing call to enumerate object keys before the Lambda runtime can retrieve any individual objects from the bucket
Change the Resource element to arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET/* to target the objects within the bucket rather than the bucket itself
Remove the Condition element and change the Principal to specify the Lambda function's IAM execution role ARN instead of the Lambda service principal
respostas_corretas: Change the Resource element to arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET/* to target the objects within the bucket rather than the bucket itself
explicacao:
Correct option:

Change the Resource element to arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET/* to target the objects within the bucket rather than the bucket itself

In Amazon S3, bucket ARNs and object ARNs are distinct resources that require separate permission grants. The bucket ARN format arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET-NAME refers to the bucket resource itself, while the object ARN format arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET-NAME/* refers to all objects within that bucket. The s3:GetObject action applies to objects, not to the bucket. When the Resource element specifies only the bucket ARN without the trailing /*, the policy grants s3:GetObject on a resource type that the action does not support, which causes the permission evaluation to fail.

Changing the Resource element to arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET/* corrects this mismatch by pointing the permission at the actual resource type that s3:GetObject requires. With this change, the policy allows the Lambda service principal to call s3:GetObject for any object within the bucket, subject to the ArnLike condition that restricts the caller to the DataProcessingFunction Lambda function ARN.

The remaining elements of the policy are correctly configured. The Service principal lambda.amazonaws.com is the appropriate principal for a bucket policy that grants S3 access to a Lambda function. The ArnLike condition using aws:SourceArn scopes the permission to the specific function, which follows the principle of least privilege for cross-service access controls.

Incorrect options:

Remove the Condition element and change the Principal to specify the Lambda function's IAM execution role ARN instead of the Lambda service principal - Removing the Condition element and switching the Principal to the Lambda function's IAM execution role ARN is one valid approach to granting S3 access to a Lambda function, but it is not the minimal change required to fix the existing policy. The current policy uses the Lambda service principal with a source ARN condition, which is a supported pattern. The root cause of the failure is the Resource element not including the /*, not the principal configuration.

Using the IAM role ARN as the principal in a bucket policy is an alternative design, not a correction to the identified error. The least disruptive fix is to adjust the Resource element to match the resource type required by the s3:GetObject action.

Add a second bucket policy statement with Effect Allow, Action s3:ListBucket, and Resource arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET, because the s3:GetObject permission requires a prior successful bucket-level listing call to enumerate object keys before the Lambda runtime can retrieve any individual objects from the bucket - The s3:ListBucket permission is used for operations that list the objects in a bucket, such as the ListObjectsV2 API call. It is not a prerequisite for s3:GetObject when the application already knows the object key. The Lambda function calling s3:GetObject with a specific object key does not require a preceding ListBucket call. Adding a ListBucket statement does not fix the Resource element error that is causing the current failure.

Even if ListBucket were needed for the application's logic, granting it without fixing the Resource element for s3:GetObject would still leave the original error unresolved. The s3:GetObject grant must reference object ARNs with the trailing /* to function correctly.

Remove the ArnLike condition block and replace it with a StringEquals condition using the aws:SourceAccount key set to the 12-digit AWS account ID to broaden the permission scope beyond a single function ARN - Replacing the ArnLike condition with aws:SourceAccount broadens the permission scope to allow any Lambda function in the account to access the bucket, rather than restricting access to the specific DataProcessingFunction. While this technically allows the function to access the bucket, it grants more access than necessary and violates the principle of least privilege. The existing ArnLike condition with the specific function ARN is the more precise and preferred design.

Furthermore, changing the condition block does not address the root cause of the failure. The policy fails because the Resource element references the bucket ARN instead of the object ARN. Modifying the condition does not resolve this resource-type mismatch.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/example-bucket-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-policy-language-overview.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
