pergunta:
A global enterprise runs all its cryptographic workloads through an AWS CloudHSM cluster that resides in a shared services AWS account managed by the central security team. A newly formed product team has provisioned a dedicated AWS account. The product team's applications must use the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account for key management and cryptographic operations.

How should a security engineer enable the new dedicated account to use the CloudHSM cluster hosted in the shared services account?
opcoes: Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the CloudHSM cluster resource ID directly with the new dedicated account. After sharing is established, create a CloudHSM cluster client configuration in the new dedicated account using the shared cluster resource ID. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to allow inbound CloudHSM client traffic on the management ports from the private IP addresses of all client instances that the new dedicated account will register with the cluster

Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the VPC subnet ID that hosts the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account with the new dedicated account. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to permit inbound traffic from the private IP addresses of client EC2 instances running in the new dedicated account

Deploy an AWS PrivateLink endpoint service in the shared services account that exposes the CloudHSM cluster. Create an interface VPC endpoint in the new dedicated account's VPC and configure the CloudHSM client software to connect through the PrivateLink endpoint

Use AWS IAM Identity Center to generate an AWS STS token that authenticates from the new dedicated account to the shared services account. Configure the STS token's cross-account permissions to allow the new account to invoke operations on the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account
respostas_corretas: Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the VPC subnet ID that hosts the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account with the new dedicated account. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to permit inbound traffic from the private IP addresses of client EC2 instances running in the new dedicated account
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the VPC subnet ID that hosts the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account with the new dedicated account. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to permit inbound traffic from the private IP addresses of client EC2 instances running in the new dedicated account

AWS CloudHSM is a network-attached hardware security module. The CloudHSM client software running on EC2 instances communicates with the HSM over a private network connection within a VPC using the CloudHSM proprietary protocol. To allow EC2 instances in a different AWS account to reach the HSM, those instances must be able to connect to the HSM's private IP address in the shared services account VPC. AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) enables resource sharing across accounts, and one of its supported shareable resource types is VPC subnets.

By sharing the subnet where the CloudHSM cluster resides, the new dedicated account can launch EC2 instances directly into that shared subnet. Those instances occupy the same network segment as the HSM and can communicate with it over private IP addresses. The CloudHSM cluster security group must be updated to allow inbound connections from the private IP addresses of the client instances, enabling the CloudHSM client-to-HSM communication on the required management ports.

This is the documented and recommended AWS approach for sharing a CloudHSM cluster across multiple accounts. The security group control restricts access to specific client instance IP addresses rather than broad IP ranges, maintaining network-level least privilege. The shared subnet model requires no additional gateway components, VPC peering, or AWS PrivateLink configurations, making it operationally straightforward and consistent with AWS architecture patterns.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ram/latest/userguide/shareable.html

Incorrect options:

Deploy an AWS PrivateLink endpoint service in the shared services account that exposes the CloudHSM cluster. Create an interface VPC endpoint in the new dedicated account's VPC and configure the CloudHSM client software to connect through the PrivateLink endpoint - AWS PrivateLink provides private connectivity between VPCs without using internet gateways or VPC peering. However, CloudHSM is not a service that supports being exposed through a PrivateLink endpoint service. CloudHSM clusters communicate using a proprietary protocol to the HSM's private IP address directly, and this communication path is not compatible with PrivateLink's model of service endpoints backed by Network Load Balancers. There is no supported mechanism to wrap a CloudHSM cluster in a PrivateLink endpoint service.

This distractor describes an architecture that does not exist for CloudHSM in practice. The correct cross-account mechanism is RAM subnet sharing, not PrivateLink.

Use AWS IAM Identity Center to generate an AWS STS token that authenticates from the new dedicated account to the shared services account. Configure the STS token's cross-account permissions to allow the new account to invoke operations on the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account - IAM Identity Center and STS tokens govern access to the AWS management API plane, not to the CloudHSM hardware data plane. The CloudHSM client software establishes a direct network socket connection to the HSM using the CloudHSM proprietary protocol. This communication does not use IAM authentication, STS temporary credentials, or any AWS service API endpoint. STS tokens cannot provide or substitute for the network connectivity required for CloudHSM client-to-HSM communication.

This option conflates management plane authentication with data plane network connectivity, which are two independent layers that operate differently for CloudHSM.

Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the CloudHSM cluster resource ID directly with the new dedicated account. After sharing is established, create a CloudHSM cluster client configuration in the new dedicated account using the shared cluster resource ID. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to allow inbound CloudHSM client traffic on the management ports from the private IP addresses of all client instances that the new dedicated account will register with the cluster - AWS RAM does not support sharing CloudHSM cluster resources by cluster ID. The shareable resource types in AWS RAM for enabling cross-account CloudHSM access are VPC subnets, not CloudHSM cluster objects. Attempting to share a CloudHSM cluster resource ID through AWS RAM will fail because CloudHSM clusters are not in the list of resource types that AWS RAM supports for sharing. The cluster ID cannot be shared as a discrete resource through this mechanism.

This option correctly identifies the security group update requirement but incorrectly identifies what the RAM sharing mechanism operates on. Sharing the subnet (not the cluster) is what enables the new account's instances to join the same network and reach the HSM over private IP addresses.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudhsm/latest/userguide/configure-sg.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ram/latest/userguide/what-is.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ram/latest/userguide/shareable.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company is deploying a new workload across multiple AWS accounts in an AWS Organizations environment. The finance team requires every supported resource to include a tag named CostCenter. The tag value must be one of three approved values that map to internal billing codes. The company must enforce the allowed CostCenter values during create and tag operations, and it also must prevent users from creating resources without the CostCenter tag.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the approved values, and create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes an AWS Lambda function whenever a noncompliant CostCenter tag is created so the function can block further tag changes

Create an AWS Organizations backup policy that requires a CostCenter tag on all supported resources and configure the policy to reject any resource operation when the CostCenter tag is missing or invalid

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the three approved values, turn on enforcement for supported resource types, and create a service control policy that denies create operations when the aws:RequestTag/CostCenter condition key is null

Create an organization-wide AWS Config rule that checks all resources for the CostCenter tag and automatically remediates missing or invalid values by applying one of the approved values based on the resource type
respostas_corretas: Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the three approved values, turn on enforcement for supported resource types, and create a service control policy that denies create operations when the aws:RequestTag/CostCenter condition key is null
explicacao:
Correct option:

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the three approved values, turn on enforcement for supported resource types, and create a service control policy that denies create operations when the aws:RequestTag/CostCenter condition key is null

AWS Organizations tag policies are the native mechanism for standardizing tag keys and allowed tag values across accounts in an organization. AWS documents that tag policies can define acceptable values for a tag such as CostCenter and can enforce noncompliant operations on supported resource types. This directly meets the requirement to allow only one of three approved CostCenter values and to prevent the tag from being changed to an unapproved value on supported services.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html

However, AWS also documents that tag policies alone do not evaluate untagged resources unless you use the required tag key capability or another control. To prevent resource creation when the CostCenter tag is missing, an SCP can deny create operations when aws:RequestTag/CostCenter is null. AWS IAM documentation shows that aws:RequestTag/tag-key is the correct condition key pattern for controlling which tag values can be passed in a request.

This combination gives the company both controls it needs. The tag policy governs the approved values and blocks noncompliant tag operations for supported resource types, while the SCP forces users to supply the CostCenter tag during creation. It is the most direct and centralized solution.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_example-tag-policies.html

Incorrect options:

Create an AWS Organizations backup policy that requires a CostCenter tag on all supported resources and configure the policy to reject any resource operation when the CostCenter tag is missing or invalid - AWS Organizations backup policies are used to manage backup plans and backup-related governance. They are not the control mechanism for required resource tags or enforcement of approved tag values. This option therefore uses the wrong type of organizational policy. The requirement is specifically about tagging governance, which is handled by tag policies and IAM or SCP tag condition keys, not backup policies.

Create an organization-wide AWS Config rule that checks all resources for the CostCenter tag and automatically remediates missing or invalid values by applying one of the approved values based on the resource type - An organization-wide Config rule can help identify noncompliant resources, and remediation can help correct drift after the fact. That makes this design useful for reporting and cleanup. It still does not meet the requirement to enforce the policy at create time. It also introduces a risky remediation pattern by automatically assigning approved values based on resource type, which may not reflect the correct financial ownership of the resource.

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the approved values, and create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes an AWS Lambda function whenever a noncompliant CostCenter tag is created so the function can block further tag changes - The tag policy portion is directionally correct because tag policies are the right service for standardizing allowed tag values across the organization. On supported resource types, they can prevent noncompliant tagging operations. The EventBridge and Lambda part is unnecessary and still reactive. A Lambda function triggered after a noncompliant event is not the right mechanism to block the original tag mutation in real time, and it does not solve the requirement to require the CostCenter tag during create operations.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies-enforcement.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_example-tag-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/WhatIsConfig.html

Domínio Domain 6: Security Foundations and Governance
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A retail e-commerce company runs a customer-facing application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The application processes orders using Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) queues and stores product assets in Amazon S3. The application scales with AWS Auto Scaling. The company's PCI compliance policy mandates least privilege access and requires that all communication between the application and AWS services use private network connectivity. A security engineer must implement private connectivity to the AWS services the application uses.

Which combination of steps should the security engineer take to meet this requirement? (Select three)

Create an interface VPC endpoint for Amazon SQS to route SQS API traffic from the EC2 instances through the AWS private network without traversing the internet

Route Amazon S3 API traffic through AWS Firewall Manager with a managed security policy that centralizes private connectivity enforcement for all S3 endpoint traffic
opcoes: Create a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 to allow the EC2 instances to reach Amazon S3 without internet connectivity
Update the resource policies on all created VPC endpoints to restrict access to only the specific SQS queues and S3 buckets that the application requires

Configure AWS PrivateLink for Amazon S3 by creating a VPC endpoint service in the application VPC, register the resulting endpoint network interfaces with Amazon Route 53 Resolver forwarding rules so that S3 DNS queries resolve to private IP addresses within the VPC CIDR range, and update security group inbound rules on all EC2 instances to allow responses from the endpoint network interface IP addresses
Update the IAM instance profile role attached to the EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group to add permissions that allow outbound network connections to the VPC interface endpoint IP addresses
respostas_corretas: Create a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 to allow the EC2 instances to reach Amazon S3 without internet connectivity
Update the resource policies on all created VPC endpoints to restrict access to only the specific SQS queues and S3 buckets that the application requires
explicacao:
Correct options:

Create an interface VPC endpoint for Amazon SQS to route SQS API traffic from the EC2 instances through the AWS private network without traversing the internet

Creating an interface VPC endpoint for SQS deploys elastic network interfaces with private IP addresses in the designated subnets. EC2 instances in the VPC send SQS API requests to these private IP addresses, and the traffic remains within the AWS network without traversing the internet. This directly satisfies the private connectivity requirement for SQS.

Create a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 to allow the EC2 instances to reach Amazon S3 without internet connectivity

Amazon S3 supports a gateway VPC endpoint, which is a route table entry rather than a network interface. Creating a gateway VPC endpoint for S3 adds prefix list entries to the route tables associated with the EC2 instance subnets so that S3 API traffic is routed directly to the S3 endpoint within the AWS network. No NAT gateway or internet gateway is needed for S3 access. Gateway endpoints for S3 are available at no additional cost and are the recommended approach for private S3 connectivity from a VPC.

Update the resource policies on all created VPC endpoints to restrict access to only the specific SQS queues and S3 buckets that the application requires

VPC endpoint policies control which principals can access which resources through the endpoint. By default, endpoint policies allow all traffic. Updating the endpoint policies to specify only the SQS queues and S3 buckets that the application uses restricts the endpoint to least privilege access and satisfies the PCI compliance requirement that all existing AWS resources use least privilege access. This prevents the endpoints from being used to access other AWS resources outside the application's defined scope.

Incorrect options:

Configure AWS PrivateLink for Amazon S3 by creating a VPC endpoint service in the application VPC, register the resulting endpoint network interfaces with Amazon Route 53 Resolver forwarding rules so that S3 DNS queries resolve to private IP addresses within the VPC CIDR range, and update security group inbound rules on all EC2 instances to allow responses from the endpoint network interface IP addresses - AWS PrivateLink for Amazon S3 through a custom endpoint service and Route 53 Resolver forwarding rules describes a complex custom architecture that does not reflect how VPC endpoints for S3 work. Amazon S3 gateway VPC endpoints are a built-in AWS feature that does not require creating a custom endpoint service, configuring PrivateLink, or setting up DNS resolver forwarding rules. The standard approach of creating a gateway endpoint and updating route tables achieves the same private connectivity outcome with far less configuration complexity.

The architecture described would be relevant for exposing a custom service hosted in one VPC to consumers in another VPC via PrivateLink, which is a different use case from providing private access to Amazon S3. Applying this pattern to S3 introduces unnecessary complexity and potential points of failure.

Update the IAM instance profile role attached to the EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group to add permissions that allow outbound network connections to the VPC interface endpoint IP addresses - IAM role permissions govern what API actions a principal is authorized to perform, not which network paths or endpoints are permitted for outbound traffic. Adding permissions to an IAM role does not affect network routing or connectivity to VPC endpoint IP addresses. Network connectivity to interface endpoints is controlled by subnet route tables, security groups, and endpoint policies, not by IAM instance profile permissions.

The IAM instance profile role already holds the appropriate permissions to call SQS and S3 APIs per the requirement that least privilege has been applied to all existing resources. Creating the VPC endpoints and ensuring the security groups allow HTTPS traffic to the endpoint IP addresses establishes the network connectivity without any changes to the IAM role.

Route Amazon S3 API traffic through AWS Firewall Manager with a managed security policy that centralizes private connectivity enforcement for all S3 endpoint traffic - AWS Firewall Manager is a security management service that centrally configures and enforces AWS WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, and security groups across accounts and Regions. It does not manage or create VPC endpoints for S3 or enforce private connectivity routing. Firewall Manager operates at the layer of web application firewall rules and network security policies, not at the layer of VPC routing for private service access.

Private connectivity to Amazon S3 from a VPC requires a gateway VPC endpoint, which is a VPC routing configuration change. Firewall Manager cannot create or manage gateway endpoints and therefore cannot satisfy the private S3 connectivity requirement.

References: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/create-interface-endpoint.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/gateway-endpoints.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-use-interface-vpc-endpoints-to-meet-your-security-objectives/

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A compliance team at a technology company has deployed an AWS Lambda function to generate daily security reports. The function gathers Amazon Inspector findings from AWS Security Hub in the us-east-1 Region and uses an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule to trigger report generation each day. A security engineer discovers that the Lambda function is failing to produce the reports. The engineer must resolve the failure and ensure the function's execution role follows the principle of least privilege.

Which solution will meet these requirements?
opcoes: Attach the AWS managed AWSSecurityHubReadOnlyAccess policy to the Lambda function's execution role to provide complete read access to all Security Hub data across all products and standards
Create a custom IAM policy that grants read-only permissions scoped to Amazon Inspector findings and AWS Security Hub resources in us-east-1, and attach it to the Lambda function's execution role

Create a separate IAM role with both the SecurityAudit and ReadOnlyAccess AWS managed policies attached, then update the Lambda function code to call the sts:AssumeRole API at runtime to assume this broader-privilege role before executing Security Hub and Inspector API queries

Write an inline IAM policy that explicitly allows Get, List, Batch, and Describe actions scoped to the arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::product/aws/inspector/* resource ARN and attach this policy to the Lambda function's execution role to restrict access to Inspector-sourced findings only
respostas_corretas: Create a custom IAM policy that grants read-only permissions scoped to Amazon Inspector findings and AWS Security Hub resources in us-east-1, and attach it to the Lambda function's execution role
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create a custom IAM policy that grants read-only permissions scoped to Amazon Inspector findings and AWS Security Hub resources in us-east-1, and attach it to the Lambda function's execution role

The Lambda function requires permission to read Amazon Inspector findings that are aggregated in AWS Security Hub. The correct least privilege approach is to create a custom IAM policy that grants only the specific read-only actions needed to query Security Hub findings and, if necessary, Inspector data in the us-east-1 Region. Attaching this custom policy directly to the function's execution role ensures the role holds exactly the permissions required and nothing more.

A custom policy scoped to the relevant services and Region satisfies both the functional requirement to access the findings data and the security requirement to apply least privilege. The engineer can identify the precise API actions the function uses, such as securityhub:GetFindings or securityhub:BatchGetFindings, and include only those actions in the policy. This targeted approach avoids granting access to Security Hub features and data sources the function does not need.

The failure is caused by the execution role lacking the necessary permissions to call Security Hub APIs. Attaching a properly scoped custom read-only policy to the execution role resolves the failure without introducing excess permissions.

Incorrect options:

Attach the AWS managed AWSSecurityHubReadOnlyAccess policy to the Lambda function's execution role to provide complete read access to all Security Hub data across all products and standards - The AWSSecurityHubReadOnlyAccess AWS managed policy grants read access to all Security Hub findings, standards, insights, and product integrations across the entire account. This provides far broader access than the function requires since the function only needs to read Inspector findings in a single Region. Attaching this managed policy violates the principle of least privilege by granting access to Security Hub data from all integrated products and all Regions. Least privilege requires granting only the specific permissions needed for the function's defined task. A custom policy scoped to the relevant actions and Region is more appropriate than a broad managed policy that covers all Security Hub capabilities.

Create a separate IAM role with both the SecurityAudit and ReadOnlyAccess AWS managed policies attached, then update the Lambda function code to call the sts:AssumeRole API at runtime to assume this broader-privilege role before executing Security Hub and Inspector API queries - Configuring the function to assume a secondary role with SecurityAudit and ReadOnlyAccess managed policies adds unnecessary complexity and grants far more permissions than the function requires. SecurityAudit grants read access to a wide range of AWS services and configuration data, while ReadOnlyAccess provides read access to virtually all AWS services. Both policies violate the principle of least privilege for a function that only needs to read Inspector findings from Security Hub. Using sts:AssumeRole at runtime also requires the execution role to hold the sts:AssumeRole permission, adds latency to each function invocation for the assume role API call, and introduces an additional role to manage. The simpler and more secure approach is to attach a minimal custom policy directly to the execution role.

Write an inline IAM policy that explicitly allows Get, List, Batch, and Describe actions scoped to the arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::product/aws/inspector/* resource ARN and attach this policy to the Lambda function's execution role to restrict access to Inspector-sourced findings only - The resource ARN arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::product/aws/inspector/* is used with some Security Hub resource-level permission restrictions, but many Security Hub API actions such as securityhub:GetFindings do not support resource-level restrictions and require a wildcard resource. Scoping all Security Hub actions to this ARN may prevent certain required API calls from succeeding because the specified resource pattern does not match the resource type expected by those actions. Additionally, this approach grants wildcard action patterns such as Get, List, Batch, and Describe on Security Hub without verifying which specific actions are required. A correct least privilege policy identifies the exact actions the function invokes rather than using wildcards. Option Create a custom IAM policy that grants read-only permissions scoped to Amazon Inspector findings and AWS Security Hub resources in us-east-1, and attach it to the Lambda function's execution role is more precise and operationally correct.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/security-iam.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/nine-aws-security-hub-best-practices/

Domínio Domain 2: Incident Response
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A financial technology company runs trading platform microservices on Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS). The company needs a solution to detect Kubernetes security risks by monitoring Amazon EKS audit logs as well as operating system events, network activity, and file system changes on EKS nodes. When risks are identified, the solution must send email alerts to the security team's mailing list.

Which solution will meet these requirements?
opcoes: Configure AWS CloudTrail to capture all Kubernetes API server calls on the EKS cluster, build a CloudWatch Logs Insights query to identify suspicious API activity patterns, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and deploy an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule that triggers an AWS Lambda function every hour to execute the query and publish identified findings to the SNS topic

Activate Amazon GuardDuty, enable EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring for the EKS cluster, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to route relevant GuardDuty findings to the SNS topic for email delivery

Activate AWS Security Hub and enable the security standards that include Amazon EKS controls, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to forward relevant Security Hub findings to the SNS topic

Enable Amazon Inspector for container image scanning, configure Amazon Detective to correlate EKS security events, set up Amazon CloudWatch log groups to capture EKS audit logs, and deploy an AWS Lambda function to process log events and dispatch email alerts to the security team
respostas_corretas: Activate Amazon GuardDuty, enable EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring for the EKS cluster, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to route relevant GuardDuty findings to the SNS topic for email delivery
explicacao:
Correct option:

Activate Amazon GuardDuty, enable EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring for the EKS cluster, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to route relevant GuardDuty findings to the SNS topic for email delivery

Amazon GuardDuty provides purpose-built threat detection for Amazon EKS through two distinct protection features. EKS Audit Log Monitoring analyzes Kubernetes audit log events to detect suspicious activity such as unauthorized API calls, privilege escalation attempts, and anomalous access patterns within the cluster control plane. EKS Runtime Monitoring deploys a GuardDuty security agent on EKS nodes to collect operating system-level telemetry including process execution, network connections, and file system activity. Together, these two features address the complete monitoring scope specified in the requirements.

Amazon EventBridge integrates natively with Amazon GuardDuty and generates events whenever GuardDuty produces a new finding. A single EventBridge rule can filter GuardDuty findings by severity or finding type and route them to an Amazon SNS topic. Configuring the security team's mailing list as an SNS email subscriber ensures that relevant findings trigger automatic email notifications to the team without requiring any custom code or additional infrastructure.

This architecture uses fully managed AWS services with native integration, minimal configuration, and no custom development. GuardDuty handles the detection logic automatically, EventBridge handles the event routing, and SNS handles the email delivery, which together provide the complete monitoring and alerting capability described in the requirements.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/runtime-monitoring.html

Incorrect options:

Activate AWS Security Hub and enable the security standards that include Amazon EKS controls, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to forward relevant Security Hub findings to the SNS topic - AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from multiple AWS security services and provides compliance dashboards based on security standards. While Security Hub includes some EKS-related controls within its security standards, these controls perform configuration compliance checks rather than real-time threat detection against audit logs and runtime events. Security Hub does not natively monitor EKS audit logs or collect operating system, network, and file system events from EKS nodes.

The requirements specifically call for monitoring EKS audit logs in addition to OS, networking, and file events, which requires GuardDuty EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring. Security Hub alone cannot satisfy this monitoring scope even when EKS security standards are enabled.

Enable Amazon Inspector for container image scanning, configure Amazon Detective to correlate EKS security events, set up Amazon CloudWatch log groups to capture EKS audit logs, and deploy an AWS Lambda function to process log events and dispatch email alerts to the security team - Amazon Inspector scans container images for software vulnerabilities and does not perform runtime threat detection against EKS audit logs or OS-level events. Amazon Detective provides investigative capabilities for analyzing findings from GuardDuty, but it does not independently detect threats or generate findings from EKS logs. CloudWatch log groups can receive EKS audit logs, but processing those logs through a Lambda function to detect threats requires custom threat detection logic that duplicates the functionality already built into GuardDuty.

This approach combines multiple services without providing the comprehensive, native threat detection that GuardDuty EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring offer. The operational complexity of building and maintaining custom threat detection logic is significantly higher than enabling GuardDuty's purpose-built EKS features.

Configure AWS CloudTrail to capture all Kubernetes API server calls on the EKS cluster, build a CloudWatch Logs Insights query to identify suspicious API activity patterns, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and deploy an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule that triggers an AWS Lambda function every hour to execute the query and publish identified findings to the SNS topic - CloudTrail captures Kubernetes API server calls and can provide valuable audit trail data for EKS. However, this approach has significant gaps. A CloudWatch Logs Insights query run on a scheduled hourly basis introduces up to one hour of detection delay between when a threat occurs and when the security team is notified. Real-time threat detection requires continuous analysis, not periodic batch queries. Additionally, building a custom Lambda function to identify security threats in raw audit log data requires developing and maintaining custom detection rules that approximate the managed threat intelligence already built into GuardDuty.

The complexity of operating a custom threat detection pipeline, combined with the inherent latency of hourly scheduled execution, makes this approach significantly inferior to GuardDuty EKS Protection, which provides continuous real-time analysis using AWS-managed threat intelligence.

References: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/kubernetes-protection.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/runtime-monitoring.html

Domínio Domain 1: Detection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company wants to integrate AWS IAM Identity Center with an external identity provider (IdP) to centralize authentication and enable automated user provisioning.

Select and arrange the necessary steps from the list below. You may use each step one time or not at all:

Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source

Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center

Create an IAM role with a trust policy that references the external IdP endpoint

Enable automated user provisioning in IAM Identity Center

Enable automated provisioning capabilities in the external IdP

Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP

Which sequence of steps should a security engineer implement to meet these requirements?
opcoes: Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 3: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP

Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source

Step 1: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 2: Enable automated user provisioning in IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Enable automated provisioning capabilities in the external IdP

Step 1: Create an IAM role with a trust policy that references the external IdP endpoint -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source
respostas_corretas: Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source
explicacao:
Correct option:

Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source

The correct sequence begins with retrieving the SAML metadata from IAM Identity Center. This metadata is required by the external IdP to configure AWS as a trusted service provider and establish the federation endpoints.

Next, retrieving the SAML metadata from the external IdP allows IAM Identity Center to obtain the necessary identity provider details, including certificates and assertion endpoints. This bidirectional metadata exchange is required to establish mutual trust.

Finally, configuring IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source completes the federation setup. At this stage, both sides trust each other, and authentication requests can be securely processed through the external IdP.

Incorrect options:

Step 1: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 2: Enable automated user provisioning in IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Enable automated provisioning capabilities in the external IdP - This sequence attempts to enable provisioning before establishing SAML federation. Provisioning depends on a valid identity source configuration. Without completing metadata exchange and trust establishment, IAM Identity Center cannot synchronize users or authenticate requests.

Step 1: Create an IAM role with a trust policy that references the external IdP endpoint -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source - Creating an IAM role with a trust policy is not part of integrating IAM Identity Center with an external IdP. IAM Identity Center handles federation independently and does not require manually created IAM roles for SAML-based authentication. This step introduces unnecessary complexity and does not contribute to the solution.

Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 3: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP - This sequence is incorrect because it tries to configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP before IAM Identity Center has the IdP’s metadata. For SAML federation to work, both sides must exchange metadata first so they can trust each other. Without the IdP metadata, IAM Identity Center does not have the necessary information such as certificates and endpoints to validate authentication responses, so the setup remains incomplete and will not function correctly.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/manage-your-identity-source.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/gs-okta.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/provision-automatically.html

Domínio Domain 6: Security Foundations and Governance
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

opcoes: Modify the application to use the AWS IoT thing name as the MQTT client ID when establishing a connection to AWS IoT Core
Configure an AWS IoT Core policy that allows iot:Subscribe on topic filters by using wildcards such as topicfilter/* to simplify topic access management across all devices

Implement AWS IoT Core custom authorizers backed by an AWS Lambda function that validates incoming client ID values against a regular expression pattern at connection time, rejecting any MQTT connection whose client ID contains special characters or does not correspond to a registered IoT thing name in the device registry
Attach an AWS IoT Core policy that permits the iot:Connect action on the resource client/${iot:ClientId}
respostas_corretas: Modify the application to use the AWS IoT thing name as the MQTT client ID when establishing a connection to AWS IoT Core
explicacao:
Correct option:

Modify the application to use the AWS IoT thing name as the MQTT client ID when establishing a connection to AWS IoT Core

Using the AWS IoT thing name as the MQTT client ID creates a strong binding between the device identity and the connection identifier. This ensures that each device connects with a predictable and controlled identifier that is registered in AWS IoT Core.

By enforcing the use of thing names, the company prevents attackers from arbitrarily crafting client IDs with injected characters. This aligns the client identity with the AWS IoT registry and reduces the risk of unauthorized topic access due to manipulated identifiers.

Attach an AWS IoT Core policy that permits the iot:Connect action on the resource client/${iot:Connection.Thing.ThingName}

Using an IoT policy that allows the iot:Connect action only when the client ID matches the thing name enforces identity-based access control. The policy variable ${iot:Connection.Thing.ThingName} ensures that the connection request is evaluated against the registered thing name.

This prevents a device from connecting with a spoofed or modified client ID. Even if an attacker attempts to inject special characters, the policy evaluation fails because the client ID does not match the associated thing name, effectively mitigating the threat.

Incorrect options:

Configure an AWS IoT Core policy that allows iot:Subscribe on topic filters by using wildcards such as topicfilter/* to simplify topic access management across all devices - Allowing broad topic access using wildcard filters increases the attack surface and weakens authorization controls. This approach does not restrict client identity or prevent misuse of client IDs. Instead, it makes it easier for attackers to access unintended topics.

Implement AWS IoT Core custom authorizers backed by an AWS Lambda function that validates incoming client ID values against a regular expression pattern at connection time, rejecting any MQTT connection whose client ID contains special characters or does not correspond to a registered IoT thing name in the device registry - AWS IoT Core custom authorizers can perform token-based or header-based authentication for devices that cannot use X.509 certificates. However, implementing a custom authorizer that validates client ID format with a Lambda function introduces additional latency on every connection request and adds an operational dependency on Lambda function availability and cold start behavior. The purpose-built iot:Connection.Thing.ThingName policy variable achieves the same client ID binding enforcement natively within the standard IoT Core policy engine without any custom code.

Custom authorizers are intended for scenarios where standard AWS IoT authentication mechanisms are insufficient, such as when connecting devices that use bearer tokens instead of X.509 certificates. For devices that already use X.509 certificate-based authentication, policy variables provide a simpler and more reliable enforcement mechanism.

Attach an AWS IoT Core policy that permits the iot:Connect action on the resource client/${iot:ClientId} - Allowing iot:Connect on client/${iot:ClientId} permits any client ID that the device presents during connection.

This does not enforce a binding between the client ID and the registered thing. As a result, attackers can still manipulate the client ID to access unauthorized topics, making this option insufficient.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/iot-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/connect-policy.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/thing-policy-variables.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/security-best-practices.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company is deploying a new application into a newly created AWS account. The application VPC and subnets already exist. The application VPC is peered with an existing VPC in another AWS account in the same AWS Region so that selected Amazon EC2 instances can connect to database servers over TCP port 1521. Amazon EC2 instances in the application VPC are launched and terminated frequently, but only a subset of those instances should be able to reach the databases in the peered VPC. A security engineer must implement a network control that ensures only the required application instances can connect to the databases.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Create a new network ACL for the application subnets that allows outbound TCP port 1521 only to the private IP addresses of the database servers in the peered VPC, and create a matching network ACL in the database subnets that allows inbound TCP port 1521 only from the entire application subnet range

Create a new security group in the database VPC that allows TCP port 1521 from the CIDR block of the application VPC, and use an Auto Scaling lifecycle hook to dynamically add and remove temporary host routes for only the approved application instances whenever they launch or terminate

Create a new security group in the database VPC, add an inbound rule that allows all traffic from the CIDR block of the application VPC, add a network ACL rule on the database subnets to allow TCP port 1521 from the application VPC CIDR block, and attach the new security group to the required database instances

Create a new security group in the application VPC with no inbound rules, create a new security group in the database VPC with an inbound rule that allows TCP port 1521 from the new application security group, attach the application security group to only the application instances that require database access, and attach the database security group to the database instances
respostas_corretas: Create a new security group in the application VPC with no inbound rules, create a new security group in the database VPC with an inbound rule that allows TCP port 1521 from the new application security group, attach the application security group to only the application instances that require database access, and attach the database security group to the database instances
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create a new security group in the application VPC with no inbound rules, create a new security group in the database VPC with an inbound rule that allows TCP port 1521 from the new application security group, attach the application security group to only the application instances that require database access, and attach the database security group to the database instances

AWS allows security groups to reference security groups in a peered VPC when the VPCs are in the same Region, even if the VPCs are in different AWS accounts. This lets the security engineer create a database-side security group rule that permits TCP port 1521 only from the specific security group attached to approved application instances.

This is the most precise solution because only the EC2 instances associated with the designated application security group can connect to the database instances. The application fleet can continue to scale dynamically, and only the subset of instances that need database access must be launched with that security group attached.

This approach also minimizes operational effort and follows least privilege. It avoids broad CIDR-based access and avoids subnet-wide controls that would affect instances that should not have database connectivity. AWS specifically documents peer security group references as a way to control traffic between peered VPCs.

Incorrect options:

Create a new security group in the database VPC, add an inbound rule that allows all traffic from the CIDR block of the application VPC, add a network ACL rule on the database subnets to allow TCP port 1521 from the application VPC CIDR block, and attach the new security group to the required database instances - Allowing all traffic from the application VPC CIDR block is much broader than required. The requirement is that only some application instances should be able to access the databases, but this design would allow any instance in the application VPC to reach the database instances as long as routing exists. The added network ACL rule also increases complexity without solving the least-privilege problem. CIDR-based controls at the subnet or VPC level do not distinguish between approved and unapproved application instances the way a security group reference does.

Create a new network ACL for the application subnets that allows outbound TCP port 1521 only to the private IP addresses of the database servers in the peered VPC, and create a matching network ACL in the database subnets that allows inbound TCP port 1521 only from the entire application subnet range - Network ACLs apply at the subnet level, not at the individual instance level. Even though the rules can be narrowed to certain IP ranges or ports, they still do not provide the same workload-level granularity as security groups for a frequently changing EC2 fleet. This option is also operationally heavier because the engineer would need to keep the ACL logic aligned with the database IP layout and the application subnet ranges. It does not scale as cleanly as a peer security group reference for dynamic instances.

Create a new security group in the database VPC that allows TCP port 1521 from the CIDR block of the application VPC, and use an Auto Scaling lifecycle hook to dynamically add and remove temporary host routes for only the approved application instances whenever they launch or terminate - The database security group in this option still grants access from the entire application VPC CIDR, which is broader than the requirement. The added lifecycle-hook and route-management logic makes the design much more complex than necessary and does not actually solve the least-privilege issue at the packet-filtering layer. Routes are also not the right control for selecting which individual instances may talk to the database when VPC peering is already in place. Security groups are the intended mechanism for this type of selective access control between EC2 instances and database servers.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/security-group-rules.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/vpc-peering-security-groups.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/working-with-vpc-peering.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A media streaming platform deploys microservices as Docker containers running on Amazon ECS. The security team requires that all data in transit between streaming clients and backend containers must remain encrypted from origin to destination. The platform expects highly variable traffic loads with sharp spikes during live broadcast events.

Which solution provides the MOST scalability and LOWEST latency while satisfying the encryption requirement?
opcoes: Place an AWS WAF web ACL configured for TLS inspection in front of the ECS containers to inspect and re-encrypt all incoming TLS traffic before forwarding requests to the backend containers

Deploy an AWS Global Accelerator standard accelerator in front of the ECS containers. Configure Global Accelerator to perform TLS offloading at the nearest AWS edge location, then re-establish a separate encrypted tunnel from the edge location to each backend container using a certificate managed by AWS Certificate Manager
Configure a Network Load Balancer to terminate the incoming TLS session and then establish a new encrypted connection to each backend container
Configure a Network Load Balancer with a TCP listener to pass TLS traffic directly through to the ECS containers without terminating the encryption at the load balancer
respostas_corretas: Configure a Network Load Balancer with a TCP listener to pass TLS traffic directly through to the ECS containers without terminating the encryption at the load balancer
explicacao:
Correct option:

Configure a Network Load Balancer with a TCP listener to pass TLS traffic directly through to the ECS containers without terminating the encryption at the load balancer

A Network Load Balancer (NLB) operating with a TCP listener passes network packets to registered targets without inspecting or altering the payload. When TLS traffic arrives at an NLB TCP listener, the NLB forwards the encrypted packets as-is to the backend containers. The containers perform TLS termination themselves, which means the encryption session runs end-to-end from the client to the container. No intermediate component decrypts and re-encrypts the traffic, satisfying the requirement for unbroken encryption from origin to destination.

Network Load Balancers are designed for extreme performance and operate at Layer 4 of the OSI model. They handle millions of requests per second at ultra-low latency because they process TCP flows rather than HTTP requests. The NLB uses a flow-hash algorithm to route packets consistently to the same target for the duration of a connection, ensuring session continuity. It scales automatically to handle sudden traffic spikes such as those generated by live broadcast events, without any pre-warming or manual capacity management.

Combining the NLB TCP pass-through capability with ECS containers that perform their own TLS termination achieves both the encryption requirement and the performance requirement simultaneously. The NLB handles connection routing and elastic scaling while the containers maintain the private keys and TLS session state. This is the recommended AWS pattern for high-throughput workloads that require unbroken encryption between clients and containerized backends.

Listeners for your Network Load Balancers:  via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/load-balancer-listeners.html

Incorrect options:

Configure a Network Load Balancer to terminate the incoming TLS session and then establish a new encrypted connection to each backend container - When an NLB terminates TLS, it decrypts incoming traffic at the load balancer and then establishes a separate TLS session to each backend target. This TLS bridging pattern breaks the unbroken encryption requirement because the NLB gains access to the plaintext payload between termination and re-encryption. The security requirement explicitly prohibits any intermediate component from decrypting the traffic.

Additionally, TLS termination at the NLB requires the load balancer to perform cryptographic operations and manage certificates, which adds processing overhead and latency compared to a pure TCP pass-through that simply routes packets without inspection.

Place an AWS WAF web ACL configured for TLS inspection in front of the ECS containers to inspect and re-encrypt all incoming TLS traffic before forwarding requests to the backend containers - AWS WAF is a web application firewall that filters and monitors HTTP and HTTPS requests based on rules such as SQL injection patterns and rate limits. WAF operates at Layer 7 and requires access to decrypted HTTP payloads to evaluate those rules. TLS inspection in WAF requires TLS termination, which breaks the unbroken encryption chain required by the security team. WAF is fundamentally incompatible with the stated encryption requirement.

AWS WAF also cannot distribute traffic across multiple containers; it must be deployed in front of a CloudFront distribution, an ALB, or an API Gateway endpoint, all of which also terminate TLS. Using WAF here does not meet either the encryption requirement or the scalability and latency requirements.

Deploy an AWS Global Accelerator standard accelerator in front of the ECS containers. Configure Global Accelerator to perform TLS offloading at the nearest AWS edge location, then re-establish a separate encrypted tunnel from the edge location to each backend container using a certificate managed by AWS Certificate Manager - AWS Global Accelerator routes traffic through the AWS global network backbone and can reduce latency by directing traffic to the nearest AWS edge location. However, Global Accelerator does not perform TLS offloading itself; it forwards traffic to a registered endpoint such as an NLB or ALB where termination occurs. Describing Global Accelerator as performing TLS termination at edge locations is technically inaccurate.

Even if Global Accelerator were layered in front of an NLB with a TCP listener, TLS offloading at an edge location and re-encryption to the backend would still break the unbroken encryption chain required by the security team. This option introduces an additional network hop and an additional termination point without satisfying the core requirement.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/introduction.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/load-balancer-listeners.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/load-balancer-types.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/load-balancer-target-groups.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company hosts a public website behind an Application Load Balancer that routes requests to a fleet of stateless Amazon EC2 instances. The application reads data from an Amazon DynamoDB table. The company wants to reduce the impact of malicious scanning activity and application-layer denial-of-service attempts. The company needs to ensure that each client IP address can retrieve data no more than 15 times during any 10-minute period. The solution must require the least implementation effort.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Enable AWS Shield Advanced on the Application Load Balancer and configure proactive engagement so the service automatically enforces a limit of 15 requests per IP address every 10 minutes
Store source IP addresses and request timestamps in the Amazon DynamoDB table, apply a 10-minute TTL to each entry, and limit the table throughput to 15 read operations
Configure AWS WAF for the Application Load Balancer and create a rate-based rule that blocks requests from any source IP address that exceeds 15 requests in a 10-minute evaluation window

Configure an Amazon CloudFront distribution in front of the Application Load Balancer, enable access logging, and use an AWS Lambda@Edge function to reject requests after a client IP address exceeds 15 requests in 10 minutes
respostas_corretas: Configure AWS WAF for the Application Load Balancer and create a rate-based rule that blocks requests from any source IP address that exceeds 15 requests in a 10-minute evaluation window
explicacao:
Correct option:

Configure AWS WAF for the Application Load Balancer and create a rate-based rule that blocks requests from any source IP address that exceeds 15 requests in a 10-minute evaluation window

AWS WAF is designed to protect web applications that are fronted by resources such as an Application Load Balancer. AWS documents that AWS WAF supports rate-based rules, which count requests from each source and apply a block or other action when the configured request rate is exceeded during the evaluation window.

This directly meets the requirement because the company wants to restrict each client IP address to only 15 reads in any 10-minute period. Since the website is already behind an ALB, attaching AWS WAF to the ALB requires little architectural change and avoids modifying the application code or the data model.

This is also the least-effort solution because it uses a native managed control at the application edge. The rate limit can be enforced before requests reach the EC2 instances or DynamoDB-backed application logic, which reduces both operational complexity and application load.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-rate-based-high-level-settings.html

Incorrect options:

Configure an Amazon CloudFront distribution in front of the Application Load Balancer, enable access logging, and use an AWS Lambda@Edge function to reject requests after a client IP address exceeds 15 requests in 10 minutes - This design could be made to work, but it adds more components and more operational complexity than necessary. The company would need to introduce CloudFront and Lambda@Edge even though the ALB can already be protected directly by AWS WAF. Because AWS WAF can attach to an Application Load Balancer and enforce rate-based rules natively, this CloudFront-based approach is not the least-effort solution. It is a plausible architecture, but it is more elaborate than required.

Enable AWS Shield Advanced on the Application Load Balancer and configure proactive engagement so the service automatically enforces a limit of 15 requests per IP address every 10 minutes - AWS Shield Advanced provides additional protection against distributed denial-of-service events, but it is not the service used to implement a precise per-IP application-layer request limit like 15 requests per 10 minutes. The requirement is specifically about enforcing a client IP request threshold. AWS WAF rate-based rules are the native mechanism for that use case, whereas Shield Advanced focuses on broader DDoS protection.

Store source IP addresses and request timestamps in the Amazon DynamoDB table, apply a 10-minute TTL to each entry, and limit the table throughput to 15 read operations - Using DynamoDB to store request timestamps and source IP addresses introduces unnecessary complexity and does not correctly enforce a per-IP request limit by itself. Reducing DynamoDB read capacity to 15 would affect the application globally rather than restricting each client IP address independently. This option also pushes rate-limiting logic into the data layer, which is not the right enforcement point for a public web application. It increases operational overhead and risks disrupting legitimate application behavior.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/how-aws-waf-works.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-rate-based-high-level-settings.html

temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company manages multiple AWS accounts by using AWS Organizations, including separate development and production accounts. An external auditor has asked for evidence that the workloads running in the production account can withstand disruptive events. The company needs a solution that not only provides evidence for the auditor but also improves the resilience of each production workload through identified remediation actions.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Review each production workload in the AWS Well-Architected Tool, evaluate the workloads against the Operational Excellence, Security, and Reliability pillars, implement the recommended mitigations, and provide the resulting documentation to the auditor

Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment in the production account by using the AWS Operational Best Practices framework, wait for the assessment to finish, and then give the auditor access to the generated reports

Use AWS Fault Injection Service to generate resilience experiment reports for the development account only, and use those reports as direct evidence that the production account workloads are resilient because the workloads share the same application code

Create a new AWS Audit Manager assessment by using the AWS Well-Architected framework, rely on the automated evidence collection to prove resilience for the production workloads, and provide the assessment report to the auditor without changing the workload architecture
respostas_corretas: Review each production workload in the AWS Well-Architected Tool, evaluate the workloads against the Operational Excellence, Security, and Reliability pillars, implement the recommended mitigations, and provide the resulting documentation to the auditor
explicacao:
Correct option:

Review each production workload in the AWS Well-Architected Tool, evaluate the workloads against the Operational Excellence, Security, and Reliability pillars, implement the recommended mitigations, and provide the resulting documentation to the auditor

The AWS Well-Architected Tool is the best fit because it is specifically designed to review workloads against AWS architectural best practices and identify risks and improvement opportunities. AWS states that the review process is intended to identify critical issues and areas that can be improved, and the Reliability pillar focuses on a workload’s ability to recover from disruptions and continue operating correctly. This option also directly satisfies the requirement to improve the resilience of each production workload. The engineer reviews the production workloads, identifies risks across the Operational Excellence, Security, and Reliability pillars, and then implements the necessary mitigations. That produces both actual resilience improvements and documented evidence for the auditor.

Among the available choices, this is the only option that explicitly combines a resilience-focused architectural review with implementation of corrective actions on the production workloads themselves. That makes it the strongest answer for both improving resilience and producing audit-ready documentation.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/userguide/intro.html

Incorrect options:

Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment in the production account by using the AWS Operational Best Practices framework, wait for the assessment to finish, and then give the auditor access to the generated reports - AWS Audit Manager helps automate evidence collection for compliance and control assessments, but it does not itself improve workload resilience. AWS also notes that the AWS Operational Best Practices framework is intended to help prepare for audits and does not guarantee that systems are resilient or that an audit will be passed. This option can help gather audit evidence, but it does not meet the requirement to improve the resilience of each production workload. It is therefore incomplete for the stated objective.

Create a new AWS Audit Manager assessment by using the AWS Well-Architected framework, rely on the automated evidence collection to prove resilience for the production workloads, and provide the assessment report to the auditor without changing the workload architecture - AWS Audit Manager has a prebuilt AWS Well-Architected framework, but AWS describes it as a way to collect evidence that is relevant for an audit rather than a mechanism to review and improve workload design directly. This option also explicitly avoids changing the workload architecture. Since the requirement says the solution must improve the resilience of each production workload, relying only on assessment evidence without implementing mitigations is insufficient.

Use AWS Fault Injection Service to generate resilience experiment reports for the development account only, and use those reports as direct evidence that the production account workloads are resilient because the workloads share the same application code - AWS Fault Injection Service reports can help demonstrate that experiments were run, but using development-only experiment reports as proof of production resilience is too indirect. Shared application code does not guarantee that the production account has the same architecture, dependencies, scaling behavior, or operational characteristics. This option therefore fails on both evidence quality and scope. The auditor asked for evidence about the production account workloads, and this approach does not provide that direct assurance.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/userguide/intro.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/wellarchitected/latest/framework/wellarchitected-framework.pdf

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/audit-manager/latest/userguide/what-is.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/audit-manager/latest/userguide/OBP.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/fis/latest/userguide/what-is.html

Domínio Domain 6: Security Foundations and Governance
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta: A retail organization manages an online catalog system across multiple Amazon Aurora database clusters. During a recent compliance audit, auditors identified several Aurora clusters without proper encryption in transit. The security team needs to enforce encrypted connections to all Aurora database endpoints. What is the recommended approach to enforce encryption requirements for all connections?
opcoes: Enable Amazon Inspector on the Aurora cluster to scan for insecure connections and automatically revoke non-TLS sessions through EventBridge integration
Deploy AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) to issue certificates for Aurora clusters and attach them to the database endpoints for SSL/TLS negotiation
Use AWS Secrets Manager to store encrypted database passwords, then create an IAM policy that allows applications to connect only via HTTPS endpoints
Modify the Aurora cluster parameter group and set the require_secure_transport database parameter to the value ON
respostas_corretas: Modify the Aurora cluster parameter group and set the require_secure_transport database parameter to the value ON
explicacao:
Correct option:

Modify the Aurora cluster parameter group and set the require_secure_transport database parameter to the value ON

The require_secure_transport parameter is an Aurora cluster-level setting that rejects any connection attempt that does not use an encrypted protocol. When set to ON, the cluster validates that every incoming connection is wrapped in TLS and terminates any connection presented without encryption. This check happens at the database engine level, before any SQL queries are processed, ensuring that no plaintext traffic can reach the cluster regardless of the client configuration.

This parameter controls the security behavior of the Aurora cluster itself, not the client side, which means the enforcement is centralized and cannot be bypassed by misconfiguring a single application. The parameter takes effect immediately after the cluster parameter group is updated, and the change applies to all new connections while existing connections are gracefully allowed to complete.

This approach directly addresses the compliance requirement with minimal operational overhead because it requires only a parameter change and does not mandate any changes to application code, proxy infrastructure, or external services. The enforcement is transparent to well-behaved clients that already use TLS and blocks only clients that attempt unencrypted connections.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/AuroraMySQL.Security.html

Incorrect options:

Deploy AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) to issue certificates for Aurora clusters and attach them to the database endpoints for SSL/TLS negotiation - AWS Certificate Manager issues certificates and ACM can integrate with some AWS services, but Aurora does not directly attach ACM certificates to database endpoints. Aurora manages its own certificate infrastructure separately. ACM is designed for HTTPS load balancers, CloudFront distributions, and API Gateway stages, not for Aurora database encryption. Attempting to use ACM for Aurora encryption misrepresents the certificate service model and introduces unnecessary infrastructure without solving the actual requirement to force encrypted connections.

Use AWS Secrets Manager to store encrypted database passwords, then create an IAM policy that allows applications to connect only via HTTPS endpoints - AWS Secrets Manager stores credentials securely, and HTTPS endpoints require encryption for API calls to Secrets Manager, but storing passwords in Secrets Manager does not enforce encryption for the database connections themselves. An application that retrieves a password from Secrets Manager can still connect to the Aurora cluster without TLS if the cluster allows it. Secrets Manager provides credential protection but does not control the cluster's requirement for encrypted connections.

Enable Amazon Inspector on the Aurora cluster to scan for insecure connections and automatically revoke non-TLS sessions through EventBridge integration - Amazon Inspector scans for vulnerabilities and compliance issues but does not enforce TLS connections. Inspector can detect that non-TLS connections are occurring, but detection is not the same as enforcement. An alert from Inspector indicates a problem after the fact, whereas the require_secure_transport parameter prevents non-TLS connections from being accepted in the first place. This option provides visibility but not the preventive control needed to fully comply with the encryption in transit requirement.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/AuroraMySQL.Security.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/UsingWithRDS.SSL.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A large enterprise runs workloads across dozens of AWS accounts managed through AWS Organizations. Company governance policy mandates that all AWS resources carry an Environment tag with one of four approved values: Development, Testing, Staging, or Production. The company must enforce this tagging requirement at resource creation time and prevent any user from updating the Environment tag to a non-approved value after creation.

Which solution will meet these requirements?
opcoes: Deploy an AWS Service Catalog portfolio and populate it with pre-approved AWS CloudFormation templates. Require all teams to deploy resources only through Service Catalog and embed the Environment tag enforcement in the CloudFormation stack parameter constraints for each product

Create an AWS Config Custom Policy rule by using AWS CloudFormation Guard. Define the rule to check that all newly created resources include an Environment tag key with one of the four approved values. Deploy the Config rule to every member account using a CloudFormation StackSet. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the value of the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key does not match any of the four approved values in the policy

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values. Configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function when a noncompliant tag value is detected and program the Lambda function to overwrite the tag with the nearest approved value

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values and configure the policy to enforce noncompliant operations. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key is null, ensuring every new resource carries the required tag at creation time
respostas_corretas: Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values and configure the policy to enforce noncompliant operations. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key is null, ensuring every new resource carries the required tag at creation time
explicacao:
Correct option:

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values and configure the policy to enforce noncompliant operations. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key is null, ensuring every new resource carries the required tag at creation time

AWS Organizations tag policies let you standardize tags across the resources in your organization's accounts. A tag policy defines the tag keys that resources must carry, specifies the allowed values for those keys, and optionally enforces compliance by preventing noncompliant operations. When you configure a tag policy to enforce noncompliant operations, any attempt to create or update a resource with a tag value not listed in the policy fails automatically. This ensures the Environment tag can only be set to one of the four approved values.

Tag policies alone do not prevent the creation of resources that lack the Environment tag entirely. A resource could be created without any tags and remain non-compliant. The SCP component closes this gap by denying resource creation requests when the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key is null. This condition key evaluates to null when the requester does not include the Environment tag in the create API call. Combining the tag policy with the SCP creates a complete preventive control: every new resource must carry the Environment tag (enforced by the SCP) and that tag must have an approved value (enforced by the tag policy).

This solution is entirely preventive and operates at the policy evaluation layer without requiring event-driven Lambda functions, periodic detection scans, or manual remediation workflows. Both controls apply across all member accounts through the Organizations management hierarchy, making the solution inherently scalable to any number of accounts. The combination of tag policies and SCPs is the AWS-recommended pattern for enforcing mandatory tagging standards at organization scale.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html

Incorrect options:

Create an AWS Config Custom Policy rule by using AWS CloudFormation Guard.
