Define the rule to check that all newly created resources include an Environment tag key with one of the four approved values. Deploy the Config rule to every member account using a CloudFormation StackSet. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the value of the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key does not match any of the four approved values in the policy - AWS Config rules are detective controls. They evaluate resources after creation and report non-compliance, but they cannot prevent the creation of a non-compliant resource in real time. A Config rule detects that a resource was created with an incorrect tag value and flags it as non-compliant, but the resource already exists at that point. Even with the SCP component, this option has a gap: the SCP only denies creation when the tag value is non-compliant but does not deny creation when the tag is entirely absent. A user could create a resource without any Environment tag and bypass the SCP entirely. Deploying Config rules to dozens of accounts via StackSets also adds operational overhead that a centralized tag policy avoids.

Deploy an AWS Service Catalog portfolio and populate it with pre-approved AWS CloudFormation templates. Require all teams to deploy resources only through Service Catalog and embed the Environment tag enforcement in the CloudFormation stack parameter constraints for each product - AWS Service Catalog with pre-approved templates can enforce tagging standards for resources deployed through the catalog. However, this approach only controls resources launched through Service Catalog. Engineers with direct AWS API access, AWS CLI access, or the ability to deploy CloudFormation stacks outside Service Catalog can create resources without any tag enforcement. Unless all direct API access is blocked by an SCP, this solution has significant gaps.

Maintaining a catalog of CloudFormation templates for every resource type the organization uses is also operationally intensive and requires continuous updates as new services and resource types are adopted. A centralized tag policy is far less maintenance-intensive.

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values. Configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function when a noncompliant tag value is detected and program the Lambda function to overwrite the tag with the nearest approved value - Using tag policies to enforce approved values is correct, but replacing the SCP preventive control with a reactive EventBridge-Lambda remediation mechanism introduces a window of non-compliance between resource creation and remediation. Overwriting a tag with the nearest approved value after creation is ambiguous and error-prone because "nearest" is not a well-defined concept for arbitrary tag values. This option also does not prevent the creation of resources without any Environment tag.

The requirements state that the company must prevent non-approved values, not detect and correct them after the fact. A preventive policy control is required.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_condition-keys.html#condition-keys-requesttag

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/operational-best-practices-for-tagging.html

Domínio Domain 6: Security Foundations and Governance
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A security engineer uses Amazon Macie to analyze a large Amazon S3 data estate for sensitive information. The environment contains many S3 buckets and a large number of objects. The engineer needs a low-overhead way to identify which buckets are likely to contain sensitive data and then perform deeper analysis only on those buckets.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend to meet these requirements with the least administrative effort?
opcoes: Create recurring Amazon Macie sensitive data discovery jobs for all buckets, export the findings into an Amazon DynamoDB table, and query the table to identify the buckets that require more analysis

Enable Amazon Macie automated sensitive data discovery so Macie continuously samples objects across the buckets, and then run targeted sensitive data discovery jobs for the buckets where Macie finds sensitive data

Configure AWS Security Hub to receive and aggregate all Macie findings from the buckets, then use Security Hub's insight queries to identify buckets with sensitive data and automatically trigger Lambda-based remediation workflows

Create an AWS Config custom rule that inventories all S3 buckets, tags the buckets that are likely to contain regulated data based on bucket names, and then run Amazon Macie jobs only for buckets whose names match the rule logic
respostas_corretas: Enable Amazon Macie automated sensitive data discovery so Macie continuously samples objects across the buckets, and then run targeted sensitive data discovery jobs for the buckets where Macie finds sensitive data
explicacao:
Correct option:

Enable Amazon Macie automated sensitive data discovery so Macie continuously samples objects across the buckets, and then run targeted sensitive data discovery jobs for the buckets where Macie finds sensitive data

Amazon Macie automated sensitive data discovery is designed for broad, ongoing visibility into where sensitive data might exist across an S3 data estate. AWS documents that automated sensitive data discovery continually evaluates bucket inventory and uses sampling techniques to identify and analyze representative objects in buckets. This gives the security engineer a low-overhead way to identify which buckets are likely to contain sensitive data without launching full scans everywhere.

After Macie identifies buckets or objects that appear to contain sensitive data, the engineer can create targeted sensitive data discovery jobs for those specific buckets. AWS documents discovery jobs as the mechanism for more directed and thorough analysis of S3 buckets and objects. This two-step approach matches the requirement to first identify relevant buckets and then perform additional scanning only where needed.

This is also the option with the least administrative overhead. It uses Macie’s native automated discovery capability for continuous sampling and then uses focused jobs only for buckets that warrant deeper inspection, rather than forcing the team to build event-driven orchestration or scan every bucket repeatedly.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd.html

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html

Incorrect options:

Create an AWS Config custom rule that inventories all S3 buckets, tags the buckets that are likely to contain regulated data based on bucket names, and then run Amazon Macie jobs only for buckets whose names match the rule logic - Using bucket names as a heuristic for regulated or sensitive content is unreliable and can easily miss buckets that contain sensitive data but do not follow a naming convention. It also creates a custom governance workflow where Macie already provides native data-aware classification capabilities.

This option therefore shifts the solution away from actual content analysis and toward guesswork based on naming patterns. Since the company already uses Macie, the least-overhead and more accurate approach is to let Macie identify buckets with sensitive data based on sampled object analysis.

Configure AWS Security Hub to receive and aggregate all Macie findings from the buckets, then use Security Hub's insight queries to identify buckets with sensitive data and automatically trigger Lambda-based remediation workflows - AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from Macie and other services, but it does not reduce the scanning workload. If Macie is running full scans on all buckets before Security Hub ingests the findings, the cost and processing overhead remain the same as running Macie standalone. Security Hub is a finding aggregation and compliance tool, not a sampling or prioritization mechanism.

Create recurring Amazon Macie sensitive data discovery jobs for all buckets, export the findings into an Amazon DynamoDB table, and query the table to identify the buckets that require more analysis - Running recurring discovery jobs for all buckets defeats the requirement to first identify which buckets contain sensitive data and then do additional scanning only for those buckets. It increases cost and administrative effort because the engineer must maintain comprehensive jobs for the entire S3 estate. Although DynamoDB could store derived results, Macie already provides findings and discovery results natively. Adding a separate table for basic identification adds overhead without solving the problem more efficiently than automated sensitive data discovery plus targeted jobs.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html

Domínio Domain 1: Detection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company uses a customer managed AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) key to protect sensitive messaging data for a communications workflow. A security engineer at the company reviews the key following policy statement that is attached to the customer managed key:

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::777788889999:role/MailProcessingRole" }, "Action": [ "kms:Encrypt", "kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey*", "kms:CreateGrant", "kms:ListGrants" ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "kms:ViaService": [ "workmail.us-east-1.amazonaws.com", "ses.us-east-1.amazonaws.com" ] } } } ] } What is the effect of this key policy statement?
opcoes: The customer managed key can be used only when the principal is MailProcessingRole and when the AWS KMS request is made through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in the us-east-1 Region
Amazon WorkMail and Amazon SES receive delegated AWS KMS permissions from the key policy and can use the customer managed key independently of the MailProcessingRole principal in account 777788889999

The kms:CreateGrant and kms:ListGrants actions in this policy extend grant management permissions to all IAM principals in the 777788889999 account when requests route through WorkMail or SES endpoints in us-east-1
The key policy allows Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES to perform cryptographic operations on behalf of any IAM role in the account because the policy uses a wildcard resource value
respostas_corretas: The customer managed key can be used only when the principal is MailProcessingRole and when the AWS KMS request is made through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in the us-east-1 Region
explicacao:
Correct option:

The customer managed key can be used only when the principal is MailProcessingRole and when the AWS KMS request is made through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in the us-east-1 Region

The key policy grants permissions to a specific principal, which is the IAM role arn:aws:iam::777788889999:role/MailProcessingRole. Those allowed permissions apply only when the request satisfies the kms:ViaService condition. That condition restricts the use of the key to requests that flow through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in the us-east-1 Region.

This means the role does not receive unrestricted direct access to call AWS KMS for those operations in arbitrary contexts. Instead, AWS KMS authorizes the request only when the named role is the principal and when the service path matches one of the allowed integrated services. The condition narrows the usage pattern to service-mediated requests through WorkMail or SES.

The policy therefore limits the customer managed key to a very specific access model. MailProcessingRole can use the key for the listed cryptographic and grant-related operations only through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in us-east-1. That is the precise effect of combining a named principal with a kms:ViaService condition in a key policy statement.

Incorrect options:

Amazon WorkMail and Amazon SES receive delegated AWS KMS permissions from the key policy and can use the customer managed key independently of the MailProcessingRole principal in account 777788889999 - The policy does not make Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES the principals that receive permission. The principal in the statement is the IAM role MailProcessingRole. The services appear only inside the kms:ViaService condition, which restricts how the principal can use the key. Because the services are not the principals in the policy, they do not gain independent authority to use the key outside the context of requests made on behalf of the allowed role. The statement controls usage by MailProcessingRole, not standalone service access delegated directly to WorkMail or SES.

The kms:CreateGrant and kms:ListGrants actions in this policy extend grant management permissions to all IAM principals in the 777788889999 account when requests route through WorkMail or SES endpoints in us-east-1 - The kms:CreateGrant and kms:ListGrants actions listed in this policy are subject to the identical principal and condition restrictions as every other action in the same policy statement. Only MailProcessingRole can invoke these actions, and only when the request routes through the WorkMail or SES endpoints in us-east-1. The policy does not extend any permissions to other IAM principals in the account. A policy statement applies its Principal, Condition, and Resource fields uniformly to all actions listed within that statement. There is no mechanism in this single-statement policy that provides broader scope to grant management actions than to the other listed cryptographic actions.

The key policy allows Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES to perform cryptographic operations on behalf of any IAM role in the account because the policy uses a wildcard resource value - The wildcard resource value in a KMS key policy statement refers to the key resource that the policy is attached to, not to all customer managed keys in the account. In AWS KMS key policies, "Resource": "*" is commonly used and does not expand the permission scope to every key in the account. The statement also does not authorize any IAM role in the account. It names one specific principal, MailProcessingRole. Because both the principal scope and the service path are restricted, this option overstates the effect of the policy and incorrectly interprets the wildcard resource element.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/conditions-kms.html#conditions-kms-via-service

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/policy-conditions.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workmail/latest/adminguide/what_is.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company operates a legacy internet-facing application that runs on Amazon EC2 instances. The application distributes traffic by using Amazon Route 53 weighted routing across two instances. The backend connects to an Amazon RDS cluster. The application no longer receives vendor updates. A recent security assessment identified that the application is vulnerable to SQL injection attacks. The security team must implement a mitigation within 24 hours. The solution must require minimal changes to the application and must not disrupt normal operations during deployment.

What should the security engineer do to meet these requirements?
opcoes: Configure Amazon RDS database parameter groups to enforce strict query validation and enable database-level protections against SQL injection attacks without modifying the application layer

Modify the security groups associated with the EC2 instances to block inbound database-related traffic from the internet, create an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules, attempt to associate the web ACL directly with the EC2 instances, and then restore the original security group configuration after testing

Deploy an Application Load Balancer in front of the existing EC2 instances, register the instances as targets, associate an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules to the load balancer, validate the mitigation, update Route 53 records to route traffic to the load balancer, and restrict direct internet access to the EC2 instances

Deploy AWS Network Firewall to inspect inbound HTTP traffic for SQL injection patterns, integrate the firewall with the VPC routing configuration, and apply rule groups to filter malicious payloads before they reach the EC2 instances
respostas_corretas: Deploy an Application Load Balancer in front of the existing EC2 instances, register the instances as targets, associate an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules to the load balancer, validate the mitigation, update Route 53 records to route traffic to the load balancer, and restrict direct internet access to the EC2 instances
explicacao:
Correct option:

Deploy an Application Load Balancer in front of the existing EC2 instances, register the instances as targets, associate an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules to the load balancer, validate the mitigation, update Route 53 records to route traffic to the load balancer, and restrict direct internet access to the EC2 instances

Deploying an Application Load Balancer with AWS WAF provides an immediate and effective mitigation against SQL injection attacks without requiring changes to the application code. AWS WAF offers managed rule groups and custom rules that can detect and block common SQL injection patterns at the edge of the application. This approach meets the requirement for minimal effort and rapid deployment. The ALB can be introduced in front of the existing EC2 instances with minimal disruption, and traffic can be gradually shifted by updating Route 53 records after validation. Restricting direct access to the EC2 instances ensures that all incoming traffic is inspected by AWS WAF.

This solution also aligns with AWS best practices for protecting legacy applications. It introduces a protective layer that can be deployed quickly while maintaining normal operations and minimizing risk during implementation.

Incorrect options:

Configure Amazon RDS database parameter groups to enforce strict query validation and enable database-level protections against SQL injection attacks without modifying the application layer - Amazon RDS parameter groups control database engine configuration but do not provide application-layer protection against SQL injection attacks. SQL injection vulnerabilities must be mitigated either in the application logic or by filtering malicious input before it reaches the application. Database-level configurations alone cannot prevent such attacks.

Deploy AWS Network Firewall to inspect inbound HTTP traffic for SQL injection patterns, integrate the firewall with the VPC routing configuration, and apply rule groups to filter malicious payloads before they reach the EC2 instances - AWS Network Firewall operates at the network layer and is not optimized for inspecting application-layer payloads such as HTTP requests for SQL injection patterns. Implementing Network Firewall also requires changes to VPC routing and rule configuration, which increases operational complexity. This does not meet the requirement for least effort and rapid deployment.

Modify the security groups associated with the EC2 instances to block inbound database-related traffic from the internet, create an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules, attempt to associate the web ACL directly with the EC2 instances, and then restore the original security group configuration after testing - AWS WAF cannot be directly associated with EC2 instances. It integrates with services such as Application Load Balancer, Amazon CloudFront, and API Gateway. Modifying security groups to block database ports does not prevent SQL injection attacks because these attacks occur at the application layer over HTTP or HTTPS. This option misunderstands both the threat model and the capabilities of AWS WAF.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-sqli-match.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-chapter.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/Route53/latest/DeveloperGuide/routing-policy.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/network-firewall/latest/developerguide/what-is-aws-network-firewall.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company recently contained and eradicated a security incident in its production environment. As part of recovery, a security engineer must restore an Amazon RDS database cluster to the last known good state. The DB cluster is configured with automated backups and a retention period of 28 days. The investigation confirms that the initial malicious activity began 3 days ago at exactly 2:25 PM. The security engineer must recover the cluster to the most recent safe point before the attack started.

Which solution should the security engineer implement?
opcoes: Restore the latest automated backup for the DB cluster, and then use database transaction logs inside the restored cluster to manually reverse changes that occurred after 3 days ago at 2:25 PM
Identify the source DB cluster, review the available DB cluster snapshots, and restore the snapshot whose creation time is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM
Review snapshots for all Amazon RDS databases in the account, choose the snapshot whose timestamp is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM, and restore that snapshot
Identify the source DB cluster and perform a point-in-time restore for the cluster by choosing a restore time of 3 days ago at 2:24 PM
respostas_corretas: Identify the source DB cluster and perform a point-in-time restore for the cluster by choosing a restore time of 3 days ago at 2:24 PM
explicacao:
Correct option:

Identify the source DB cluster and perform a point-in-time restore for the cluster by choosing a restore time of 3 days ago at 2:24 PM

Amazon RDS DB clusters that have automated backups enabled can be restored to an arbitrary point in time within the backup retention window. Because the attack started 3 days ago at 2:25 PM and the retention period is 28 days, the cluster can be restored to a clean point immediately before the incident, such as 3 days ago at 2:24 PM. AWS documents that point-in-time restore is supported up to the configured retention period.

This is the most precise recovery method because it restores to the last known good time rather than forcing the company to rely on the timing of a snapshot. Snapshot-based recovery can leave a larger data loss window if the nearest snapshot was taken well before the attack. Point-in-time restore directly matches the requirement to recover the cluster to the last known good version.

Using the source DB cluster identifier for a restore to a specific timestamp is the native recovery path for an RDS or Aurora-style DB cluster in this situation. It minimizes unnecessary data loss and is exactly what automated backups are intended to support.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/USER_PIT.html

Incorrect options:

Identify the source DB cluster, review the available DB cluster snapshots, and restore the snapshot whose creation time is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM - Restoring from the snapshot whose timestamp is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM is less accurate than a point-in-time restore. Snapshots are discrete recovery points, while point-in-time restore can recover to a specific timestamp within the automated backup window. If the nearest snapshot is earlier than the desired time, the company loses more data than necessary. This option also ignores the fact that the cluster already has automated backups enabled specifically to support more granular recovery. Since the requirement is to restore to the last known good version just before the attack, PITR is the better and more precise choice.

Review snapshots for all Amazon RDS databases in the account, choose the snapshot whose timestamp is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM, and restore that snapshot - Listing snapshots for all RDS databases in the company is unnecessarily broad and increases the chance of restoring the wrong database. The engineer already knows which DB cluster must be recovered, so recovery should target that specific cluster rather than searching across unrelated databases. It also still relies on snapshots instead of point-in-time restore. Even if the correct snapshot were selected, the result would usually be less precise than restoring the exact cluster to 2:24 PM, one minute before the attack began.

Restore the latest automated backup for the DB cluster, and then use database transaction logs inside the restored cluster to manually reverse changes that occurred after 3 days ago at 2:25 PM - This option is incorrect because Amazon RDS already provides point-in-time restore for a DB cluster that has automated backups enabled. The requirement is to recover the database to the last known good time, which is 3 days ago at 2:24 PM, not to restore some later recovery point and then manually reverse changes. A manual rollback approach is more error-prone and unnecessary when RDS can restore the cluster directly to the exact timestamp just before the attack began.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/USER_PIT.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/rds/restore-db-cluster-to-point-in-time.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/APIReference/API_RestoreDBClusterToPointInTime.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/Aurora.Managing.Backups.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/aurora-restore-snapshot.html

Domínio Domain 2: Incident Response
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company must store all objects that contain sensitive data in an Amazon S3 bucket. The company will use server-side encryption for the bucket. The operations team manages access to the company’s S3 buckets, and the security team manages access to encryption keys. The company wants a separation-of-duties design so that a configuration mistake by only one of these teams cannot result in unauthorized access to plaintext data.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption with a customer managed AWS KMS key and also give the operations team permission to administer the KMS key so the bucket and key remain aligned during troubleshooting

Require the operations team to create a bucket policy that enforces uploads by using server-side encryption with customer managed AWS KMS keys, and require the security team to manage a key policy that controls access to the KMS keys

Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption for the bucket by using server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys, and require the security team to manage IAM permissions for the encryption keys

Require the security team to create a customer managed AWS KMS key and configure a key policy that allows any principal in the account to decrypt with the key, and require the operations team to leverage S3 default encryption without a bucket policy that enforces SSE-KMS on object uploads
respostas_corretas: Require the operations team to create a bucket policy that enforces uploads by using server-side encryption with customer managed AWS KMS keys, and require the security team to manage a key policy that controls access to the KMS keys
explicacao:
Correct option:

Require the operations team to create a bucket policy that enforces uploads by using server-side encryption with customer managed AWS KMS keys, and require the security team to manage a key policy that controls access to the KMS keys

This design creates the intended separation of duties by splitting control between the S3 bucket policy and the AWS KMS key policy. The operations team controls the bucket policy and can require that object uploads use server-side encryption with customer managed AWS KMS keys. The security team separately controls the key policy that governs who can use the KMS key.

This means a mistake by only one team is not enough to expose plaintext data. If the operations team misconfigures S3 access but the security team does not permit use of the KMS key, unauthorized users still cannot decrypt the objects. If the security team allows broad key use but the bucket policy correctly restricts uploads and access patterns, the S3 side still enforces its control boundary. Both layers must align for successful plaintext access.

This is the strongest answer because it uses SSE-KMS with a customer managed key, which gives the security team independent control over key usage. It directly satisfies the requirement that a configuration error by only one team must not compromise the data. AWS managed S3 encryption keys do not provide that same independent security-team-controlled policy layer.

Incorrect options:

Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption for the bucket by using server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys, and require the security team to manage IAM permissions for the encryption keys - SSE-S3 uses Amazon S3 managed keys, which means there is no separate customer-controlled key policy for the security team to manage. The option says the security team will create an IAM policy that controls access to the encryption keys, but there are no customer-managed KMS keys in this design for them to control. Because the encryption layer is not independently governed by the security team, this option fails the separation-of-duties requirement. A mistake on the S3 side could be enough to expose access because the key-control plane is not separately owned in the way the question requires.

Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption with a customer managed AWS KMS key and also give the operations team permission to administer the KMS key so the bucket and key remain aligned during troubleshooting - This option uses a customer managed KMS key, which is directionally better than SSE-S3. However, it gives the operations team permission to administer the KMS key as well as the bucket configuration, which breaks the separation-of-duties requirement. If the same team can control both the S3 enforcement layer and the encryption key permissions, then a mistake by that one team could result in unauthorized plaintext access. The question specifically wants to prevent that outcome, so combining those permissions in the operations team is not acceptable.

Require the security team to create a customer managed AWS KMS key and configure a key policy that allows any principal in the account to decrypt with the key, and require the operations team to leverage S3 default encryption without a bucket policy that enforces SSE-KMS on object uploads - Creating a customer managed KMS key is part of a good design, but allowing any principal in the account to decrypt with the key defeats the purpose of having the security team control access tightly. Broad decrypt permissions make the key policy far too permissive. This option also relies only on default encryption and does not include a bucket policy that enforces SSE-KMS on object uploads. Default encryption helps, but it is not the same as an explicit enforcement policy combined with a tightly controlled KMS key policy. The design is therefore weaker than the required split-control model.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/concepts.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingServerSideEncryption.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/bucket-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/default-bucket-encryption.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/control-access.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A logistics company deploys a containerized application on Amazon ECS. The application retrieves configuration files and reference data from a specific Amazon S3 bucket. A security engineer attached an IAM policy to the ECS task execution role that should grant the application read access to the bucket. However, whenever the application attempts to fetch objects from S3, it receives AccessDenied errors.

A security engineer must resolve this issue without reducing the security of the S3 bucket or the application.

Which solution will meet these requirements?
opcoes: Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to inspect and validate the IAM policy attached to the task execution role. Verify that the policy grants the required S3 permissions. Confirm that the containerized application successfully assumes the task execution role during the container startup sequence

Create an Amazon S3 VPC endpoint in the VPC where the ECS tasks run. Configure the endpoint policy to explicitly allow s3:GetObject, s3:GetObjectVersion, and s3:ListBucket actions for the task execution role on the target bucket ARN. Enable VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail data event logging to verify that all S3 requests from the ECS tasks route successfully through the VPC endpoint
Disable the S3 Block Public Access feature on the target S3 bucket. Review AWS CloudTrail logs to determine whether the task execution role is being assumed correctly by the application

Replace the IAM policy attached to the task execution role with an inline policy that grants s3:* on all resources. Then use CloudTrail logs to identify the actions the application actually uses and progressively restrict the inline policy over time
respostas_corretas: Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to inspect and validate the IAM policy attached to the task execution role. Verify that the policy grants the required S3 permissions. Confirm that the containerized application successfully assumes the task execution role during the container startup sequence
explicacao:
Correct option:

Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to inspect and validate the IAM policy attached to the task execution role. Verify that the policy grants the required S3 permissions. Confirm that the containerized application successfully assumes the task execution role during the container startup sequence

AWS IAM Access Analyzer provides policy validation that checks IAM policies against AWS best practices and policy grammar rules. When an application receives AccessDenied errors, the most common root causes are that the IAM policy does not grant the required actions or resources, the application is not successfully assuming the intended role, or a resource-based policy on the target resource explicitly denies access. Access Analyzer can identify policy misconfigurations such as missing actions, incorrect resource ARNs, or overly restrictive condition keys.

Validating the IAM policy through Access Analyzer and confirming role assumption addresses the two most probable root causes without making any changes to the S3 bucket configuration. If the policy is incorrectly scoped, Access Analyzer highlights the exact issue with actionable findings. If the application is not assuming the task execution role at all, that becomes visible during investigation through CloudTrail or ECS task metadata analysis. This approach is methodical and preserves the existing security posture of both the bucket and the application.

Correct diagnostic workflows start with understanding the problem before making changes. Rather than immediately adding bucket-level permissions or modifying the bucket's access configuration, using Access Analyzer to validate the policy and verifying role assumption respects the least-privilege principle. After confirming the root cause, the engineer applies a targeted fix to the IAM policy without weakening the bucket's security configuration, fully meeting the stated constraints.

Incorrect options:

Replace the IAM policy attached to the task execution role with an inline policy that grants s3:* on all resources. Then use CloudTrail logs to identify the actions the application actually uses and progressively restrict the inline policy over time - Granting s3:* on all resources gives the ECS task execution role full S3 access across every bucket in the account, not just the target bucket. This is a serious security violation that directly contradicts the requirement not to decrease security. Even with the intent to tighten permissions later, granting overly broad permissions as a temporary troubleshooting measure introduces unacceptable risk in a production environment.

Replacing a managed policy with an inline policy also changes the permission model and makes the configuration harder to audit and manage at scale. The broad-access interim period, even if brief, is operationally and compliance-wise unacceptable.

Create an Amazon S3 VPC endpoint in the VPC where the ECS tasks run. Configure the endpoint policy to explicitly allow s3:GetObject, s3:GetObjectVersion, and s3:ListBucket actions for the task execution role on the target bucket ARN. Enable VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail data event logging to verify that all S3 requests from the ECS tasks route successfully through the VPC endpoint - An S3 VPC endpoint routes traffic from the VPC to S3 through the AWS private network rather than through the internet, and the endpoint policy controls which requests through the endpoint are permitted. However, creating a VPC endpoint and configuring its policy does not resolve an AccessDenied error caused by a missing or misconfigured IAM policy on the task execution role. The IAM policy evaluation and the endpoint policy are additive controls; both must allow the action for the request to succeed.

If the task execution role's IAM policy does not grant the required S3 actions, the AccessDenied error persists regardless of the VPC endpoint configuration. This option adds observability through VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail, but it also potentially expands the scope of S3 access granted through the endpoint policy, reducing security rather than preserving it.

Disable the S3 Block Public Access feature on the target S3 bucket. Review AWS CloudTrail logs to determine whether the task execution role is being assumed correctly by the application - Disabling S3 Block Public Access explicitly reduces the security posture of the S3 bucket, which the question prohibits. S3 Block Public Access prevents public ACLs and public bucket policies from making the bucket accessible to unauthenticated users. Disabling it for a bucket storing sensitive logistics data introduces unnecessary risk. The application uses IAM role-based authentication, so Block Public Access does not affect authenticated API calls and is not related to the AccessDenied error.

Reviewing CloudTrail logs is a valid diagnostic step, but it must not be combined with a reduction in bucket security. CloudTrail analysis should be performed without altering any bucket configuration settings.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/what-is-access-analyzer.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/troubleshoot_access-denied.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_execution_IAM_role.html

Domínio Domain 4: Identity and Access Management
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A development team publishes an open-source toolset for a SaaS application in a public repository. During a security review, the company discovers that the repository contains an IAM access key and secret key that provide access to internal AWS resources. A security engineer must determine any unauthorized usage of the exposed credentials and must immediately prevent any further misuse.

Which combination of actions should the security engineer take to meet these requirements? (Select two)

Create an AWS Config rule to detect exposed access keys in source code repositories and automatically revoke the keys when detected
opcoes: Deactivate the compromised IAM access key in the IAM user account to immediately prevent any further use of the credentials
Use AWS Identity and Access Management Access Analyzer to identify which AWS resources were accessed and determine whether the exposed credentials were used
Generate an IAM credential report and review the last-used information for the access key to determine recent usage activity
Enable AWS CloudTrail Insights and use anomaly detection to identify unusual API activity patterns associated with the exposed credentials going forward
respostas_corretas: Deactivate the compromised IAM access key in the IAM user account to immediately prevent any further use of the credentials
Use AWS Identity and Access Management Access Analyzer to identify which AWS resources were accessed and determine whether the exposed credentials were used
explicacao:
Correct options:

Use AWS Identity and Access Management Access Analyzer to identify which AWS resources were accessed and determine whether the exposed credentials were used

IAM Access Analyzer simplifies the forensic process through its Policy Generation and Unused Access features.

Identify Usage: You can use Access Analyzer to review the specific services and actions an IAM entity (the compromised user) has used over a specific period. By analyzing the CloudTrail logs, it can generate a report of all "Last Accessed" information for that user.

Forensic Scope: This allows a security engineer to quickly see if the credentials were used to touch sensitive services (like S3 or RDS) that the developer doesn't normally use, helping define the "blast radius" of the breach without manually writing complex SQL queries in Athena.

Deactivate the compromised IAM access key in the IAM user account to immediately prevent any further use of the credentials

Deactivating the exposed IAM access key is the most immediate and effective way to prevent further misuse. Once credentials are exposed in a public repository, they must be considered compromised, and continued use poses a significant security risk.

By disabling the key, the security engineer ensures that any malicious actor who has obtained the credentials can no longer use them to access AWS resources. This aligns with AWS security best practices for credential compromise response.

Incorrect options:

Create an AWS Config rule to detect exposed access keys in source code repositories and automatically revoke the keys when detected - AWS Config can evaluate resource configurations but does not natively detect exposed credentials in external code repositories or automatically revoke access keys. This option introduces a control that is not applicable to the described scenario and does not address immediate remediation or investigation.

Enable AWS CloudTrail Insights and use anomaly detection to identify unusual API activity patterns associated with the exposed credentials going forward - CloudTrail Insights can detect unusual API activity patterns, but it is designed for ongoing anomaly detection rather than immediate investigation of a known compromised key. It does not provide direct evidence of past usage of the specific access key, nor does it prevent further misuse.

Generate an IAM credential report and review the last-used information for the access key to determine recent usage activity - The IAM credential report only shows the time the key was last used; it does not provide the detail needed to see what resources were accessed or who (which IP/location) used them.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access-analyzer.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access-analyzer-concepts.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_getting-report.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-insights.html

Domínio Domain 2: Incident Response
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A financial institution operates a large fleet of Amazon Linux 2 EC2 instances that process confidential transaction data. The company's security policy enforces three strict compliance requirements:

No SSH or RDP management ports may be exposed on any EC2 instance, either internally or externally.

All remote session activity must be captured in a tamper-proof, centrally managed audit log.

All remote access must be authenticated and authorized through the company's approved centralized cloud identity management system.

The infrastructure team periodically connects to these EC2 instances to investigate performance issues.

Which solution provides remote access to the EC2 instances while satisfying all three compliance requirements in the most operationally efficient manner?
opcoes: Attach an IAM instance role to each EC2 instance that grants the AWS Systems Manager service the permissions it needs to manage the instance. Create an IAM policy that allows Systems Manager Session Manager actions. Assign this policy to the infrastructure team's permission set in the company's centralized identity management system so that all remote sessions route through Session Manager without any open network ports

Install a certified third-party privileged access management solution on each EC2 instance in the fleet. Configure the agent to enforce multi-factor authentication using the company's SAML-based identity provider before granting any interactive shell session. Forward all session keystroke recordings to a centralized audit vault maintained by the third-party vendor, configure automated alerts for sessions exceeding a 30-minute duration threshold, and produce weekly compliance summary reports for all privileged access events across the entire fleet
Enable the EC2 serial console at the AWS account level. Create an IAM policy that allows the infrastructure team's IAM role to use the EC2 serial console on all instances in the fleet

Use AWS Systems Manager Automation runbooks to temporarily open SSH access on the EC2 instances whenever the infrastructure team requires access. Configure the runbooks to close the ports automatically after each session ends
respostas_corretas: Attach an IAM instance role to each EC2 instance that grants the AWS Systems Manager service the permissions it needs to manage the instance. Create an IAM policy that allows Systems Manager Session Manager actions. Assign this policy to the infrastructure team's permission set in the company's centralized identity management system so that all remote sessions route through Session Manager without any open network ports
explicacao:
Correct option:

Attach an IAM instance role to each EC2 instance that grants the AWS Systems Manager service the permissions it needs to manage the instance. Create an IAM policy that allows Systems Manager Session Manager actions. Assign this policy to the infrastructure team's permission set in the company's centralized identity management system so that all remote sessions route through Session Manager without any open network ports

AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides browser-based and CLI-based shell access to EC2 instances without requiring open inbound ports, bastion hosts, or SSH key management. Session Manager communicates with the SSM Agent installed on each instance through an outbound HTTPS connection that the agent initiates to the Systems Manager service endpoint. Because all connectivity is outbound from the instance, no inbound ports need to be open on the instance or its security group, satisfying the first compliance requirement.

Session Manager automatically logs all session activity to Amazon S3 or Amazon CloudWatch Logs. These logs are written by the Systems Manager service, not by the session user, making them tamper-proof from the perspective of anyone conducting the session. This satisfies the second compliance requirement for a centrally managed, immutable audit trail. Session Manager also integrates natively with AWS IAM Identity Center, allowing the infrastructure team's permission sets to control who can initiate sessions, which satisfies the third compliance requirement for centralized identity-based authorization.

The solution addresses all three compliance requirements using fully managed AWS services with no third-party dependencies, no open network ports, and no additional software to install beyond the SSM Agent (which is pre-installed on Amazon Linux 2 AMIs). Any engineer who authenticates through the company's centralized identity system and holds the appropriate Session Manager permission receives controlled, audited access without any exposure of management ports.

Incorrect options:

Enable the EC2 serial console at the AWS account level. Create an IAM policy that allows the infrastructure team's IAM role to use the EC2 serial console on all instances in the fleet - The EC2 serial console provides out-of-band access through the instance's serial port, typically used for troubleshooting boot or network configuration failures. However, the EC2 serial console does not integrate with the company's centralized cloud identity management system for per-session authentication and authorization. Access through the serial console uses instance-level operating system credentials rather than centrally managed identities, failing the third compliance requirement.

The EC2 serial console also does not record session activity in a tamper-proof centrally managed audit log. Session Manager provides far more comprehensive session recording and centralized identity integration than the serial console.

Install a certified third-party privileged access management solution on each EC2 instance in the fleet. Configure the agent to enforce multi-factor authentication using the company's SAML-based identity provider before granting any interactive shell session. Forward all session keystroke recordings to a centralized audit vault maintained by the third-party vendor, configure automated alerts for sessions exceeding a 30-minute duration threshold, and produce weekly compliance summary reports for all privileged access events across the entire fleet - Third-party privileged access management solutions typically require inbound network connectivity on a proprietary port for client-to-agent communication. This likely exposes a management port on the instance, violating the first compliance requirement. Third-party agents also introduce additional software to deploy, patch, and maintain on every EC2 instance in a large fleet, increasing operational complexity and the attack surface.

Using a third-party solution is operationally more expensive than AWS Systems Manager Session Manager, which is available as a fully managed AWS service with native integration into the company's AWS identity management environment. AWS recommends native services over third-party tools when a native solution fully satisfies all stated requirements.

Use AWS Systems Manager Automation runbooks to temporarily open SSH access on the EC2 instances whenever the infrastructure team requires access. Configure the runbooks to close the ports automatically after each session ends - Dynamically opening and closing SSH ports using Automation runbooks is a just-in-time access pattern. However, this approach still violates the first compliance requirement because it exposes SSH port 22 on the security group during each access window. The compliance requirement states that no remote access management ports can be exposed at any time. Even a brief exposure window is non-compliant.

This pattern also introduces operational risk because if the runbook that closes the port fails or times out, the security group is left in a non-compliant state with an open SSH port. The approach relies on automation working correctly every time rather than having no open ports as a baseline configuration.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager-logging.html

Domínio Domain 3: Infrastructure Security
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
An organization uses AWS Identity Center for workforce identity management across multiple AWS accounts and has delegated AWS Security Hub administration to a security team member's account. A CloudTrail organization trail logs API calls to an S3 bucket via AWS Organizations, and a CloudTrail Lake event data store has been configured to retain 7 years of organization-wide API events. An IAM user's credentials have been compromised and the incident response team must immediately disable the user's access across all organization accounts and collect 7 days of API activity for forensic analysis.

Which solution will meet these requirements?
opcoes: Remove all IAM policies attached to the compromised user in the management account and query CloudTrail findings through Security Hub to collect 7 days of actions
Disable the user's access in AWS Identity Center by removing user assignments and query the CloudTrail Lake organization event data store to retrieve 7 days of API activity
Revoke all temporary credentials for the user using STS GetCallerIdentity and query Security Hub for historical activity across all accounts over a duration of 7 days
Disable the compromised IAM user account in the management account and query the CloudTrail S3 logs using Athena to retrieve 7 days of API activity
respostas_corretas: Disable the user's access in AWS Identity Center by removing user assignments and query the CloudTrail Lake organization event data store to retrieve 7 days of API activity
explicacao:
Correct option:

Disable the user's access in AWS Identity Center by removing user assignments and query the CloudTrail Lake organization event data store to retrieve 7 days of API activity

AWS Identity Center is the central identity provider for workforce identities across the organization. When a user is assigned to an AWS account through Identity Center, their access spans all linked accounts automatically. Removing user assignments in Identity Center immediately revokes access across all organization accounts with a single action, providing organization-wide access denial without requiring account-by-account IAM user modifications. This approach ensures the compromised user cannot access any resources in any organization account.

CloudTrail Lake event data stores are specifically designed for long-term retention and queryable analysis of organization-wide CloudTrail events. Unlike S3-based CloudTrail logs which require complex Athena queries or third-party tools, CloudTrail Lake provides native SQL-like query capabilities through its console and API. The organization event data store configured for 7-year retention is pre-populated with all member account activity and can be queried to retrieve exactly 7 days of activity for the specific compromised user without additional setup or data migration.

This combination provides both immediate and comprehensive remediation: Identity Center disables access at the source (identity provider) ensuring organization-wide protection, and CloudTrail Lake provides efficient forensic analysis of the 7-day activity window required for incident investigation.

Incorrect options:

Disable the compromised IAM user account in the management account and query the CloudTrail S3 logs using Athena to retrieve 7 days of API activity - Disabling an IAM user in the management account only affects access within that single account. In a multi-account organization using Identity Center, users do not have IAM user accounts in member accounts; their access is derived from Identity Center assignments. This approach fails to disable access in any member accounts where the user may have been granted permissions. Additionally, querying S3-based CloudTrail logs with Athena requires manual table creation and SQL expertise, and S3 logs may not be configured for all organization accounts, resulting in incomplete forensic data.

Remove all IAM policies attached to the compromised user in the management account and query CloudTrail findings through Security Hub to collect 7 days of actions - Removing IAM policies from a management account IAM user still does not address member account access in an Identity Center environment. IAM policy removal is not the appropriate remediation for an Identity Center user; permission sets control access for Identity Center users across accounts. Security Hub provides security findings and compliance status but is not designed for CloudTrail event query or forensic analysis. This approach uses tools designed for security monitoring rather than incident response and does not provide the 7-day activity data needed for forensic investigation.

Revoke all temporary credentials for the user using STS GetCallerIdentity and query Security Hub for historical activity across all accounts over a duration of 7 days - STS GetCallerIdentity is a diagnostic API call that returns information about the currently authenticated principal; it does not revoke credentials or disable access. Even if this were possible, it would only affect temporary credentials issued after the command executes, leaving previously issued credentials still valid. Security Hub aggregates findings from security services but is not a forensic analysis tool and cannot provide detailed 7-day activity logs for the compromised user in the format needed for incident investigation.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-lake.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/optimize-security-operations-with-aws-security-incident-response/

Domínio Domain 2: Incident Response
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company discovers that several EC2 instances have been compromised and used to send phishing emails, resulting in an unexpected spike in AWS charges related to data transfer and bandwidth costs. The security team must detect future cost anomalies as early as possible to identify similar compromises before significant financial damage occurs.

Which approach enables the earliest detection of abnormal costs?
opcoes: Enable Cost Anomaly Detection with machine learning algorithms and configure individual SNS alerts for each detected anomaly

Create an AWS Budgets cost budget for the account, configure threshold alerts to send notifications to an Amazon SNS topic, and leverage budget threshold breaches to identify malicious usage as quickly as possible
Create CloudWatch custom metrics for bandwidth usage and set static threshold-based alarms to notify on exceeding limits
Review AWS Cost Explorer reports daily to identify spending patterns that deviate from historical averages
respostas_corretas: Enable Cost Anomaly Detection with machine learning algorithms and configure individual SNS alerts for each detected anomaly
explicacao:
Correct option:

Enable Cost Anomaly Detection with machine learning algorithms and configure individual SNS alerts for each detected anomaly

AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses machine learning algorithms trained on your historical cost patterns to identify spending that deviates significantly from normal behavior. The system monitors costs in real time and detects anomalies with minimal false positives by understanding day-of-week patterns, seasonal trends, and growth trajectories specific to your account.

When an anomaly is detected, Cost Anomaly Detection sends immediate SNS alerts that provide context about the deviation, including the magnitude of the spike and services affected. This delivers notifications as soon as anomalies are detected, typically within hours of abnormal spending occurring, enabling rapid incident response before costs escalate further.

Machine learning-based detection is superior to static thresholds because it adapts to legitimate cost changes and organization growth, reducing alert fatigue while maintaining sensitivity to genuine security incidents like compromised instances running unauthorized services.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/manage-ad.html

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/ad-SNS.html

Incorrect options:

Create CloudWatch custom metrics for bandwidth usage and set static threshold-based alarms to notify on exceeding limits - Static threshold-based alarms on bandwidth usage require administrators to predict appropriate limits in advance and cannot adapt to changing network patterns or legitimate usage growth. Thresholds set too low generate excessive false positives, while thresholds set too high might miss actual anomalies. Static alarms lack the intelligence to distinguish between authorized changes and security incidents, and this approach would not effectively detect cost spikes from novel attack vectors using previously unused services.

Review AWS Cost Explorer reports daily to identify spending patterns that deviate from historical averages - Manual daily review of Cost Explorer reports means detection is delayed by a full day at minimum, as anomalies that occur overnight would not be noticed until the following business day. By the time a cost spike is manually identified, significant charges could have accumulated. This reactive approach prioritizes convenience over security and fails to meet the requirement for earliest possible detection of abnormal costs.

Create an AWS Budgets cost budget for the account, configure threshold alerts to send notifications to an Amazon SNS topic, and leverage budget threshold breaches to identify malicious usage as quickly as possible - AWS Budgets can send alerts when actual or forecasted cost crosses thresholds, and the billing data Budgets uses is updated at least once per day. That makes it useful for governance, but not the earliest service for anomaly detection. This option is also less adaptive than anomaly detection because it depends on fixed thresholds rather than learned expected spending patterns. As a result, it is not as effective as Cost Anomaly Detection for catching unexpected malicious cost spikes early.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/manage-ad.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/ad-SNS.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws-cloud-financial-management/faster-anomaly-resolution-with-enhanced-root-cause-analysis-in-aws-cost-anomaly-detection/

Domínio Domain 1: Detection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A security operations team maintains all AWS KMS customer managed keys for an organization. Only security team members can administer these keys directly. A data engineering team has a batch processing workflow that requires occasional short-term cryptographic access to specific keys. The security team must provide this access while retaining full control over the access lifecycle.

Which solution meets these requirements with the least operational overhead?
opcoes: Generate new KMS key material outside of AWS, import it into a new customer managed key whenever the data engineering team requests access, then delete the key when the access window closes

Store the KMS key ARN and alias in an AWS Secrets Manager secret, grant the data engineering team IAM permission to retrieve the secret, and delete the secret from Secrets Manager when the team no longer needs to reference the key during batch operations
Create a KMS key grant that allows the data engineering team's batch process to perform the required cryptographic actions, then revoke the grant when the team no longer needs access

Export the KMS key material to an on-premises hardware security module and provide the data engineering team with credentials to access the HSM so the team can perform the required cryptographic operations independently
respostas_corretas: Create a KMS key grant that allows the data engineering team's batch process to perform the required cryptographic actions, then revoke the grant when the team no longer needs access
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create a KMS key grant that allows the data engineering team's batch process to perform the required cryptographic actions, then revoke the grant when the team no longer needs access

AWS KMS key grants provide a built-in delegation mechanism that is specifically designed for temporary or programmatic access without requiring key policy modifications. A grant specifies the grantee principal, the permitted cryptographic actions such as kms:Decrypt and kms:GenerateDataKey, and optional constraints such as an encryption context. Once created, the grant takes effect immediately and allows the grantee to perform the specified operations without changes to the key policy or the grantee's IAM policies.

Grants are operationally efficient because both creation and revocation require a single API call each. Revoking a grant removes the grantee's access immediately and completely. Because grants are separate from the key policy, the security team can manage temporary access through the grant API without touching the key policy document that governs permanent administrative permissions. The entire temporary access lifecycle is managed independently from the key policy.

The grant-based approach also provides a cleaner audit trail through CloudTrail, where kms:CreateGrant and kms:RevokeGrant events record who created and revoked each grant. This separation of temporary delegation from permanent policy management is the principal reason AWS KMS grants exist.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/grants.html

Incorrect options:

Export the KMS key material to an on-premises hardware security module and provide the data engineering team with credentials to access the HSM so the team can perform the required cryptographic operations independently - Exporting KMS key material to an on-premises HSM removes the security boundary that AWS KMS provides. Key material exported from a standard AWS KMS customer managed key cannot be used in a compliant manner outside the KMS service because AWS KMS does not allow key material export from keys where the material was generated internally. Exporting key material changes the trust model and requires managing a separate HSM with its own access controls, logging, and operational procedures. This approach significantly increases operational complexity while reducing the security controls available through AWS KMS.

Store the KMS key ARN and alias in an AWS Secrets Manager secret, grant the data engineering team IAM permission to retrieve the secret, and delete the secret from Secrets Manager when the team no longer needs to reference the key during batch operations - Storing the KMS key ARN or alias in Secrets Manager provides no cryptographic access to the key. The key ARN is a non-secret identifier visible in IAM policies, the AWS console, and CloudTrail logs. Placing it in Secrets Manager does not grant or restrict KMS permissions in any way. The data engineering team would still need explicit IAM permissions allowing the required KMS actions, so Secrets Manager adds operational overhead without addressing the access control requirement. Deleting the Secrets Manager secret removes access only to the ARN string, not the underlying KMS permissions.

Generate new KMS key material outside of AWS, import it into a new customer managed key whenever the data engineering team requests access, then delete the key when the access window closes - Importing key material into a new KMS key for each access cycle requires generating high-quality key material outside of AWS in a FIPS-compliant environment, wrapping the material correctly for the KMS import API, and securely managing the raw key material storage between cycles. Deleting a KMS key initiates a mandatory minimum 7-day waiting period before permanent deletion, making this approach unsuitable for scenarios requiring frequent or short-duration access windows. Additionally, data encrypted with one imported key instance cannot be decrypted after the key is deleted, creating long-term data accessibility risks.

References: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/grants.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_CreateGrant.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/managing-permissions-with-grants-in-aws-key-management-service/

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
An IAM user receives an Access Denied error when trying to retrieve objects from an Amazon S3 bucket. The IAM user and the S3 bucket are in the same AWS account. The bucket uses server-side encryption with AWS KMS keys and encrypts all objects at rest by using a customer managed KMS key from the same account. The bucket does not have a bucket policy. The IAM user has an IAM policy that allows s3:List* and s3:Get* on the bucket and its objects. The same IAM policy also allows kms:Decrypt on the customer managed KMS key. A security engineer must determine a possible reason why the IAM user still cannot access the objects.

Which explanation is most likely correct?
opcoes: The customer managed KMS key policy was changed so that the AWS account no longer has permission to use IAM policies to control access to the key
The IAM policy must also allow the kms:DescribeKey permission for the customer managed KMS key before the user can retrieve SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3
The Amazon S3 bucket requires a bucket policy that explicitly allows the IAM user to access the objects, even when the user and the bucket are in the same AWS account
The IAM user also needs the kms:GenerateDataKey permission on the customer managed KMS key to retrieve existing SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3
respostas_corretas: The customer managed KMS key policy was changed so that the AWS account no longer has permission to use IAM policies to control access to the key
explicacao:
Correct option:

The customer managed KMS key policy was changed so that the AWS account no longer has permission to use IAM policies to control access to the key

For an IAM principal to use a customer managed KMS key, AWS KMS authorization depends on both the IAM policy and the KMS key policy. AWS documents that unless the key policy allows the account to use IAM policies for access control, permissions granted only through IAM policies are not sufficient to use the key. If the key policy was edited to remove the account-level permission that enables IAM policy delegation, an IAM user can have kms:Decrypt in IAM and still receive Access Denied.

This fits the scenario precisely. The user already has s3:Get*, s3:List*, and kms:Decrypt in IAM, and there is no bucket policy denying access. A broken or restrictive KMS key policy is therefore a realistic reason the user still cannot access the SSE-KMS encrypted objects. Amazon S3 needs to use the KMS key on behalf of the requester during retrieval, and if the key policy does not allow that authorization path, the request fails.

This is a common AWS exam trap. Many learners assume IAM permissions alone are enough for a customer managed KMS key, but KMS key policy evaluation is critical. In same-account scenarios, the default key policy usually enables IAM policies to control access. If that default behavior has been removed, access breaks even when the IAM policy appears correct.

Incorrect options:

The IAM policy must also allow the kms:DescribeKey permission for the customer managed KMS key before the user can retrieve SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3 - kms:DescribeKey can be useful for viewing metadata about a KMS key, but it is not the core permission required to retrieve an existing SSE-KMS encrypted object from Amazon S3. The essential KMS permission for reading such an object is kms:Decrypt. Since the scenario already grants kms:Decrypt, adding kms:DescribeKey is not the most likely missing permission. A failure caused by key policy configuration is much more plausible than the absence of a metadata-read permission.

The IAM user also needs the kms:GenerateDataKey permission on the customer managed KMS key to retrieve existing SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3 -kms:GenerateDataKey is typically needed when new data keys must be generated for encryption operations. Retrieving an existing SSE-KMS encrypted object from Amazon S3 does not normally require the IAM user to generate a new data key. The critical read path permission is kms:Decrypt, which the user already has in IAM. Therefore, the absence of kms:GenerateDataKey is not the most likely reason for the Access Denied error in this scenario.

The Amazon S3 bucket requires a bucket policy that explicitly allows the IAM user to access the objects, even when the user and the bucket are in the same AWS account - An S3 bucket policy is not required for same-account access when IAM identity-based policies already allow the relevant S3 actions and there is no explicit deny. In this scenario, the user already has s3:List* and s3:Get* permissions in IAM, so the absence of a bucket policy is not itself a problem. Bucket policies are often used for cross-account access or for additional restrictions, but they are not mandatory here. Since the bucket has no bucket policy, there is no bucket-policy-based deny to explain the failure.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/control-access.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company runs a frontend application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The EC2 instances use Amazon EBS volumes for storage, and the application stores large media files in Amazon S3. The company has implemented preventive and detective controls against ransomware attacks. The security team now needs to design a disaster recovery solution that can restore the environment to a known good state if ransomware bypasses existing controls. The solution must support an RPO of 1 hour and must allow rapid reconstruction of the application environment.

Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
opcoes: Configure AWS Backup to take hourly backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and store infrastructure definitions as AWS CloudFormation templates to enable rapid environment reconstruction
Configure AWS Backup to take daily backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and use Amazon Security Lake to analyze logs and trigger automated remediation workflows during incidents
Use Amazon Security Lake and AWS Security Hub to centralize findings and define recovery procedures, and store AWS CloudFormation templates to rebuild infrastructure when needed

Enable AWS Config rules to monitor the configuration compliance of EC2 instances and S3 buckets against a backup frequency policy. Create an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function every 30 minutes that validates the backup compliance status of all protected resources and sends an Amazon SNS alert to the security team if any resource has not received a compliant backup within the required RPO window
respostas_corretas: Configure AWS Backup to take hourly backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and store infrastructure definitions as AWS CloudFormation templates to enable rapid environment reconstruction
explicacao:
Correct option:

Configure AWS Backup to take hourly backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and store infrastructure definitions as AWS CloudFormation templates to enable rapid environment reconstruction

Using AWS Backup with an hourly backup schedule ensures that both EBS volumes and S3 data are protected with a recovery point objective of 1 hour. AWS Backup provides centralized and automated backup management, which is critical for maintaining consistent recovery points across multiple services.

Storing infrastructure definitions as AWS CloudFormation templates allows the company to quickly recreate the entire environment, including load balancers, EC2 instances, and networking components. This ensures that recovery is not limited to data restoration but also includes rebuilding the application stack in a controlled and repeatable way.

This combination directly addresses ransomware recovery requirements by enabling both data restoration and infrastructure redeployment. It meets the RPO requirement, minimizes recovery time, and aligns with best practices for disaster recovery and infrastructure as code.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/plan-options-and-configuration.html

Incorrect options:

Configure AWS Backup to take daily backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and use Amazon Security Lake to analyze logs and trigger automated remediation workflows during incidents - Daily backups do not meet the required RPO of 1 hour. Any data changes within the last 24 hours would be lost in the event of a ransomware attack. Although Amazon Security Lake provides valuable centralized logging and analytics capabilities, it does not contribute to meeting backup and recovery objectives. This option focuses more on detection and analysis than on recovery.

Use Amazon Security Lake and AWS Security Hub to centralize findings and define recovery procedures, and store AWS CloudFormation templates to rebuild infrastructure when needed - Amazon Security Lake and AWS Security Hub are useful for detection, visibility, and incident response coordination, but they do not provide data backup or recovery capabilities. While storing CloudFormation templates supports infrastructure reconstruction, the absence of frequent backups means that the solution cannot meet the RPO requirement. This option lacks a critical data protection component.

Enable AWS Config rules to monitor the configuration compliance of EC2 instances and S3 buckets against a backup frequency policy. Create an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function every 30 minutes that validates the backup compliance status of all protected resources and sends an Amazon SNS alert to the security team if any resource has not received a compliant backup within the required RPO window - Configuring AWS Config rules and Lambda functions to monitor backup compliance and send alerts does not create backups or recover data. This approach provides compliance monitoring and alerting, which are useful operational controls, but the Lambda function only notifies the security team of missing backups rather than executing the recovery itself. Receiving an SNS alert does not constitute a recovery solution and does not address the DR requirement to return to normal operations after a ransomware event bypasses preventive and detective controls.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/whatisbackup.html
