https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/plan-options-and-configuration.html

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company observes that one of its Amazon EC2 instances has suddenly begun consuming an unusually high amount of CPU. The security team cannot yet determine whether the behavior is the result of a compromise or whether the operating system is running legitimate background maintenance tasks. Before beginning the investigation, a security engineer must preserve evidence and reduce the risk of accidental loss of the potentially affected resource.

Which combination of actions should the security engineer take first? (Select three)

Restart the EC2 instance immediately so that background cleanup tasks finish and the CPU usage pattern returns to normal before evidence collection begins

Detach the IAM instance profile from the EC2 instance and terminate the instance after exporting the system logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs
opcoes: Turn off termination protection for the EC2 instance if the instance currently has API termination protection enabled
Collect the EC2 instance metadata and apply a quarantine tag to the EC2 instance
Create snapshots of the Amazon EBS data volumes that are attached to the EC2 instance
Turn on termination protection for the EC2 instance if API termination protection is not already enabled
respostas_corretas: Collect the EC2 instance metadata and apply a quarantine tag to the EC2 instance
Create snapshots of the Amazon EBS data volumes that are attached to the EC2 instance
Turn on termination protection for the EC2 instance if API termination protection is not already enabled
explicacao:
Correct option:

Turn on termination protection for the EC2 instance if API termination protection is not already enabled

Enabling termination protection is an important first step when an EC2 instance might be involved in a security incident. It reduces the risk that the instance is accidentally terminated during containment or investigation activities, which would destroy valuable evidence and make forensic analysis more difficult. AWS documents that termination protection helps prevent accidental termination through the EC2 API or console. In an incident-response workflow, preserving the affected resource is critical, especially before investigators determine whether the activity is malicious or benign.

By enabling this control early, the security engineer preserves the option to investigate the running instance safely without introducing unnecessary operational risk.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/collect-relevant-artifacts.html

Create snapshots of the Amazon EBS data volumes that are attached to the EC2 instance

Creating snapshots of the attached Amazon EBS volumes preserves the disk state of the instance at the time of suspicion. This is a core evidence-preservation step because the volume contents might contain logs, malware artifacts, persistence mechanisms, or modified files that are necessary for analysis.

AWS incident response guidance emphasizes preserving resources, snapshots, and other evidence before making disruptive changes. EBS snapshots are a native and efficient way to capture the state of persistent storage for later forensic review or recovery. This step supports both investigation and recovery. Even if the instance later needs to be isolated or replaced, the preserved volume state remains available for detailed examination.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/introduction.html

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/introduction.html

Collect the EC2 instance metadata and apply a quarantine tag to the EC2 instance

Capturing EC2 instance metadata is an early investigative step because it records important contextual information such as instance configuration, attached roles, networking details, and other environment attributes. This metadata can help investigators understand the scope of the issue and reconstruct the instance state. Applying a quarantine tag is also a practical preparation step because it marks the resource clearly for incident handling workflows. Tags can be used operationally to signal containment state, prevent confusion during response, and trigger automated controls in mature incident-response environments.

Together, metadata collection and quarantine tagging help preserve context and support an organized response without immediately destroying or altering critical evidence.

How to automate incident response in the AWS Cloud for EC2 instances:  via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-automate-incident-response-in-aws-cloud-for-ec2-instances/

Incorrect options:

Turn off termination protection for the EC2 instance if the instance currently has API termination protection enabled - Disabling termination protection increases the risk that the instance is accidentally terminated during the investigation. That is the opposite of what a security engineer should do when evidence preservation is a priority. Before investigators understand whether the instance is compromised, they should protect it from accidental deletion rather than making it easier to terminate. This option weakens the response posture instead of strengthening it.

Restart the EC2 instance immediately so that background cleanup tasks finish and the CPU usage pattern returns to normal before evidence collection begins - Restarting the instance immediately can destroy volatile evidence and change the runtime state before investigators understand what is happening. High CPU usage might be caused by malicious activity, and a restart can erase important clues in memory or alter the timing of system artifacts. Incident response begins with preservation and controlled containment, not with disruptive remediation actions performed before evidence capture. This option therefore introduces unnecessary risk.

Detach the IAM instance profile from the EC2 instance and terminate the instance after exporting the system logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs - Detaching the instance profile and terminating the instance is far too disruptive as an initial action. Termination can destroy the running resource and remove valuable forensic context before the investigation is complete. Although role detachment and stronger containment might be appropriate later in a confirmed compromise scenario, the question asks what should be done before investigating. Preserving the resource is the priority at that stage, not terminating it.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/collect-relevant-artifacts.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/introduction.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/Using_ChangingDisableAPITermination.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-automate-incident-response-in-aws-cloud-for-ec2-instances/

pergunta:
A company receives an Amazon GuardDuty finding that indicates potential credential compromise on an Amazon EC2 instance hosting a critical web application. The finding type is UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/InstanceCredentialExfiltration. Further investigation confirms that API credentials associated with the EC2 instance profile were used from a geographic location where the company does not operate. A security engineer must take immediate action to block the malicious activity.

What should the security engineer do first to mitigate the threat?
opcoes: Install and configure the AWS Systems Manager Agent on the EC2 instance and generate an inventory report to assess the system state
Create an AWS WAF rule to block requests originating from the suspicious geographic location and associate the rule with the application endpoint
Install the Amazon Inspector agent on the EC2 instance and run a vulnerability assessment using the CVE rules package
Revoke all active IAM role sessions associated with the EC2 instance profile to immediately invalidate the compromised temporary credentials
respostas_corretas: Revoke all active IAM role sessions associated with the EC2 instance profile to immediately invalidate the compromised temporary credentials
explicacao:
Correct option:

Revoke all active IAM role sessions associated with the EC2 instance profile to immediately invalidate the compromised temporary credentials

Revoking active IAM role sessions is the fastest and most direct way to invalidate compromised temporary credentials. When instance profile credentials are exfiltrated, attackers can use the temporary access keys to perform API actions until the session expires. Revoking the session immediately cuts off the attacker’s ability to continue using those credentials.

This action aligns with incident response best practices, where containment is the first priority. By invalidating the active sessions, the security engineer prevents further unauthorized API calls without requiring changes to infrastructure or application availability.

This step is also minimally disruptive and can be executed quickly. After containment, the security engineer can proceed with deeper investigation, rotation of credentials, and hardening of the environment.

Incorrect options:

Create an AWS WAF rule to block requests originating from the suspicious geographic location and associate the rule with the application endpoint - Blocking traffic by geographic location using AWS WAF applies only to web traffic that flows through services integrated with WAF, such as Application Load Balancers or CloudFront. The attack involves API calls made using stolen credentials, which are not mitigated by WAF rules. Therefore, this option does not address the actual threat vector.

Install and configure the AWS Systems Manager Agent on the EC2 instance and generate an inventory report to assess the system state - Installing and using AWS Systems Manager to collect inventory data can help with post-incident investigation and system visibility. However, it does not provide immediate containment of the threat. The attacker can continue to use the compromised credentials while the inventory process runs, which makes this option inappropriate as the first step.

Install the Amazon Inspector agent on the EC2 instance and run a vulnerability assessment using the CVE rules package - Running Amazon Inspector to identify vulnerabilities is useful for improving the security posture of the instance over time. This option focuses on vulnerability assessment rather than incident containment. It does not stop the active misuse of stolen credentials and therefore does not meet the requirement.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use_revoke-sessions.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_temp_control-access_disable-perms.html

pergunta:
A company runs a removable fleet of Amazon EC2 instances for an application. Each instance in the fleet can be taken out of service without affecting application availability. All instances currently share the same security group named ProdFleet. Amazon GuardDuty and AWS Config are enabled in the AWS account. A security engineer must automatically stop any EC2 instance from sending outbound traffic when Amazon GuardDuty produces a cryptocurrency-related finding for that instance. The engineer creates a new security group named isolate that has no outbound rules. The engineer also creates an AWS Lambda function that removes an affected EC2 instance from the ProdFleet security group and adds the instance to the isolate security group.

What additional step should the security engineer implement to complete this solution?
opcoes: Configure Amazon GuardDuty to invoke the AWS Lambda function directly whenever GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding
Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever AWS Config detects a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* configuration change event for an EC2 instance
Create an AWS Systems Manager Automation runbook that checks AWS Config snapshots for CryptoCurrency:EC2/* findings and then updates the EC2 instance security groups when a matching snapshot is found
Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever Amazon GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding
respostas_corretas: Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever Amazon GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding
explicacao:
Correct option:

Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever Amazon GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding

Amazon GuardDuty findings are emitted to Amazon EventBridge, and AWS documents using EventBridge rules to route GuardDuty findings to downstream targets such as AWS Lambda for automated response. That makes EventBridge the native trigger mechanism for invoking the Lambda function when a specific finding type such as CryptoCurrency:EC2/* occurs.

This fits the requirement because the security engineer already built the remediation function and the isolate security group. What is still needed is the event-driven linkage between the GuardDuty finding and the Lambda function. An EventBridge rule can filter on the GuardDuty finding type and invoke the function immediately when the cryptocurrency finding is generated.

This solution also has low operational overhead and uses the intended AWS integration path. GuardDuty produces the detection, EventBridge routes the matching event, and Lambda performs the containment by changing the instance security group association.

Incorrect options:

Configure Amazon GuardDuty to invoke the AWS Lambda function directly whenever GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding - Amazon GuardDuty does not directly invoke AWS Lambda functions as a target integration for findings. The standard event-driven pattern is for GuardDuty to emit findings to Amazon EventBridge, and then for EventBridge to invoke Lambda or another target. This option is close in spirit, but it skips the required routing service. Because GuardDuty is not configured to call Lambda directly for finding events in this workflow, the option is incorrect.

Create an AWS Systems Manager Automation runbook that checks AWS Config snapshots for CryptoCurrency:EC2/* findings and then updates the EC2 instance security groups when a matching snapshot is found - AWS Systems Manager Automation can be useful for running remediation workflows, but the trigger logic described here is incorrect. AWS Config snapshots do not contain GuardDuty finding events as the signal to drive remediation. This design is also heavier than necessary. The engineer already has a Lambda function that performs the required isolation action, so the missing component is the proper event source and routing path from GuardDuty, which is EventBridge.

Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever AWS Config detects a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* configuration change event for an EC2 instance - This option correctly uses EventBridge as an event-routing mechanism, which makes it look plausible. However, it still uses the wrong event source because AWS Config does not detect GuardDuty cryptocurrency finding types as configuration change events. The finding the engineer cares about is generated by GuardDuty. Therefore, the EventBridge rule must match GuardDuty findings, not AWS Config events.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_findings_eventbridge.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eventbridge/latest/ref/events-ref-guardduty.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_finding-types-ec2.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/findings_management.html

pergunta: A company uses AWS Organizations and keeps the default FullAWSAccess service control policy attached. The company wants to apply an additional guardrail to every AWS account in a particular organizational unit (OU). With the exception of a defined set of global AWS services, all API activity for accounts in that OU must be restricted to the us-east-1 Region. The company needs a service control policy (SCP) that will apply to both the current member accounts and any new accounts that are added to the OU in the future.
opcoes: Which SCP should the security engineer attach to the OU to meet these requirements?

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "NotAction": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] }

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "NotAction": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } { { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "Action": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] }
respostas_corretas: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "NotAction": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } { { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "Action": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] }
explicacao:
Correct option:

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "NotAction": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] }

A service control policy that uses Effect: "Deny" together with NotAction for the allowed global services and StringNotEquals for aws:RequestedRegion correctly blocks actions outside the approved Region. This design denies every action except the listed global services whenever the request is made for a Region other than us-east-1.

This pattern works well with the default FullAWSAccess SCP because SCPs establish a permissions boundary for accounts in the organization. By attaching this SCP to the OU, the company applies the regional restriction centrally to all existing accounts in that OU and to any accounts added later. The policy does not grant permissions. Instead, it filters what principals in the affected accounts can do.

The use of NotAction is essential because some AWS services are global and do not operate within a single regional endpoint model. Excluding the approved global services from the deny statement preserves access to those services while preventing use of other services outside us-east-1. This is the standard SCP design for enforcing Region restrictions with exceptions for global services.

Incorrect options:

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "NotAction": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } - This policy uses Effect: "Deny" with NotAction, which is directionally close to the right structure. However, it uses StringEquals for aws:RequestedRegion and therefore denies requests when the requested Region is us-east-1.

That behavior is the opposite of the requirement. The company wants to allow usage in us-east-1 and deny usage outside that Region except for designated global services. Because the condition logic is reversed, this policy blocks the approved Region rather than protecting it.

{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } - SCPs do not work like ordinary IAM identity policies for granting access. Even though Allow statements can exist in SCP evaluation, the common and correct way to enforce organizational guardrails with the default FullAWSAccess policy is to use explicit deny logic to block what should not be allowed.

This policy also uses Action instead of NotAction, which means it only references the listed global services rather than exempting them from a broader deny. As written, it does not create the required regional boundary for all other AWS service usage.

{ { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "Action": [ <ApprovedGlobalServices> ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } - This policy is incorrect because it applies the deny only to the listed global services instead of restricting all other AWS services. The requirement is to allow global services everywhere while limiting all other service usage to us-east-1. By using Action instead of NotAction, the policy ends up denying only the specified global services outside us-east-1, while allowing all regional services such as EC2 and RDS to operate in any Region. This fails to enforce the intended Region restriction.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_condition-keys.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_elements_notaction.html

Domínio Domain 6: Security Foundations and Governance
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A multinational consulting firm maintains a distributed IT infrastructure across 12 AWS accounts organized within an AWS Organizations structure. Each account serves a different business unit and operates independently, but there is a centralized security and compliance team that manages a dedicated organizational account responsible for security oversight. The firm must catalog all instances of sensitive data including financial records, client intellectual property, and personal information stored across all Amazon S3 buckets in the entire organization. The findings must be visible through a single centralized platform without requiring security analysts to log into individual accounts.

Which solution best meets these requirements?
opcoes: Set the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub and Amazon Macie. Configure the AWS Glue Data Catalog to index all S3 buckets across member accounts as a centralized metadata repository, and use AWS Lake Formation to manage access permissions and sensitive data classifications

Configure the security account as the delegated administrator for both Amazon Macie and AWS Security Hub. Enable and configure Macie to scan all S3 buckets across the organization's member accounts and publish all sensitive data findings directly to the central Security Hub instance

Designate the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub only. Configure Amazon Inspector in each of the 12 member accounts to scan S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and route all findings to the central Security Hub instance in the security account

Enable and configure Amazon Macie independently in each of the 12 member accounts to identify sensitive data. Integrate each account's Macie instance with AWS Trusted Advisor and consolidate all sensitive data findings in the Trusted Advisor console visible to the security account
respostas_corretas: Configure the security account as the delegated administrator for both Amazon Macie and AWS Security Hub. Enable and configure Macie to scan all S3 buckets across the organization's member accounts and publish all sensitive data findings directly to the central Security Hub instance
explicacao:
Correct option:

Configure the security account as the delegated administrator for both Amazon Macie and AWS Security Hub. Enable and configure Macie to scan all S3 buckets across the organization's member accounts and publish all sensitive data findings directly to the central Security Hub instance

Amazon Macie as a delegated administrator service in AWS Organizations allows a single designated account, in this case the security account, to enable and manage Macie scans across all member accounts without requiring separate Macie deployments in each account. Delegation is automatic through the Organizations API, so Macie in the security account has the authorization to scan resources in all member accounts.

When Macie discovers sensitive data in S3 buckets across any member account, it publishes findings to AWS Security Hub, also configured with the security account as delegated administrator. Security Hub serves as the centralized dashboard where all Macie findings appear in a unified interface. The security team logs into the security account once and reviews sensitive data discoveries from all 12 member accounts in a single pane of glass.

This architecture eliminates the need for analysts to log into each member account separately and ensures no discoveries are missed because a single Macie configuration scans all accounts under delegated administration. The centralized findings in Security Hub provide a complete inventory of sensitive data across the organization.

Use Security Hub custom actions to remediate S3 resources based on Macie discovery results:  via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-security-hub-custom-actions-to-remediate-s3-resources-based-on-macie-discovery-results/

via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-security-hub-custom-actions-to-remediate-s3-resources-based-on-macie-discovery-results/

Incorrect options:

Designate the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub only. Configure Amazon Inspector in each of the 12 member accounts to scan S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and route all findings to the central Security Hub instance in the security account - Amazon Inspector is designed to identify software vulnerabilities and exposures in EC2 instances, container images, and Lambda functions. It does not scan S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and is not capable of discovering financial records, client IP, or personal information stored in objects. Configuring Inspector to analyze S3 for sensitive data misrepresents Inspector's capabilities, which are limited to vulnerability assessment on compute resources.

Set the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub and Amazon Macie. Configure the AWS Glue Data Catalog to index all S3 buckets across member accounts as a centralized metadata repository, and use AWS Lake Formation to manage access permissions and sensitive data classifications - AWS Glue Data Catalog and AWS Lake Formation are data governance and metadata services designed for cataloging data assets and managing access permissions, not for discovering sensitive data patterns within existing S3 objects. These services require the data to be registered and indexed beforehand, not for automated sensitive data discovery. Lake Formation is an access control and governance layer, not a sensitive data detection tool, and it would not discover the sensitive data that already resides untagged in S3 buckets.

Enable and configure Amazon Macie independently in each of the 12 member accounts to identify sensitive data. Integrate each account's Macie instance with AWS Trusted Advisor and consolidate all sensitive data findings in the Trusted Advisor console visible to the security account - While Macie can be deployed in individual member accounts to identify sensitive data, requiring independent Macie instances in each of the 12 accounts creates significant operational overhead. Each account must have Macie independently enabled, configured, and monitored. Macie does not have a native integration with AWS Trusted Advisor for findings consolidation. Attempting to route Macie findings to Trusted Advisor requires custom development and does not produce a unified sensitive data inventory view.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/what-is-macie.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/securityhub-v2-concepts.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/findings.html

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-security-hub-custom-actions-to-remediate-s3-resources-based-on-macie-discovery-results/

Domínio Domain 5: Data Protection
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso

pergunta:
A company is designing a web application that serves external users through multiple microservices hosted on Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS). The architecture must authenticate users securely while issuing temporary credentials and minimizing the operational burden of managing user identities. The company wants a centralized authentication mechanism that avoids maintaining custom credential stores and ensures that all requests to backend microservices are validated before processing.

Select and arrange the necessary steps from the list below to build a secure authentication strategy. You may use each step one time or not at all:

Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication

Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions

Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices

Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices

Create an Amazon DynamoDB table to store user credentials for each microservice

Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool

Which sequence of steps should the security engineer implement to meet these requirements?
opcoes: Step 1: Create an Amazon DynamoDB table to store user credentials for each microservice -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication

Step 1: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices

Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices

Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices
respostas_corretas: Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices
explicacao:
Correct option:

Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices

Amazon Cognito user pools provide a fully managed authentication service that handles user sign-in, identity management, and token issuance. This removes the need to build and maintain a custom credential store and supports scalable authentication for external users.

An example authentication session using Cognito User Pools:  via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/authentication.html

Configuring a Cognito application client allows the web application to securely interact with the user pool and obtain authentication tokens. These tokens are used to represent the user identity in subsequent requests to backend services.

Using Amazon API Gateway with Lambda authorizers ensures that all incoming requests are validated before reaching the ECS microservices. This centralizes authentication, enforces consistent access control, and enables a scalable and secure architecture.

via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-use-lambda-authorizer.html

Incorrect options:

Step 1: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices - AWS IAM Identity Center is designed for workforce identity use cases and is not intended for authenticating external application users. It does not integrate with web application authentication flows in the same way as Cognito.

Although API Gateway with Lambda authorizers is correctly included, the absence of Cognito user pools prevents proper token-based authentication. This makes the solution unsuitable for the given requirements.

Step 1: Create an Amazon DynamoDB table to store user credentials for each microservice -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication - Storing user credentials in DynamoDB introduces unnecessary operational overhead and requires building a custom authentication system. This contradicts the requirement to minimize management effort.

Additionally, the sequence is incorrect because it attempts to configure an application client before establishing the user pool. The steps do not form a valid authentication workflow.

Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices - While Cognito user pools are correctly included, this option replaces token-based authentication with IAM Identity Center for user access, which is not appropriate for external users.

IAM roles for microservices are useful for service-level permissions but do not address user authentication. This sequence does not establish a proper authentication and authorization flow for the application.

References:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/authentication.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/cognito-user-pools.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-use-lambda-authorizer.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/what-is.html

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html

Domínio Domain 4: Identity and Access Management
temas: Domínio 4: Gestão de Identidade e Acesso
